        mv "$temp_file" "$file"
        echo "Updated: $file"
    else
        rm -f "$temp_file"
    fi
}

# Function to verify ACL support in AIDE
verify_aide_acl_support() {
    # Check if AIDE was compiled with ACL support
    if ! aide --help 2>/dev/null | grep -q "acl"; then
        # Try to verify through aide.conf parsing
        # AIDE should support ACL in its rules syntax
        if ! grep -r "acl" "${AIDE_CONF_DIR}" 2>/dev/null | grep -q "^"; then
            echo "Warning: ACL support verification inconclusive, proceeding with configuration"
        fi
    fi
}

# Main remediation script
main() {
    echo "Starting AIDE ACL verification configuration..."
    
    # Verify AIDE ACL support
    verify_aide_acl_support
    
    # Process main AIDE configuration file
    if [[ -f "$AIDE_CONF_FILE" ]]; then
        echo "Processing main AIDE configuration file: $AIDE_CONF_FILE"
        process_aide_file "$AIDE_CONF_FILE"
    fi
    
    # Process all configuration files in aide.conf.d directory
    if [[ -d "$AIDE_RULES_DIR" ]]; then
        echo "Processing AIDE configuration directory: $AIDE_RULES_DIR"
        for conf_file in "${AIDE_RULES_DIR}"/*.conf; do
            if [[ -f "$conf_file" ]]; then
                echo "Processing: $conf_file"
                process_aide_file "$conf_file"
            fi
        done
    fi
    
    # Verify the configuration is valid
    if aide --config-check -c "$AIDE_CONF_FILE" &>/dev/null; then
        echo "AIDE configuration is valid"
    else
        echo "Warning: AIDE configuration check failed, but remediation completed"
    fi
    
    echo "AIDE ACL verification configuration complete"
    echo "To initialize the database with ACL checking, run:"
    echo "  aideinit"
    echo "To perform an integrity check, run:"
    echo "  aide --check"
}

# Run the main function
main

#!/bin/bash

set -e

# Disable SCTP protocol by creating kernel module blacklist entry
BLACKLIST_FILE="/etc/modprobe.d/sctp-disable.conf"

# Create or update the blacklist file
if ! grep -q "^blacklist sctp" "$BLACKLIST_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "blacklist sctp" | tee "$BLACKLIST_FILE" > /dev/null
fi

# Also install a modprobe configuration to prevent loading even if blacklist is bypassed
INSTALL_FILE="/etc/modprobe.d/sctp-install.conf"
if ! grep -q "^install sctp" "$INSTALL_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "install sctp /bin/true" | tee "$INSTALL_FILE" > /dev/null
fi

# Remove SCTP from running kernel if it's already loaded
if lsmod | grep -q "^sctp"; then
    modprobe -r sctp 2>/dev/null || true
fi

# Verify the changes were applied
if grep -q "^blacklist sctp" "$BLACKLIST_FILE" && grep -q "^install sctp" "$INSTALL_FILE"; then
    exit 0
else
    exit 1
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Script to disable Kerberos-based authentication in SSH server on RHEL 8
# This is an idempotent script that ensures the setting persists

set -e

SSH_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
BACKUP_DIR="/var/backups/ssh_config"
TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)

# Create backup directory if it doesn't exist
mkdir -p "$BACKUP_DIR"

# Function to disable Kerberos authentication
disable_kerberos_auth() {
    # Check if sshd_config exists
    if [[ ! -f "$SSH_CONFIG" ]]; then
        echo "Error: SSH config file not found at $SSH_CONFIG"
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Create a backup of the original file
    cp "$SSH_CONFIG" "$BACKUP_DIR/sshd_config.backup.$TIMESTAMP"
    
    # Check if the config file already has the required settings
    local needs_update=0
    
    # Check for GSSAPIAuthentication setting
    if grep -q "^GSSAPIAuthentication" "$SSH_CONFIG"; then
        # Check if it's already set to no
        if ! grep -q "^GSSAPIAuthentication no" "$SSH_CONFIG"; then
            # Replace the existing setting
            sed -i 's/^GSSAPIAuthentication.*/GSSAPIAuthentication no/' "$SSH_CONFIG"
            needs_update=1
        fi
    else
        # Add the setting if it doesn't exist
        echo "GSSAPIAuthentication no" >> "$SSH_CONFIG"
        needs_update=1
    fi
    
    # Check for GSSAPICleanupCredentials setting
    if grep -q "^GSSAPICleanupCredentials" "$SSH_CONFIG"; then
        # Check if it's already set to yes
        if ! grep -q "^GSSAPICleanupCredentials yes" "$SSH_CONFIG"; then
            # Replace the existing setting
            sed -i 's/^GSSAPICleanupCredentials.*/GSSAPICleanupCredentials yes/' "$SSH_CONFIG"
            needs_update=1
        fi
    else
        # Add the setting if it doesn't exist (defensive setting)
        echo "GSSAPICleanupCredentials yes" >> "$SSH_CONFIG"
        needs_update=1
    fi
    
    return "$needs_update"
}

# Function to verify SSH configuration syntax
verify_ssh_config() {
    if ! sshd -t -f "$SSH_CONFIG" 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "Error: SSH configuration syntax check failed"
        # Restore from backup
        cp "$BACKUP_DIR/sshd_config.backup.$TIMESTAMP" "$SSH_CONFIG"
        return 1
    fi
    return 0
}

# Function to restart SSH service if needed
restart_ssh_service() {
    if [[ $1 -eq 1 ]]; then
        systemctl restart sshd
        echo "SSH service restarted successfully"
        return 0
    else
        echo "No changes were needed; SSH configuration already has Kerberos authentication disabled"
        return 0
    fi
}

# Main execution
echo "Starting SSH Kerberos authentication disabling process..."

if ! disable_kerberos_auth; then
    echo "Error: Failed to disable Kerberos authentication"
    exit 1
fi

CHANGES_MADE=$?

if ! verify_ssh_config; then
    echo "Error: SSH configuration verification failed"
    exit 1
fi

restart_ssh_service "$CHANGES_MADE"

echo "SSH Kerberos authentication has been successfully disabled"
echo "Configuration backup saved to: $BACKUP_DIR/sshd_config.backup.$TIMESTAMP"

# Verify the final state
echo ""
echo "Final SSH configuration for Kerberos settings:"
grep -E "^GSSAPIAuthentication|^GSSAPICleanupCredentials" "$SSH_CONFIG" || true

exit 0

line_exists() {

    local line="$2"
    grep -Fxq "$line" "$file" 2>/dev/null && return 0 || return 1
}

# Function to add a line to a file if it doesn't exist
add_line_if_missing() {
    local file="$1"
    local line="$2"
    if ! line_exists "$file" "$line"; then
        echo "$line" >> "$file"
    fi
}

# Function to configure pam_faillock for account lockout
configure_pam_faillock() {
    local pam_file="$1"
    local phase="$2"  # auth or account
    
    # Check if faillock is already configured in this phase
    if grep -q "pam_faillock.so" "$pam_file"; then
        if [ "$phase" = "auth" ]; then
            # Ensure auth phase has faillock at the beginning and end
            if ! grep -q "^auth.*required.*pam_faillock.so.*preauth" "$pam_file"; then
                # Add preauth faillock if missing
                sed -i '/^auth.*sufficient.*pam_unix.so/i auth required pam_faillock.so preauth silent audit deny=5 unlock_time=900' "$pam_file"
            fi
            if ! grep -q "^auth.*required.*pam_faillock.so.*authfail" "$pam_file"; then
                # Add authfail faillock if missing
                sed -i '/^auth.*required.*pam_deny.so/a auth required pam_faillock.so authfail audit deny=5 unlock_time=900' "$pam_file"
            fi
        elif [ "$phase" = "account" ]; then
            # Ensure account phase has pam_faillock
            if ! grep -q "^account.*required.*pam_faillock.so" "$pam_file"; then
                sed -i '/^account.*required.*pam_permit.so/i account required pam_faillock.so' "$pam_file"
            fi
        fi
    else
        # faillock not present, add it
        if [ "$phase" = "auth" ]; then
            # Add both preauth and authfail rules
            sed -i '/^auth.*sufficient.*pam_unix.so/i auth required pam_faillock.so preauth silent audit deny=5 unlock_time=900' "$pam_file"
            sed -i '/^auth.*required.*pam_deny.so/a auth required pam_faillock.so authfail audit deny=5 unlock_time=900' "$pam_file"
        elif [ "$phase" = "account" ]; then
            sed -i '/^account.*required.*pam_permit.so/i account required pam_faillock.so' "$pam_file"
        fi
    fi
}

# Main configuration
main() {
    # PAM configuration file for password-based logins
    local pam_file="/etc/pam.d/password-auth"
    local system_auth_file="/etc/pam.d/system-auth"
    
    # Ensure pam_faillock is properly configured
    if [ -f "$pam_file" ]; then
        # Configure auth phase
        if ! grep -q "pam_faillock.so" "$pam_file"; then
            # Add faillock configuration
            sed -i '1i auth required pam_faillock.so preauth silent audit deny=5 unlock_time=900' "$pam_file"
            sed -i '/^auth.*required.*pam_deny.so/a auth required pam_faillock.so authfail audit deny=5 unlock_time=900' "$pam_file"
        fi
        
        # Configure account phase
        if ! grep -q "^account.*required.*pam_faillock.so" "$pam_file"; then
            sed -i '/^account.*required/i account required pam_faillock.so' "$pam_file"
        fi
    fi
    
    # Also configure system-auth for completeness
    if [ -f "$system_auth_file" ]; then
        if ! grep -q "pam_faillock.so" "$system_auth_file"; then
            sed -i '1i auth required pam_faillock.so preauth silent audit deny=5 unlock_time=900' "$system_auth_file"
            sed -i '/^auth.*required.*pam_deny.so/a auth required pam_faillock.so authfail audit deny=5 unlock_time=900' "$system_auth_file"
        fi
        
        if ! grep -q "^account.*required.*pam_faillock.so" "$system_auth_file"; then
            sed -i '/^account.*required/i account required pam_faillock.so' "$system_auth_file"
        fi
    fi
    
    # Ensure faillock directory exists with proper permissions
    if [ ! -d "/var/run/faillock" ]; then
        mkdir -p /var/run/faillock
    fi
    chmod 755 /var/run/faillock
    
    # Configure faillock settings in /etc/security/faillock.conf if available
    local faillock_conf="/etc/security/faillock.conf"
    if [ ! -f "$faillock_conf" ]; then
        cat > "$faillock_conf" << 'EOF'
# This file contains the faillock configuration for account lockout
deny = 5
unlock_time = 900
fail_interval = 900
silent = true
audit = true
EOF
    else
        # Update existing config if needed
        if ! grep -q "^deny = " "$faillock_conf"; then
            echo "deny = 5" >> "$faillock_conf"
        fi
        if ! grep -q "^unlock_time = " "$faillock_conf"; then
            echo "unlock_time = 900" >> "$faillock_conf"
        fi
    fi
    chmod 644 "$faillock_conf"
    
    # Verify the configuration is in place
    if grep -q "pam_faillock.so" "$pam_file"; then
        echo "PAM account lockout enforcement has been successfully configured."
        exit 0
    else
        echo "Failed to configure PAM account lockout enforcement."
        exit 1
    fi
}

#!/bin/bash

# This script ensures audit rules are configured for sudo configuration directory modifications on RHEL 8

set -e

# Define the audit rule for /etc/sudoers.d directory
# This rule monitors write and attribute changes to the sudoers configuration directory
AUDIT_RULE='-w /etc/sudoers.d/ -p wa -k sudoers_modifications'

# Define the auditd rules file location
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/sudoers.rules"

# Ensure audit package is installed
if ! rpm -q audit audit-libs >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    dnf install -y audit audit-libs
fi

# Create the audit rules directory if it doesn't exist
mkdir -p /etc/audit/rules.d

# Check if our specific rule already exists in the sudoers.rules file
if [ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
    # Check if the exact rule exists
    if grep -q "^-w /etc/sudoers.d/ -p wa -k sudoers_modifications$" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
        echo "Audit rule for sudoers directory already exists and is correctly configured"
    else
        # Remove any existing sudoers monitoring rules (with different patterns)
        sed -i '/sudoers/d' "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
        
        # Add our rule
        echo "$AUDIT_RULE" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
        echo "Updated audit rule for sudoers directory in $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    fi
else
    # Create the file with our rule
    echo "$AUDIT_RULE" > "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    echo "Created $AUDIT_RULES_FILE with sudoers audit rule"
fi

# Ensure proper permissions on the rules file
chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

# Disable Ctrl-Alt-Del reboot action on RHEL 8 using systemd
# This approach survives system updates and persists across reboots

if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root"
    exit 1

# This is the systemd way to handle Ctrl-Alt-Del on modern RHEL systems

# Check if the service is currently active or enabled
if systemctl is-enabled ctrl-alt-del.target &>/dev/null 2>&1; then

    systemctl disable ctrl-alt-del.target

# This is idempotent - masking an already masked service does nothing
systemctl mask ctrl-alt-del.target

# Verify the action was successful
if systemctl is-enabled ctrl-alt-del.target &>/dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "Warning: ctrl-alt-del.target is still enabled"
else
    echo "Successfully disabled Ctrl-Alt-Del reboot functionality"
fi

    echo "ctrl-alt-del.target is properly masked"
fi

exit 0

#!/bin/bash
set -e

# Enable and configure GNOME session idle timeout lockdown
# This script enforces a non-overridable session idle delay

# Set the session idle delay value (in seconds) - 900 = 15 minutes
IDLE_DELAY=900

# Create the directory for dconf system database if it doesn't exist
mkdir -p /etc/dconf/db/gdm.d

# Create a dconf profile configuration file for GNOME desktop settings
cat > /etc/dconf/profile/gdm << 'EOF'
user-db:user
system-db:gdm
EOF

# Create the dconf system database directory
mkdir -p /etc/dconf/db/gdm.d

# Create the dconf database file with session idle settings locked down
cat > /etc/dconf/db/gdm.d/00-session-idle << EOF
[org/gnome/desktop/session]
idle-delay=${IDLE_DELAY}

[org/gnome/desktop/screensaver]
lock-enabled=true
EOF

# Lock down the settings so users cannot override them
mkdir -p /etc/dconf/db/gdm.d/locks

cat > /etc/dconf/db/gdm.d/locks/session-idle << 'EOF'
/org/gnome/desktop/session/idle-delay
/org/gnome/desktop/screensaver/lock-enabled
EOF

# Update the dconf database
dconf update

# Ensure GNOME Session is properly configured at the system level
# Create directory for GNOME Session default settings if needed
mkdir -p /etc/gsettings-schema.d

# Update dconf database again to ensure all changes are applied
dconf update

# Verify that the settings are locked by checking dconf
if command -v dconf &> /dev/null; then
    # Read back the setting to confirm it was applied
    dconf_value=$(dconf read /org/gnome/desktop/session/idle-delay 2>/dev/null || echo "uint32 900")
    
    # Ensure the value is set to our idle delay
    if [ "$dconf_value" != "uint32 $IDLE_DELAY" ]; then
        # If not set correctly, update manually
        dconf write /org/gnome/desktop/session/idle-delay "uint32 $IDLE_DELAY"
    fi
fi

# Make sure the GNOME settings are properly enforced for all users
# by updating the system-wide dconf defaults
cat > /etc/dconf/db/local.d/00-idle-timeout << EOF
[org/gnome/desktop/session]
idle-delay=${IDLE_DELAY}

[org/gnome/desktop/screensaver]
lock-enabled=true
EOF

# Lock the idle-delay key to prevent user overrides
mkdir -p /etc/dconf/db/local.d/locks

cat > /etc/dconf/db/local.d/locks/idle-timeout << 'EOF'
/org/gnome/desktop/session/idle-delay
/org/gnome/desktop/screensaver/lock-enabled
EOF

# Update dconf database to apply all changes
dconf update

# Verify that dconf database files exist and are readable
if [ -f /etc/dconf/db/gdm ]; then
    chmod 644 /etc/dconf/db/gdm
fi

if [ -f /etc/dconf/db/local ]; then
    chmod 644 /etc/dconf/db/local
fi

# Ensure proper permissions on configuration directories
chmod 755 /etc/dconf/db
chmod 755 /etc/dconf/profile

# Create a script to persist settings across dconf updates
# This ensures the settings remain locked even if dconf is updated
cat > /etc/profile.d/gnome-idle-lock.sh << 'EOF'
#!/bin/bash
# Ensure GNOME idle timeout is enforced

# Only run if dconf is available
if command -v dconf &> /dev/null; then
    # Set the idle delay to 900 seconds (15 minutes) if not already set
    CURRENT_IDLE=$(dconf read /org/gnome/desktop/session/idle-delay 2>/dev/null)
    if [ "$CURRENT_IDLE" != "uint32 900" ]; then
        dconf write /org/gnome/desktop/session/idle-delay "uint32 900"
    fi
    
    # Ensure lock is enabled
    LOCK_ENABLED=$(dconf read /org/gnome/desktop/screensaver/lock-enabled 2>/dev/null)
    if [ "$LOCK_ENABLED" != "true" ]; then
        dconf write /org/gnome/desktop/screensaver/lock-enabled "true"
    fi
fi
EOF

chmod 644 /etc/profile.d/gnome-idle-lock.sh

# Create a cron job to periodically verify and enforce the settings
cat > /etc/cron.d/gnome-idle-lock-verify << 'EOF'
# Verify GNOME idle timeout settings every hour
0 * * * * root /usr/bin/dconf update >/dev/null 2>&1
EOF

chmod 644 /etc/cron.d/gnome-idle-lock-verify

# Final dconf update to ensure all changes are applied
dconf update

# Success message
echo "GNOME session idle timeout has been configured and locked down."
echo "Idle delay is set to 900 seconds (15 minutes)"
echo "Users cannot override this setting through GUI preferences"

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Disable kexec (kernel image loading) to prevent malicious kernel boot without reboot

# Check if running as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root"
    exit 1
fi

# Check if system is RHEL 8
if ! grep -q "release 8" /etc/redhat-release 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Warning: This system does not appear to be RHEL 8"
fi

# Runtime disabling of kexec (cannot be re-enabled without reboot)
if [[ -f /proc/sys/kernel/kexec_load_disabled ]]; then
    current_value=$(cat /proc/sys/kernel/kexec_load_disabled)
    if [[ "$current_value" != "1" ]]; then
        echo 1 > /proc/sys/kernel/kexec_load_disabled
        if [[ $(cat /proc/sys/kernel/kexec_load_disabled) -eq 1 ]]; then
            echo "Runtime: kexec loading disabled successfully"
        else
            echo "Error: Failed to disable kexec at runtime"
            exit 1
        fi
    else
        echo "Runtime: kexec loading already disabled"
    fi
else
    echo "Warning: /proc/sys/kernel/kexec_load_disabled not found"
fi

# Persistent configuration via kernel boot parameter
sysctl_file="/etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-kexec.conf"

# Create sysctl configuration file for persistence across reboots
if [[ ! -f "$sysctl_file" ]]; then
    echo "kernel.kexec_load_disabled = 1" > "$sysctl_file"
    chmod 644 "$sysctl_file"
    echo "Persistent: Created $sysctl_file"
else
    # Check if the setting already exists
    if grep -q "kernel.kexec_load_disabled" "$sysctl_file"; then
        # Update the value if it exists
        sed -i 's/^kernel\.kexec_load_disabled\s*=\s*.*/kernel.kexec_load_disabled = 1/' "$sysctl_file"
        echo "Persistent: Updated existing kexec_load_disabled setting in $sysctl_file"
    else
        # Append if file exists but setting doesn't
        echo "kernel.kexec_load_disabled = 1" >> "$sysctl_file"
        echo "Persistent: Added kexec_load_disabled setting to $sysctl_file"
    fi
fi

# Apply the sysctl settings
sysctl -p "$sysctl_file" > /dev/null 2>&1 || true

# Also configure via GRUB to disable kexec at boot time (additional defense layer)
grub_config="/etc/default/grub"
if [[ -f "$grub_config" ]]; then
    # Check if kexec_load_disabled is already in GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
    if ! grep -q "kexec_load_disabled=1" "$grub_config"; then
        # Add the parameter to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX (or create it if it doesn't exist)
        if grep -q "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" "$grub_config"; then
            # Append to existing GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
            sed -i 's/^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX="\(.*\)"$/GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX="\1 kexec_load_disabled=1"/' "$grub_config"
        else
            # Create new GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX variable
            echo 'GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX="kexec_load_disabled=1"' >> "$grub_config"
        fi
        
        # Regenerate GRUB configuration
        if command -v grub2-mkconfig &> /dev/null; then
            grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
            echo "GRUB: Updated boot configuration with kexec_load_disabled=1 parameter"
        fi
    else
        echo "GRUB: kexec_load_disabled parameter already present"
    fi
fi

# Verify the runtime setting is disabled
if [[ -f /proc/sys/kernel/kexec_load_disabled ]]; then
    final_value=$(cat /proc/sys/kernel/kexec_load_disabled)
    if [[ "$final_value" -eq 1 ]]; then
        echo "Verification: kexec loading is currently disabled"
    else
        echo "Error: kexec loading is still enabled"
        exit 1
    fi
fi

echo "Successfully disabled kernel image loading (kexec) at runtime and configured persistence"
exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Script to configure SSH server on RHEL 8 for immediate idle-timeout disconnection
# without additional keep-alive probes

set -e

# Define SSH configuration file
SSH_CONFIG_FILE="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
SSH_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"

# Create SSH config directory if it doesn't exist (for modular configuration)
if [ ! -d "$SSH_CONFIG_DIR" ]; then
    mkdir -p "$SSH_CONFIG_DIR"
    chmod 755 "$SSH_CONFIG_DIR"
fi

# Create a new configuration file for idle-timeout settings
CUSTOM_CONFIG_FILE="$SSH_CONFIG_DIR/99-idle-timeout.conf"

# SSH configuration parameters for immediate idle-timeout disconnection
# ClientAliveInterval: seconds between keep-alive messages (we'll set to 300 = 5 minutes)
# ClientAliveCountMax: number of keep-alive messages to send before disconnecting (set to 0 for immediate disconnect)
cat > "$CUSTOM_CONFIG_FILE" << 'EOF'
# Configuration for immediate idle-timeout disconnection
# This configuration ensures SSH connections drop immediately after the idle interval elapses
# without waiting for additional keep-alive probe responses

# Idle timeout interval in seconds (300 = 5 minutes)
ClientAliveInterval 300

# Number of keep-alive probes to send before disconnecting (0 = disconnect immediately)
ClientAliveCountMax 0
EOF

# Set proper permissions on the custom config file
chmod 644 "$CUSTOM_CONFIG_FILE"

# Verify SSH configuration syntax
if ! sshd -t 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "SSH configuration syntax error. Removing the problematic config file."
    rm -f "$CUSTOM_CONFIG_FILE"
    exit 1
fi

# Check if we need to reload/restart SSH service
# Get the current SSH service status
if systemctl is-active --quiet sshd; then
    # Reload SSH configuration to apply changes to new connections
    systemctl reload sshd
else
    # Enable and start SSH if it's not running
    systemctl enable sshd
    systemctl start sshd
fi

# Verify that the configuration is in place by checking the running SSH daemon
# We'll use sshd -T to show the effective configuration
EFFECTIVE_CLIENT_ALIVE_INTERVAL=$(sshd -T 2>/dev/null | grep "^clientaliveinterval " | awk '{print $2}')
EFFECTIVE_CLIENT_ALIVE_COUNT=$(sshd -T 2>/dev/null | grep "^clientalivecount " | awk '{print $2}')

# Verify the settings
if [ "$EFFECTIVE_CLIENT_ALIVE_COUNT" = "0" ]; then
    # Configuration is correct
    exit 0
else
    echo "Failed to configure SSH with ClientAliveCountMax=0. Current value: $EFFECTIVE_CLIENT_ALIVE_COUNT"
    exit 1
fi

#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Enable and start the audit daemon
systemctl enable auditd
systemctl start auditd

# Get the fully qualified domain name
FQDN=$(hostname -f)

# Create or update the audit.rules file to include the node name identifier
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules"
AUDIT_CONFIG_FILE="/etc/audit/audit.rules"

# Ensure the rules.d directory exists
mkdir -p /etc/audit/rules.d

# Function to add node name identifier if not present
add_node_identifier() {
    local rules_file="$1"
    
    # Check if the file exists and already has the node identifier
    if grep -q "^-D$" "$rules_file" 2>/dev/null; then
        # Check if node identifier is already present
        if grep -q "\-N.*$FQDN" "$rules_file" 2>/dev/null; then
            return 0
        else
            # Remove the old node identifier line if it exists
            sed -i "/^-N/d" "$rules_file"
            # Add the new node identifier after -D line
            sed -i "/^-D$/a\\-N $FQDN" "$rules_file"
            return 1
        fi
    else
        # If file doesn't have -D flag, add both
        if ! grep -q "\-N.*$FQDN" "$rules_file" 2>/dev/null; then
            echo "-D" >> "$rules_file"
            echo "-N $FQDN" >> "$rules_file"
            return 1
        fi
        return 0
    fi
}

# Check if we need to reload the audit daemon
RELOAD_NEEDED=0

# Handle /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules
if [ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
    if ! add_node_identifier "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
        RELOAD_NEEDED=1
    fi
else
    # Create the file with required settings
    echo "-D" > "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    echo "-N $FQDN" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    echo "-b 8192" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    echo "-f 1" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    RELOAD_NEEDED=1
fi

# Also configure the main audit configuration file for auditd settings
if [ ! -f "$AUDIT_CONFIG_FILE" ] || ! grep -q "node_name = $FQDN" "$AUDIT_CONFIG_FILE"; then
    # Ensure the file exists
    if [ ! -f "$AUDIT_CONFIG_FILE" ]; then
        touch "$AUDIT_CONFIG_FILE"
    fi
    
    # Remove old node_name entries if they exist
    sed -i "/^node_name = /d" "$AUDIT_CONFIG_FILE"
    
    # Add the node_name setting
    echo "node_name = $FQDN" >> "$AUDIT_CONFIG_FILE"
    RELOAD_NEEDED=1
fi

# Verify and load the audit rules
if [ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
    # Test the rules syntax
    if augenrules --check 2>/dev/null || auditctl -l &>/dev/null; then
        if [ $RELOAD_NEEDED -eq 1 ]; then
            # Load the new rules
            augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || auditctl -R "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
            # Restart auditd to ensure the node identifier is applied
            systemctl restart auditd
        fi
    fi
fi

# Enable audit at boot
auditctl -b 8192
auditctl -f 1

# Verify the configuration
if auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -q "^-N $FQDN"; then
    exit 0
elif grep -q "^-N $FQDN" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
    # Configuration is set in rules file, will take effect after next boot
    exit 0
else
    # Set it directly for immediate effect
    auditctl -N "$FQDN"
    exit 0
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Script to ensure SSH server uses centralized cryptographic policy with FIPS 140-2 validated ciphers
set -euo pipefail

# Define SSH configuration paths
SSH_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/ssh"
SSH_CONFIG_FILE="${SSH_CONFIG_DIR}/sshd_config"
SSH_CONFIG_D_DIR="${SSH_CONFIG_DIR}/sshd_config.d"
CRYPTO_POLICY_DIR="/etc/crypto-policies/back-ends/opensshserver.config"

# Define the expected ciphers for FIPS 140-2 compliance (strongest-to-weakest)
FIPS_CIPHERS="aes256-gcm@openssh.com,aes128-gcm@openssh.com,aes256-ctr,aes192-ctr,aes128-ctr"

# Function to check if running on RHEL 8
check_rhel8() {
    if [ ! -f /etc/redhat-release ]; then
        echo "Error: This system is not RHEL-based"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    # Check for RHEL 8
    if ! grep -q "release 8" /etc/redhat-release; then
        echo "Warning: This system does not appear to be RHEL 8"
    fi
}

# Function to ensure update-crypto-policies is available
ensure_crypto_policy_tool() {
    if ! command -v update-crypto-policies &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Installing crypto-policies package..."
        yum install -y crypto-policies
    fi
}

# Function to set system-wide crypto policy to FIPS
set_system_crypto_policy() {
    echo "Setting system crypto policy to FIPS..."
    update-crypto-policies --set FIPS
}

# Function to remove SSH cipher overrides from sshd_config
remove_cipher_overrides() {
    if [ -f "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE" ]; then
        echo "Removing any local cipher overrides from $SSH_CONFIG_FILE..."
        # Comment out or remove any Ciphers, MACs, KexAlgorithms, and HostKeyAlgorithms lines
        sed -i 's/^Ciphers /#Ciphers /g' "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE"
        sed -i 's/^MACs /#MACs /g' "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE"
        sed -i 's/^KexAlgorithms /#KexAlgorithms /g' "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE"
        sed -i 's/^HostKeyAlgorithms /#HostKeyAlgorithms /g' "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE"
    fi
}

# Function to remove SSH cipher overrides from sshd_config.d
remove_cipher_overrides_from_sshd_config_d() {
    if [ -d "$SSH_CONFIG_D_DIR" ]; then
        echo "Checking for cipher overrides in $SSH_CONFIG_D_DIR..."
        for config_file in "$SSH_CONFIG_D_DIR"/*.conf; do
            if [ -f "$config_file" ]; then
                # Only process if file contains cipher-related configurations
                if grep -q "^Ciphers\|^MACs\|^KexAlgorithms\|^HostKeyAlgorithms" "$config_file"; then
                    echo "Removing cipher overrides from $config_file..."
                    sed -i 's/^Ciphers /#Ciphers /g' "$config_file"
                    sed -i 's/^MACs /#MACs /g' "$config_file"
                    sed -i 's/^KexAlgorithms /#KexAlgorithms /g' "$config_file"
                    sed -i 's/^HostKeyAlgorithms /#HostKeyAlgorithms /g' "$config_file"
                fi
            fi
        done
    fi
}

# Function to verify opensshserver.config backend
verify_opensshserver_backend() {
    echo "Verifying opensshserver backend configuration..."
    
    # Check if the opensshserver.config backend exists for FIPS policy
    BACKEND_FILE="/etc/crypto-policies/back-ends/opensshserver.config"
    
    if [ ! -f "$BACKEND_FILE" ]; then
        echo "Backend file $BACKEND_FILE not found. The system might not have FIPS policy properly configured."
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Check if Ciphers line exists and is uncommented
    if ! grep -q "^Ciphers " "$BACKEND_FILE"; then
        echo "Warning: Ciphers line not properly configured in $BACKEND_FILE"
        # If file exists but doesn't have proper cipher config, we might need to ensure it's set
        if grep -q "#Ciphers\|^[^#]*Ciphers" "$BACKEND_FILE"; then
            echo "Uncommenting Ciphers line in $BACKEND_FILE..."
            sed -i 's/^#Ciphers /Ciphers /g' "$BACKEND_FILE"
        fi
    fi
    
    return 0
}

# Function to ensure SSH service uses the system crypto policy
configure_ssh_for_system_policy() {
    echo "Ensuring SSH configuration uses system crypto policy..."
    
    # Make sure sshd_config includes the crypto-policies configuration
    if [ -d "$SSH_CONFIG_D_DIR" ]; then
        # Create 01-crypto-policies.conf if it doesn't exist
        CRYPTO_CONF="${SSH_CONFIG_D_DIR}/01-crypto-policies.conf"
        if [ ! -f "$CRYPTO_CONF" ]; then
            echo "# This file is managed by system crypto-policies" > "$CRYPTO_CONF"
            echo "# Configuration is inherited from /etc/crypto-policies/back-ends/opensshserver.config" >> "$CRYPTO_CONF"
            chmod 644 "$CRYPTO_CONF"
        fi
    fi
}

# Function to restart SSH service
restart_ssh_service() {
    echo "Restarting SSH service..."
    systemctl restart sshd
}

# Function to verify SSH configuration
verify_ssh_configuration() {
    echo "Verifying SSH configuration..."
    
    # Check if sshd can parse the configuration
    if sshd -t &> /dev/null; then
        echo "SSH configuration is valid"
        return 0
    else
        echo "Error: SSH configuration has errors"
        sshd -t
        return 1
    fi
}

# Main execution
main() {
    echo "=== SSH Server Cryptographic Policy Remediation ==="
    
    # Check system requirements
    check_rhel8
    
    # Ensure crypto-policies tool is installed
    ensure_crypto_policy_tool
    
    # Set system crypto policy to FIPS
    set_system_crypto_policy
    
    # Remove any local SSH cipher overrides
    remove_cipher_overrides
    remove_cipher_overrides_from_sshd_config_d
    
    # Verify and ensure proper backend configuration
    verify_opensshserver_backend
    
    # Configure SSH to use system policy
    configure_ssh_for_system_policy
    
    # Verify SSH configuration before restarting
    if ! verify_ssh_configuration; then
        echo "Error: SSH configuration validation failed"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    # Restart SSH service to apply changes
    restart_ssh_service
    
    # Final verification
    echo "=== Remediation Complete ==="
    echo "System crypto policy: $(update-crypto-policies --show)"
    
    # Show current SSH cipher configuration (if available)
    if grep -q "^Ciphers" /etc/crypto-policies/back-ends/opensshserver.config 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "SSH Ciphers configuration from system policy:"
        grep "^Ciphers" /etc/crypto-policies/back-ends/opensshserver.config || echo "No explicit cipher configuration found"
    fi
}

# Run main function
main
exit 0

# RHEL 8 Audit Configuration Script for File Deletion via Rename
# This script ensures audit rules are configured to track file deletion via rename operations

# Check if running as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
   echo "This script must be run as root"
   exit 1
fi

# Ensure audit daemon is installed
if ! command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Installing audit package..."
    yum install -y audit audit-libs
fi

# Enable and start the audit daemon
systemctl enable auditd
systemctl start auditd

# Create a temporary audit rules file with the deletion tracking rules
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/deletion_tracking.rules"

# Ensure the audit rules directory exists
mkdir -p "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR"

# Create the audit rules for file deletion tracking via rename
cat > "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" << 'EOF'
# Audit rules for file deletion via rename operations
# These rules track attempts to remove files using rename system calls
# for users with UID 1000 and above (regular users and root)

# 32-bit system call interface for rename
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S rename -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k deletion

# 64-bit system call interface for rename  
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S rename -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k deletion

# 32-bit system call interface for renameat
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S renameat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k deletion

# 64-bit system call interface for renameat
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S renameat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k deletion

# 32-bit system call interface for renameat2
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S renameat2 -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k deletion

# 64-bit system call interface for renameat2
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S renameat2 -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k deletion
EOF

# Load the rules into the audit system
auditctl -R "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

# Make sure the rules persist across reboots by updating the main audit.rules file
# Remove any existing deletion tracking rules to ensure idempotency
sed -i '/^-a always,exit.*rename.*auid>=1000.*k deletion$/d' /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules 2>/dev/null || true

# Append the deletion tracking rules to the main audit.rules file if they don't already exist
if ! grep -q "k deletion" /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules 2>/dev/null; then
    cat >> /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules << 'EOF'

# Audit rules for file deletion via rename operations (added by remediation script)
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S rename -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k deletion
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S rename -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k deletion
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S renameat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k deletion
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S renameat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k deletion
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S renameat2 -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k deletion
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S renameat2 -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k deletion
EOF
fi

# Reload the audit rules to ensure they take effect immediately
auditctl -R /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules 2>/dev/null || true

# Verify the rules are loaded
echo "Verifying audit rules for deletion tracking..."
auditctl -l | grep -E "rename.*deletion|renameat.*deletion" || echo "Note: Rules may be loaded but grep output format may differ"

# Ensure audit daemon is running
if ! systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
    echo "Starting audit daemon..."
    systemctl start auditd
fi

echo "Audit configuration for file deletion tracking completed successfully."
echo "The system will now audit all file deletion attempts via rename operations for users with UID >= 1000."

    aide --init

        mv /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz
    fi

#!/bin/bash

# Run AIDE check and capture output

EXIT_CODE=$?

# Send results to root mailbox
{
    echo "AIDE Integrity Check Report"
    echo "============================"
    echo "Timestamp: $(date)"
    echo "Exit Code: $EXIT_CODE"
    echo ""
    echo "AIDE Output:"
    echo "$AIDE_OUTPUT"
} | mail -s "AIDE Integrity Check Report - $(hostname)" root

exit $EXIT_CODE
EOF

chmod 755 "$AIDE_CHECK_SCRIPT"

# Create or update the cron job for AIDE checks
# Schedule for 2 AM daily
CRON_JOB="0 2 * * * $AIDE_CHECK_SCRIPT"

if [ -f "$CRON_FILE" ]; then
    # Remove the old job if it exists
    grep -v "aide-check-with-email.sh" "$CRON_FILE" > "$CRON_FILE.tmp" || true
    if [ -s "$CRON_FILE.tmp" ]; then
        mv "$CRON_FILE.tmp" "$CRON_FILE"
    else
        rm -f "$CRON_FILE.tmp"
        rm -f "$CRON_FILE"
    fi
fi

# Add the new cron job
echo "$CRON_JOB" > "$CRON_FILE"
chmod 644 "$CRON_FILE"

echo "Configuration details:"

echo ""
echo "To test the configuration, run:"
echo "  $AIDE_CHECK_SCRIPT"

#!/bin/bash

set -e

# SSH server configuration file
SSHD_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
BACKUP_DIR="/var/backups/sshd_config_backups"
TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)

# Create backup directory if it doesn't exist
if [ ! -d "$BACKUP_DIR" ]; then
    mkdir -p "$BACKUP_DIR"
fi

# Create a backup of the current sshd_config
cp "$SSHD_CONFIG" "$BACKUP_DIR/sshd_config.${TIMESTAMP}.bak"

# Function to check if a configuration option exists and set it appropriately
ensure_ssh_option() {
    local option=$1
    local value=$2
    local config_file=$3
    
    # Check if the option exists in the config
    if grep -q "^${option}" "$config_file"; then
        # Option exists, update it
        sed -i "s/^${option}.*/${option} ${value}/" "$config_file"
    else
        # Check for commented out version
        if grep -q "^#${option}" "$config_file"; then
            # Uncomment and set the value
            sed -i "s/^#${option}.*/${option} ${value}/" "$config_file"
        else
            # Option doesn't exist, add it at the end
            echo "${option} ${value}" >> "$config_file"
        fi
    fi
}

# Ensure IgnoreUserKnownHosts is set to yes
# This prevents SSH from using user's known_hosts file for host authentication
ensure_ssh_option "IgnoreUserKnownHosts" "yes" "$SSHD_CONFIG"

# Ensure IgnoreRhosts is set to yes (security best practice)
# This prevents SSH from using .rhosts or .shosts files
ensure_ssh_option "IgnoreRhosts" "yes" "$SSHD_CONFIG"

# Ensure HostbasedAuthentication is disabled
# This prevents authentication based on host names and user names
ensure_ssh_option "HostbasedAuthentication" "no" "$SSHD_CONFIG"

echo "RhostsAuthentication: $(grep -w 'RhostsAuthentication' $SSHD_CONFIG | awk '{print $2}')"

```bash
#!/bin/bash

set -e

# SSH server configuration file
SSHD_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
BACKUP_DIR="/var/backups/sshd_config_backups"
TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S)

# Create backup directory if it doesn't exist
if [ ! -d "$BACKUP_DIR" ]; then
    mkdir -p "$BACKUP_DIR"
fi

# Create a backup of the current sshd_config
cp "$SSHD_CONFIG" "$BACKUP_DIR/sshd_config.${TIMESTAMP}.bak"

# Function to check if a configuration option exists and set it appropriately
ensure_ssh_option() {
    local option=$1
    local value=$2
    local config_file=$3
    
    # Check if the option exists in the config
    if grep -q "^${option}" "$config_file"; then
        # Option exists, update it
        sed -i "s/^${option}.*/${option} ${value}/" "$config_file"
    else
        # Check for commented out version
        if grep -q "^#${option}" "$config_file"; then
            # Uncomment and set the value
            sed -i "s/^#${option}.*/${option} ${value}/" "$config_file"
        else
            # Option doesn't exist, add it at the end
            echo "${option} ${value}" >> "$config_file"
        fi
    fi
}

# Ensure IgnoreUserKnownHosts is set to yes
# This prevents SSH from using user's known_hosts file for host authentication
ensure_ssh_option "IgnoreUserKnownHosts" "yes" "$SSHD_CONFIG"

# Ensure IgnoreRhosts is set to yes (security best practice)
# This prevents SSH from using .rhosts or .shosts files
ensure_ssh_option "IgnoreRhosts" "yes" "$SSHD_CONFIG"

# Ensure HostbasedAuthentication is disabled
# This prevents authentication based on host names and user names
ensure_ssh_option "HostbasedAuthentication" "no" "$SSHD_CONFIG"

# Ensure RhostsRSAAuthentication is disabled (deprecated but good to set)
ensure_ssh_option "RhostsRSAAuthentication" "no" "$SSHD_CONFIG"

# Ensure RhostsAuthentication is disabled
ensure_ssh_option "RhostsAuthentication" "no" "$SSHD_CONFIG"

# Validate the configuration syntax
if ! sshd -t > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "Error: SSH configuration validation failed. Restoring backup..."
    cp "$BACKUP_DIR/sshd_config.${TIMESTAMP}.bak" "$SSHD_CONFIG"
    exit 1
fi

# Check if SSH service is running and restart it if necessary
if systemctl is-active --quiet sshd; then
    systemctl reload sshd
fi

# Ensure the SSH service is enabled to start at boot
systemctl enable sshd

# Verify the configuration was applied correctly
echo "Verification of SSH configuration:"
echo "IgnoreUserKnownHosts: $(grep -w 'IgnoreUserKnownHosts' $SSHD_CONFIG | awk '{print $2}')"
echo "IgnoreRhosts: $(grep -w 'IgnoreRhosts' $SSHD_CONFIG | awk '{print $2}')"
echo "HostbasedAuthentication: $(grep -w 'HostbasedAuthentication' $SSHD_CONFIG | awk '{print $2}')"
echo "RhostsRSAAuthentication: $(grep -w 'RhostsRSAAuthentication' $SSHD_CONFIG | awk '{print $2}')"
echo "RhostsAuthentication: $(grep -w 'RhostsAuthentication' $SSHD_CONFIG | awk '{print $2}')"

echo "SSH hardening configuration applied successfully."
echo "Backup of previous configuration saved to: $BACKUP_DIR/sshd_config.${TIMESTAMP}.bak"
```

#!/bin/bash

# Kernel Page-Table Isolation (KPTI) enablement script for RHEL 8
# This script enables KPTI to mitigate Meltdown and strengthen KASLR defenses

set -euo pipefail

# Verify running as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root"
    exit 1
fi

# Verify RHEL 8 system
if ! grep -q "VERSION_ID=\"8" /etc/os-release; then
    echo "This script is designed for RHEL 8 only"
    exit 1
fi

# Function to enable KPTI in GRUB2 configuration
enable_kpti_grub2() {
    local grub_config="/etc/default/grub"
    
    # Verify GRUB2 configuration file exists
    if [[ ! -f "$grub_config" ]]; then
        echo "GRUB2 configuration file not found at $grub_config"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    # Check if pti parameter already exists
    if grep -q "GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX.*pti" "$grub_config"; then
        echo "KPTI (pti=on) is already enabled in GRUB2 configuration"
    else
        # Add pti=on to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX if not present
        if grep -q "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" "$grub_config"; then
            # Remove trailing quote if present and add pti parameter
            sed -i 's/^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX="\(.*\)"$/GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX="\1 pti=on"/' "$grub_config"
        else
            # Create GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX entry if it doesn't exist
            echo 'GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX="pti=on"' >> "$grub_config"
        fi
        echo "KPTI parameter added to GRUB2 configuration"
    fi
}

# Function to regenerate GRUB2 configuration
regenerate_grub2_config() {
    # Check if system uses UEFI or BIOS
    if [[ -d /sys/firmware/efi ]]; then
        # UEFI system
        grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg
        echo "GRUB2 configuration regenerated for UEFI system"
    else
        # BIOS system
        grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg
        echo "GRUB2 configuration regenerated for BIOS system"
    fi
}

# Function to verify KPTI is enabled in kernel command line
verify_kpti_enabled() {
    if grep -q "pti=on" /proc/cmdline 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "KPTI is currently active (pti=on found in /proc/cmdline)"
        return 0
    elif grep -q "pti=off" /proc/cmdline 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "Warning: KPTI is currently disabled (pti=off found in /proc/cmdline)"
        return 1
    else
        echo "KPTI setting not found in current kernel command line"
        echo "KPTI will be enabled after next reboot"
        return 0
    fi
}

# Main execution
echo "Enabling Kernel Page-Table Isolation (KPTI) on RHEL 8..."

# Enable KPTI in GRUB2
enable_kpti_grub2

# Regenerate GRUB2 configuration to apply changes to all kernels
regenerate_grub2_config

# Verify current status
verify_kpti_enabled

echo ""
echo "KPTI enablement complete."
echo "The pti=on parameter has been added to the kernel boot parameters."
echo "This will apply to all currently installed kernels and any newly installed kernels."
echo "Please reboot the system for changes to take effect: sudo reboot"

#!/bin/bash
set -e

# Configure kernel networking stack to reject source-routed IPv6 packets
# This disables acceptance of source-routed packets on all IPv6 interfaces

# Apply runtime changes using sysctl
sysctl -w net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_source_route=0
sysctl -w net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_source_route=0

# Ensure the changes persist across reboots by writing to sysctl configuration
SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE="/etc/sysctl.d/99-ipv6-source-route.conf"

# Create or update the sysctl configuration file
cat > "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE" << 'EOF'
# Disable acceptance of source-routed IPv6 packets
# Source-routed packets can be used to bypass network security controls
net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_source_route = 0
net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_source_route = 0
EOF

# Set appropriate permissions
chmod 644 "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE"

# Verify the runtime settings
echo "Verifying runtime settings..."
all_setting=$(sysctl -n net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_source_route)
default_setting=$(sysctl -n net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_source_route)

if [ "$all_setting" = "0" ] && [ "$default_setting" = "0" ]; then
    echo "SUCCESS: Source-routed IPv6 packets are now disabled"
    echo "  - net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_source_route = $all_setting"
    echo "  - net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_source_route = $default_setting"
    echo "  - Configuration persisted in $SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE"
else
    echo "ERROR: Failed to disable source-routed IPv6 packets"
    exit 1
fi

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# RHEL 8 Smart Card Support Installation Script
# This script ensures that smart-card support for multifactor authentication is installed

set -e

# Function to check if a package is installed
is_installed() {
    rpm -q "$1" &>/dev/null
    return $?
}

# Check if running on RHEL 8
if ! grep -q "release 8" /etc/redhat-release 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Warning: This system may not be RHEL 8. Proceeding anyway..."
fi

# Main smart card support package
PCSC_PACKAGE="pcsc-lite"
PCSC_LIBS_PACKAGE="pcsc-lite-libs"

# Additional packages for smart card support
OPENSC_PACKAGE="opensc"
AUTHSELECT_PACKAGE="authselect"
PCSCLITE_DEVEL="pcsc-lite-devel"

# Ensure DNF/YUM is updated
echo "Updating package manager..."
dnf makecache &>/dev/null || yum makecache &>/dev/null || true

# Install smart card support packages
echo "Installing smart card support packages..."

# Install pcsc-lite (core smart card daemon)
if ! is_installed "$PCSC_PACKAGE"; then
    echo "Installing $PCSC_PACKAGE..."
    dnf install -y "$PCSC_PACKAGE" || yum install -y "$PCSC_PACKAGE"
else
    echo "$PCSC_PACKAGE is already installed"
fi

# Install pcsc-lite-libs (libraries for smart card support)
if ! is_installed "$PCSC_LIBS_PACKAGE"; then
    echo "Installing $PCSC_LIBS_PACKAGE..."
    dnf install -y "$PCSC_LIBS_PACKAGE" || yum install -y "$PCSC_LIBS_PACKAGE"
else
    echo "$PCSC_LIBS_PACKAGE is already installed"
fi

# Install OpenSC for smart card support
if ! is_installed "$OPENSC_PACKAGE"; then
    echo "Installing $OPENSC_PACKAGE..."
    dnf install -y "$OPENSC_PACKAGE" || yum install -y "$OPENSC_PACKAGE"
else
    echo "$OPENSC_PACKAGE is already installed"
fi

# Install authselect for MFA configuration
if ! is_installed "$AUTHSELECT_PACKAGE"; then
    echo "Installing $AUTHSELECT_PACKAGE..."
    dnf install -y "$AUTHSELECT_PACKAGE" || yum install -y "$AUTHSELECT_PACKAGE"
else
    echo "$AUTHSELECT_PACKAGE is already installed"
fi

# Ensure pcscd service is enabled and started
echo "Configuring pcscd service..."
systemctl enable pcscd
systemctl start pcscd

# Verify installation
echo "Verifying smart card support installation..."
if is_installed "$PCSC_PACKAGE" && is_installed "$PCSC_LIBS_PACKAGE" && is_installed "$OPENSC_PACKAGE"; then
    echo "Smart card support successfully installed and configured"
    echo "Smart card daemon (pcscd) service status:"
    systemctl status pcscd --no-pager || true
    exit 0
else
    echo "Warning: Some smart card packages may not be installed"
    exit 1
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Audit rule configuration for sudoers file monitoring
AUDIT_RULE="-w /etc/sudoers -p wa -k sudoers_changes"
AUDIT_RULE_DIR="-w /etc/sudoers.d/ -p wa -k sudoers_changes"

# Determine if we're using augenrules or auditctl
if command -v augenrules &> /dev/null && [ -d /etc/audit/rules.d ]; then
    RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
    RULES_FILE="${RULES_DIR}/sudoers_changes.rules"
    AUDIT_TOOL="augenrules"
else
    RULES_DIR="/etc/audit"
    RULES_FILE="${RULES_DIR}/audit.rules"
    AUDIT_TOOL="auditctl"
fi

# Create rules directory if it doesn't exist
mkdir -p "${RULES_DIR}"

# Check if the sudoers monitoring rule already exists in the rules file
if [ -f "${RULES_FILE}" ]; then
    if grep -q "sudoers_changes" "${RULES_FILE}"; then
        echo "Audit rule for sudoers already configured in ${RULES_FILE}"
        RULE_EXISTS=true
    else
        RULE_EXISTS=false
    fi
else
    RULE_EXISTS=false
fi

# Add the audit rules if they don't exist
if [ "$RULE_EXISTS" = false ]; then
    echo "${AUDIT_RULE}" >> "${RULES_FILE}"
    echo "${AUDIT_RULE_DIR}" >> "${RULES_FILE}"
    echo "Added audit rules for sudoers monitoring to ${RULES_FILE}"
fi

# Load the rules using appropriate tool
if [ "${AUDIT_TOOL}" = "augenrules" ]; then
    augenrules --load
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        echo "Successfully loaded audit rules with augenrules"
    else
        echo "Failed to load audit rules with augenrules" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
else
    # For auditctl, we need to ensure the rules are added to the main audit.rules file
    if [ ! -f "${RULES_FILE}" ] || ! grep -q "sudoers_changes" "${RULES_FILE}"; then
        echo "${AUDIT_RULE}" >> "${RULES_FILE}"
        echo "${AUDIT_RULE_DIR}" >> "${RULES_FILE}"
    fi
    auditctl -R "${RULES_FILE}" 2>/dev/null || auditctl -w /etc/sudoers -p wa -k sudoers_changes && auditctl -w /etc/sudoers.d/ -p wa -k sudoers_changes
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        echo "Successfully loaded audit rules with auditctl"
    else
        echo "Failed to load audit rules with auditctl" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
fi

# Verify the rules are loaded
if auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -q "sudoers_changes"; then
    echo "Audit rule verification successful"
else
    echo "Warning: Could not verify audit rule is loaded" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Ensure the audit daemon service is enabled and running
systemctl enable auditd
systemctl start auditd

echo "Sudoers audit monitoring configured successfully"
exit 0

#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Script to configure systemd login manager for automatic session termination
# on inactivity

LOGIND_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/systemd/logind.conf.d"
LOGIND_CONFIG_FILE="${LOGIND_CONFIG_DIR}/99-session-idle-timeout.conf"

# Ensure the configuration directory exists
mkdir -p "$LOGIND_CONFIG_DIR"

# Define the idle timeout in seconds (900 seconds = 15 minutes)
IDLE_TIMEOUT_SEC=900

# Function to check if a key exists in a configuration file
key_exists() {
    local file="$1"
    local key="$2"
    
    if [[ ! -f "$file" ]]; then
        return 1
    fi
    
    grep -q "^${key}=" "$file"
}

# Function to get the current value of a key
get_key_value() {
    local file="$1"
    local key="$2"
    
    if [[ ! -f "$file" ]]; then
        echo ""
        return
    fi
    
    grep "^${key}=" "$file" | cut -d'=' -f2- || echo ""
}

# Create or update the configuration file
if [[ ! -f "$LOGIND_CONFIG_FILE" ]]; then
    # Create new configuration file
    cat > "$LOGIND_CONFIG_FILE" << EOF
# Session idle timeout configuration
# Sessions will be terminated after this period of inactivity
# Value is in seconds
IdleAction=terminate
IdleActionSec=${IDLE_TIMEOUT_SEC}
EOF
    chmod 0644 "$LOGIND_CONFIG_FILE"
else
    # Check if IdleActionSec is already set to the desired value
    CURRENT_IDLE_ACTION=$(get_key_value "$LOGIND_CONFIG_FILE" "IdleAction" || echo "")
    CURRENT_IDLE_SEC=$(get_key_value "$LOGIND_CONFIG_FILE" "IdleActionSec" || echo "")
    
    # Update file if values don't match desired settings
    if [[ "$CURRENT_IDLE_ACTION" != "terminate" ]] || [[ "$CURRENT_IDLE_SEC" != "$IDLE_TIMEOUT_SEC" ]]; then
        # Remove old settings if they exist
        if key_exists "$LOGIND_CONFIG_FILE" "IdleAction"; then
            sed -i '/^IdleAction=/d' "$LOGIND_CONFIG_FILE"
        fi
        if key_exists "$LOGIND_CONFIG_FILE" "IdleActionSec"; then
            sed -i '/^IdleActionSec=/d' "$LOGIND_CONFIG_FILE"
        fi
        
        # Add the new settings
        echo "IdleAction=terminate" >> "$LOGIND_CONFIG_FILE"
        echo "IdleActionSec=${IDLE_TIMEOUT_SEC}" >> "$LOGIND_CONFIG_FILE"
    fi
fi

# Verify the configuration was written correctly
if ! grep -q "^IdleAction=terminate" "$LOGIND_CONFIG_FILE"; then
    echo "ERROR: Failed to set IdleAction in $LOGIND_CONFIG_FILE"
    exit 1
fi

if ! grep -q "^IdleActionSec=${IDLE_TIMEOUT_SEC}" "$LOGIND_CONFIG_FILE"; then
    echo "ERROR: Failed to set IdleActionSec in $LOGIND_CONFIG_FILE"
    exit 1
fi

# Reload systemd-logind to apply changes
systemctl reload systemd-logind

echo "SUCCESS: Session idle timeout configured to ${IDLE_TIMEOUT_SEC} seconds (15 minutes)"
echo "Configuration written to: $LOGIND_CONFIG_FILE"

#!/bin/bash

# RHEL 8 Shell Initialization Files Permission Hardening Script
# Ensures all interactive user shell initialization files are no more permissive than 0740

# Target files to check and fix
TARGET_FILES=(
    ".bashrc"
    ".bash_profile"
    ".bash_login"
    ".profile"
    ".zshrc"
    ".zsh_profile"
    ".cshrc"
    ".tcshrc"
    ".login"
    ".kshrc"
)

# Maximum allowed permissions in octal (0740 = rwxr-----)
MAX_PERMS=0740

# Function to get current permissions in octal
get_perms() {
    stat -c "%a" "$1" 2>/dev/null || echo "000"
}

# Function to compare octal permissions
# Returns 0 if current <= max (less permissive or equal)
# Returns 1 if current > max (more permissive)
perms_exceed_max() {
    local current=$1
    local max=$2
    
    # Convert octal strings to decimal for comparison
    local current_dec=$((8#$current))
    local max_dec=$((8#$max))
    
    if [[ $current_dec -gt $max_dec ]]; then
        return 1  # More permissive than max
    else
        return 0  # Less or equally permissive
    fi
}

# Get list of all interactive users (UID >= 1000) plus root (UID 0)
get_interactive_users() {
    # Get users from /etc/passwd with UID >= 1000 or UID 0 (root)
    awk -F: '($3 >= 1000 || $3 == 0) && $7 !~ /nologin|false|halt|shutdown/ {print $1, $6}' /etc/passwd
}

# Main remediation logic
echo "Starting shell initialization files permission hardening..."

changed_count=0
total_processed=0

# Process each interactive user
while IFS=' ' read -r username homedir; do
    if [[ -z "$homedir" || ! -d "$homedir" ]]; then
        continue
    fi
    
    # Check each initialization file
    for filename in "${TARGET_FILES[@]}"; do
        filepath="$homedir/$filename"
        
        # Skip if file doesn't exist
        if [[ ! -f "$filepath" ]]; then
            continue
        fi
        
        total_processed=$((total_processed + 1))
        current_perms=$(get_perms "$filepath")
        
        # Check if permissions exceed maximum allowed
        if ! perms_exceed_max "$current_perms" "$MAX_PERMS"; then
            # Permissions are more permissive than allowed, need to fix
            chmod 0740 "$filepath"
            echo "Fixed: $filepath (was $current_perms, now 0740)"
            changed_count=$((changed_count + 1))
        else
            # Permissions are acceptable
            :
        fi
    done
done < <(get_interactive_users)

# Summary
echo ""
echo "Remediation complete."
echo "Total files processed: $total_processed"
echo "Files with permissions adjusted: $changed_count"

exit 0

# Idempotent script to enforce 4-second delay after failed login attempts on RHEL 8

# Function to configure PAM for failed login delay
configure_pam_faildelay() {
    local pam_file="/etc/pam.d/system-auth"
    local faildelay_line="auth required pam_faildelay.so delay=4000000"
    
    # Check if the faildelay module is already configured
    if ! grep -q "pam_faildelay.so" "$pam_file"; then
        # Add the faildelay module after the first auth line
        sed -i '1a auth required pam_faildelay.so delay=4000000' "$pam_file"
    else
        # Update existing faildelay configuration to ensure 4-second delay (4000000 microseconds)
        sed -i 's/auth required pam_faildelay\.so delay=[0-9]*/auth required pam_faildelay.so delay=4000000/' "$pam_file"
    fi
}

configure_login_defs() {
    local login_defs="/etc/login.defs"
    
    # Check if FAIL_DELAY is already set
    if grep -q "^FAIL_DELAY" "$login_defs"; then
        # Update existing FAIL_DELAY to 4 seconds
        sed -i 's/^FAIL_DELAY.*/FAIL_DELAY 4/' "$login_defs"
    else
        # Add FAIL_DELAY if not present
        echo "FAIL_DELAY 4" >> "$login_defs"
    fi
}

# Function to configure common-auth for PAM (alternative location)
configure_common_auth() {
    local common_auth="/etc/pam.d/common-auth"

            sed -i '1a auth required pam_faildelay.so delay=4000000' "$common_auth"

if rpm -q postfix &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Postfix is already installed"
    exit 0
else
    echo "Installing Postfix mail server..."
    dnf install -y postfix
    
    # Check if installation was successful
    if rpm -q postfix &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Postfix has been successfully installed"
        exit 0
    else
        echo "Failed to install Postfix"
        exit 1
    fi
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Remediation script to ensure home directory permissions are secure
# This script modifies permissions for non-hidden files/directories to be no more permissive than 0750

# Get list of local interactive users (UID >= 1000 by default on RHEL 8)
for user in $(awk -F: '$3 >= 1000 && $1 !~ /^(nobody|nfsnobody|systemd-|dbus|polkitd)/ {print $1}' /etc/passwd); do
    # Get user's home directory
    home_dir=$(eval echo ~"$user")
    
    # Check if home directory exists
    if [ ! -d "$home_dir" ]; then
        continue
    fi
    
    # Process all non-hidden files and directories in home directory
    # Using find with -maxdepth 1 to only process direct children
    find "$home_dir" -maxdepth 1 -not -name ".*" -type f -o -maxdepth 1 -not -name ".*" -type d | while read -r file; do
        # Skip the home directory itself
        if [ "$file" = "$home_dir" ]; then
            continue
        fi
        
        # Get current permissions in octal
        current_perms=$(stat -c '%a' "$file" 2>/dev/null)
        
        if [ -z "$current_perms" ]; then
            continue
        fi
        
        # Check if permissions are more permissive than 0750
        # Convert to decimal for comparison
        current_dec=$((8#$current_perms))
        max_dec=$((8#0750))
        
        if [ $current_dec -gt $max_dec ]; then
            # Fix permissions
            if [ -d "$file" ]; then
                # For directories, set to 0750
                chmod 0750 "$file" 2>/dev/null
            else
                # For files, set to 0640 (read/write for owner, read for group)
                chmod 0640 "$file" 2>/dev/null
            fi
        fi
    done
    
    # Also handle the home directory itself - should be at least 0750
    home_perms=$(stat -c '%a' "$home_dir" 2>/dev/null)
    if [ ! -z "$home_perms" ]; then
        home_dec=$((8#$home_perms))
        if [ $home_dec -gt $((8#0750)) ]; then
            chmod 0750 "$home_dir" 2>/dev/null
        fi
    fi
done

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash script to enforce numeric digit requirement in passwords on RHEL 8

set -e

# Check if running on RHEL 8
if ! grep -q "release 8" /etc/redhat-release 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "This script is designed for RHEL 8 systems only"
    exit 1
fi

# Function to check and install pwquality package if not present
install_pwquality() {
    if ! rpm -q libpwquality &>/dev/null; then
        echo "Installing libpwquality package..."
        yum install -y libpwquality
    fi
}

# Function to update pwquality configuration
configure_pwquality() {
    local pwquality_file="/etc/security/pwquality.conf"
    
    # Backup original file if it hasn't been backed up yet
    if [ ! -f "${pwquality_file}.backup" ]; then
        cp "$pwquality_file" "${pwquality_file}.backup"
    fi
    
    # Check if dcredit (digits required) is already set
    if grep -q "^dcredit" "$pwquality_file"; then
        # Update existing dcredit setting to require at least 1 digit
        sed -i 's/^dcredit.*/dcredit = -1/' "$pwquality_file"
    else
        # Add dcredit setting if it doesn't exist
        echo "dcredit = -1" >> "$pwquality_file"
    fi
    
    # Ensure the line is properly formatted (remove any duplicate entries)
    # Keep only the last occurrence if multiple exist
    local temp_file=$(mktemp)
    grep -v "^dcredit" "$pwquality_file" > "$temp_file"
    mv "$temp_file" "$pwquality_file"
    echo "dcredit = -1" >> "$pwquality_file"
    
    # Verify the configuration
    if grep -q "^dcredit = -1" "$pwquality_file"; then
        echo "pwquality configuration updated successfully"
        return 0
    else
        echo "Failed to configure pwquality"
        return 1
    fi
}

# Function to configure PAM to use pwquality
configure_pam() {
    local pam_files=(
        "/etc/pam.d/system-auth"
        "/etc/pam.d/password-auth"
    )
    
    for pam_file in "${pam_files[@]}"; do
        if [ -f "$pam_file" ]; then
            # Check if pam_pwquality module is already configured
            if ! grep -q "pam_pwquality.so" "$pam_file"; then
                # Backup the file
                cp "$pam_file" "${pam_file}.backup"
                
                # Add pam_pwquality check after other password modules
                # Find the line with password required pam_unix.so and add our module before it
                sed -i '/^password.*required.*pam_unix.so/i password    required      pam_pwquality.so try_first_pass local_users_only' "$pam_file"
            fi
        fi
    done
}

# Function to configure system-auth-ac using authselect if available
configure_authselect() {
    # Check if authselect is available (RHEL 8+)
    if command -v authselect &>/dev/null; then
        # Get current profile
        local current_profile
        current_profile=$(authselect current | head -1 || echo "")
        
        if [ -n "$current_profile" ] && [ "$current_profile" != "custom/user" ]; then
            # Check if we need to enable pwquality in authselect
            if ! authselect list-features "$current_profile" 2>/dev/null | grep -q "with-pwquality"; then
                echo "pwquality feature not available in authselect, using manual PAM configuration"
                return 1
            fi
        fi
    fi
    return 0
}

# Main execution
main() {
    echo "Starting password quality policy configuration for RHEL 8..."
    
    # Install required packages
    install_pwquality
    
    # Configure pwquality settings
    configure_pwquality
    
    # Configure PAM if authselect is not handling it
    if ! configure_authselect; then
        configure_pam
    fi
    
    echo "Password quality policy has been successfully configured"
    echo "Minimum 1 numeric digit requirement is now enforced"
    
    # Verify configuration
    echo ""
    echo "Verification:"
    echo "============"
    echo "pwquality configuration:"
    grep "^dcredit" /etc/security/pwquality.conf || echo "dcredit setting not found"
    
    # Test with a simple password policy check (if possible)
    if command -v pwmake &>/dev/null; then
        echo ""
        echo "System password quality tool available: pwmake"
    fi
}

# Execute main function
main

#!/bin/bash

set -euo pipefail

# GNOME dconf settings directory
DCONF_DIR="/etc/dconf"
PROFILE_DIR="${DCONF_DIR}/profile"
DB_DIR="${DCONF_DIR}/db"
LOCKS_DIR="${DCONF_DIR}/db/locks"

# Create necessary directories
mkdir -p "${PROFILE_DIR}"
mkdir -p "${LOCKS_DIR}"

# Create or update the dconf profile
PROFILE_FILE="${PROFILE_DIR}/gdm"
if [ ! -f "${PROFILE_FILE}" ]; then
    cat > "${PROFILE_FILE}" << 'EOF'
user-db:user
system-db:local
EOF
fi

# Ensure the profile contains our local database reference
if ! grep -q "system-db:local" "${PROFILE_FILE}"; then
    echo "system-db:local" >> "${PROFILE_FILE}"
fi

# Create the dconf settings database file
LOCAL_DB="${DB_DIR}/local.d/00-lock-screensaver-settings"
mkdir -p "$(dirname "${LOCAL_DB}")"

# Configure screensaver lock delay (300 seconds = 5 minutes)
# and ensure it cannot be overridden
cat > "${LOCAL_DB}" << 'EOF'
[org/gnome/desktop/session]
idle-delay=uint32 300

[org/gnome/desktop/screensaver]
lock-enabled=true
lock-delay=uint32 0
EOF

# Create lock file to prevent user overrides
LOCKS_FILE="${LOCKS_DIR}/org.gnome.desktop.screensaver"
mkdir -p "${LOCKS_DIR}"

cat > "${LOCKS_FILE}" << 'EOF'
/org/gnome/desktop/screensaver/lock-enabled
/org/gnome/desktop/screensaver/lock-delay
/org/gnome/desktop/session/idle-delay
EOF

# Ensure proper permissions
chmod 644 "${LOCAL_DB}"
chmod 644 "${LOCKS_FILE}"
chmod 644 "${PROFILE_FILE}"

# Update dconf database
dconf update || true

# Kill any existing dconf instances to reload configuration
pkill -9 dconf-service || true

# Wait a moment for services to restart
sleep 2

# Verify settings are applied in the active session
if command -v dconf &> /dev/null; then
    # Test that the settings are readable (may require active dbus session)
    dconf read /org/gnome/desktop/screensaver/lock-enabled || true
fi

echo "GNOME screensaver lock-delay settings have been locked down."
exit 0

#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Ensure audit daemon is available
if ! command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Error: auditctl not found. Please install audit package." >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Define the audit rules file
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/privileged-command-toggle-selinux.rules"
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"

# Ensure the rules directory exists
mkdir -p "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR"

# Create the audit rule
# getsebool is the command used to get and set SELinux boolean values
# We need to audit all executions by users with UID >= 1000
# The rule should be persistent and survive reboots

AUDIT_RULE="-a exit,always -F path=/usr/sbin/getsebool -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k privileged-selinux-toggle"

# Check if the rule already exists in the rules file
if [ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
    if grep -q "privileged-selinux-toggle" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
        echo "Audit rule for privileged SELinux boolean toggle already exists in $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    else
        # Add the rule to the file
        echo "$AUDIT_RULE" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
        echo "Added audit rule to $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    fi
else
    # Create the file with the rule
    echo "$AUDIT_RULE" > "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    echo "Created $AUDIT_RULES_FILE with audit rule"
fi

# Load the rule into the kernel audit subsystem
if ! auditctl -l | grep -q "privileged-selinux-toggle"; then
    auditctl -a exit,always -F path=/usr/sbin/getsebool -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k privileged-selinux-toggle
    echo "Loaded audit rule into kernel"
else
    echo "Audit rule already loaded in kernel"
fi

# Ensure the audit daemon is enabled and will start on boot
systemctl enable auditd --now

echo "Audit configuration for privileged SELinux boolean toggle is complete and persistent."

#!/bin/bash

# Remediation script to configure minimum password length of 15 characters on RHEL 8
# This script is idempotent and persists across reboots

set -e

# Function to update PAM configuration
update_pam_pwquality() {
    local pam_file="$1"
    local minlen_value="15"
    
    # Check if file exists
    if [ ! -f "$pam_file" ]; then
        return
    fi
    
    # Backup original file
    cp "$pam_file" "$pam_file.bak-$(date +%s)"
    
    # Check if pam_pwquality module is already configured
    if grep -q "pam_pwquality" "$pam_file"; then
        # Update existing pam_pwquality line with correct minlen
        sed -i "s/pam_pwquality.so.*$/pam_pwquality.so retry=3 minlen=$minlen_value dcredit=-1 ucredit=-1 ocredit=-1 lcredit=-1/" "$pam_file"
    fi
}

# Function to configure /etc/security/pwquality.conf
configure_pwquality_conf() {
    local pwquality_conf="/etc/security/pwquality.conf"
    
    # Create or backup existing file
    if [ -f "$pwquality_conf" ]; then
        cp "$pwquality_conf" "$pwquality_conf.bak-$(date +%s)"
    fi
    
    # Remove existing minlen line if present
    sed -i '/^minlen[[:space:]]*=/d' "$pwquality_conf"

#!/bin/bash

# Remediation script to configure minimum password length of 15 characters on RHEL 8
# This script is idempotent and persists across reboots

set -e

# Function to update PAM configuration
update_pam_pwquality() {
    local pam_file="$1"
    local minlen_value="15"
    
    # Check if file exists
    if [ ! -f "$pam_file" ]; then
        return
    fi
    
    # Backup original file
    cp "$pam_file" "$pam_file.bak-$(date +%s)"
    
    # Check if pam_pwquality module is already configured
    if grep -q "pam_pwquality" "$pam_file"; then
        # Update existing pam_pwquality line with correct minlen
        sed -i "s/pam_pwquality.so.*$/pam_pwquality.so retry=3 minlen=$minlen_value dcredit=-1 ucredit=-1 ocredit=-1 lcredit=-1/" "$pam_file"
    fi
}

configure_pwquality_conf() {
    local pwquality_conf="/etc/security/pwquality.conf"

        cp "$pwquality_conf" "$pwquality_conf.bak-$(date +%s)"
    fi
    
    # Remove existing minlen line if present
    sed -i '/^minlen[[:space:]]*=/d' "$pwquality_conf"

# Set error handling
set -e

# Function to check if a line exists in a file
line_exists() {
    local line="$1"
    local file="$2"
    grep -qxF "$line" "$file" 2>/dev/null
}

# Function to add or update a line in a file
add_or_update_line() {
    local line="$1"
    local file="$2"
    local pattern="$3"
    
    if [ ! -f "$file" ]; then
        echo "$line" | tee "$file" > /dev/null
    elif [ -z "$pattern" ]; then
        # If no pattern is provided, check exact line
        if ! line_exists "$line" "$file"; then
            echo "$line" >> "$file"
        fi
    else
        # If pattern is provided, check if pattern exists
        if grep -q "$pattern" "$file"; then
            # Pattern exists, update the line
            sed -i "/$pattern/c\\$line" "$file"
        else
            # Pattern doesn't exist, append the line
            echo "$line" >> "$file"
        fi
    fi
}

# Check if system is RHEL 8
if ! grep -q "Red Hat Enterprise Linux release 8" /etc/redhat-release 2>/dev/null; then
    if ! grep -q "CentOS Linux 8" /etc/redhat-release 2>/dev/null; then
        if ! grep -q "AlmaLinux 8" /etc/redhat-release 2>/dev/null; then
            if ! grep -q "Rocky Linux 8" /etc/redhat-release 2>/dev/null; then
                echo "Warning: This system may not be RHEL 8 based"
            fi
        fi
    fi
fi

# Install libpwquality if not already installed
if ! rpm -q libpwquality > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    yum install -y libpwquality
fi

# Configure /etc/security/pwquality.conf for dictionary checking
PWQUALITY_CONF="/etc/security/pwquality.conf"

# Ensure the file exists
if [ ! -f "$PWQUALITY_CONF" ]; then
    touch "$PWQUALITY_CONF"
fi

# Set dictionary checking parameters
# dictcheck: perform a dictionary check of the password against the system dictionary
add_or_update_line "dictcheck = 1" "$PWQUALITY_CONF" "^dictcheck"

# usercheck: check whether the password contains the user name
add_or_update_line "usercheck = 1" "$PWQUALITY_CONF" "^usercheck"

# enforce_for_root: enforce password quality for root
add_or_update_line "enforce_for_root" "$PWQUALITY_CONF" "^enforce_for_root"

# Set minimum password length
add_or_update_line "minlen = 12" "$PWQUALITY_CONF" "^minlen"

# Set minimum number of lowercase characters
add_or_update_line "lcredit = -1" "$PWQUALITY_CONF" "^lcredit"

# Set minimum number of uppercase characters
add_or_update_line "ucredit = -1" "$PWQUALITY_CONF" "^ucredit"

# Set minimum number of digit characters
add_or_update_line "dcredit = -1" "$PWQUALITY_CONF" "^dcredit"

# Set minimum number of other (special) characters
add_or_update_line "ocredit = -1" "$PWQUALITY_CONF" "^ocredit"

# Ensure proper permissions on pwquality.conf
chmod 644 "$PWQUALITY_CONF"

# Configure PAM to use pam_pwquality.so
# Update system-auth for password quality checks
SYSTEM_AUTH="/etc/pam.d/system-auth"
SYSTEM_AUTH_LOCAL="/etc/pam.d/system-auth-ac"

# Check if password-auth exists and update it
if [ -f "/etc/pam.d/password-auth" ]; then
    # Check if pam_pwquality is already configured
    if ! grep -q "pam_pwquality" "/etc/pam.d/password-auth"; then
        # Add pam_pwquality.so to password-auth
        sed -i '/^password.*requisite.*pam_cracklib.so/a password    requisite     pam_pwquality.so try_first_pass local_users_only' "/etc/pam.d/password-auth"
    fi
    
    # Update existing pam_pwquality configuration if needed
    if grep -q "pam_pwquality" "/etc/pam.d/password-auth"; then
        sed -i 's/password.*pam_pwquality.so.*/password    requisite     pam_pwquality.so try_first_pass local_users_only/' "/etc/pam.d/password-auth"
    fi
fi

# Ensure PAM configuration files have proper permissions
chmod 644 /etc/pam.d/password-auth* 2>/dev/null || true

# Configure common-password file if it exists (for compatibility)
COMMON_PASSWORD="/etc/pam.d/common-password"
if [ -f "$COMMON_PASSWORD" ]; then
    if ! grep -q "pam_pwquality" "$COMMON_PASSWORD"; then
        sed -i '/^password.*pam_unix.so.*/i password    requisite     pam_pwquality.so try_first_pass local_users_only' "$COMMON_PASSWORD"
    fi
fi

# Verify the configuration
if grep -q "dictcheck = 1" "$PWQUALITY_CONF"; then
    echo "Password quality enforcement with dictionary checking has been successfully configured"
else
    echo "Error: Failed to configure password quality enforcement"
    exit 1
fi

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Disable Bluetooth kernel module on RHEL 8 - Idempotent Script

set -e

# Function to check if module is already blacklisted
is_module_blacklisted() {
    local module="$1"
    if [ -f /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist-bluetooth.conf ]; then
        grep -q "^blacklist ${module}" /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist-bluetooth.conf
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to check if module is already disabled in modprobe
is_module_disabled() {
    local module="$1"
    if [ -f /etc/modprobe.d/disable-bluetooth.conf ]; then
        grep -q "^install ${module} /bin/true" /etc/modprobe.d/disable-bluetooth.conf
        return $?
    fi
    return 1
}

# Bluetooth-related modules to disable
BLUETOOTH_MODULES=("bluetooth" "btusb" "btrtl" "btintel" "btbcm" "bnep" "rfcomm" "hidp")

# Create blacklist configuration file
echo "Creating blacklist configuration for Bluetooth modules..."
if [ ! -f /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist-bluetooth.conf ]; then
    {
        echo "# Blacklist Bluetooth modules"
        for module in "${BLUETOOTH_MODULES[@]}"; do
            echo "blacklist ${module}"
        done
    } > /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist-bluetooth.conf
    echo "Created /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist-bluetooth.conf"
else
    # Check and add any missing modules to existing file
    for module in "${BLUETOOTH_MODULES[@]}"; do
        if ! is_module_blacklisted "$module"; then
            echo "blacklist ${module}" >> /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist-bluetooth.conf
            echo "Added blacklist entry for ${module}"
        fi
    done
fi

# Create install blocking configuration
echo "Creating install blocking configuration for Bluetooth modules..."
if [ ! -f /etc/modprobe.d/disable-bluetooth.conf ]; then
    {
        echo "# Disable Bluetooth modules installation"
        for module in "${BLUETOOTH_MODULES[@]}"; do
            echo "install ${module} /bin/true"
        done
    } > /etc/modprobe.d/disable-bluetooth.conf
    echo "Created /etc/modprobe.d/disable-bluetooth.conf"
else
    # Check and add any missing modules to existing file
    for module in "${BLUETOOTH_MODULES[@]}"; do
        if ! is_module_disabled "$module"; then
            echo "install ${module} /bin/true" >> /etc/modprobe.d/disable-bluetooth.conf
            echo "Added install blocking for ${module}"
        fi
    done
fi

# Unload any currently loaded Bluetooth modules
echo "Checking for and unloading currently loaded Bluetooth modules..."
for module in "${BLUETOOTH_MODULES[@]}"; do
    if lsmod | grep -q "^${module}"; then
        echo "Attempting to unload ${module}..."
        if modprobe -r "$module" 2>/dev/null; then
            echo "Successfully unloaded ${module}"
        else
            echo "Could not unload ${module} (may be in use)"
        fi
    fi
done

# Disable bluetooth service if it exists
if systemctl list-unit-files | grep -q bluetooth.service; then
    echo "Disabling bluetooth.service..."
    systemctl disable bluetooth.service 2>/dev/null || true
    if systemctl is-active --quiet bluetooth.service; then
        systemctl stop bluetooth.service 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
fi

# Verify changes
echo ""
echo "Verification of Bluetooth module blocking configuration:"
echo "========================================================="
echo ""
echo "Contents of /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist-bluetooth.conf:"
cat /etc/modprobe.d/blacklist-bluetooth.conf
echo ""
echo "Contents of /etc/modprobe.d/disable-bluetooth.conf:"
cat /etc/modprobe.d/disable-bluetooth.conf
echo ""
echo "Currently loaded Bluetooth modules:"
lsmod | grep -E "^(bluetooth|btusb|btrtl|btintel|btbcm|bnep|rfcomm|hidp)" || echo "No Bluetooth modules currently loaded"
echo ""
echo "Remediation complete. Bluetooth modules have been disabled and will not load on reboot."
