#!/bin/bash
set -e

# Configuration
REQUIRED_BACKLOG=8192
GRUB_CFG="/etc/default/grub"
KERNEL_PARAM="audit_backlog_limit=${REQUIRED_BACKLOG}"

# Function to check if parameter exists in GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
check_parameter() {
    grep -q "audit_backlog_limit=" "$GRUB_CFG" 2>/dev/null || return 1
}

# Function to get current value
get_current_value() {
    grep "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" "$GRUB_CFG" | grep -oP "audit_backlog_limit=\K[0-9]+" || echo "0"
}

# Function to set parameter in GRUB config
set_parameter() {
    local current_value
    current_value=$(get_current_value)
    
    if [ "$current_value" -eq "$REQUIRED_BACKLOG" ]; then
        echo "audit_backlog_limit is already set to $REQUIRED_BACKLOG"
        return 0
    fi
    
    if check_parameter; then
        # Replace existing parameter
        sed -i "s/audit_backlog_limit=[0-9]\+/audit_backlog_limit=${REQUIRED_BACKLOG}/g" "$GRUB_CFG"
    else
        # Add new parameter to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
        sed -i "s/\(^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"[^\"]*\)\"/\1 ${KERNEL_PARAM}\"/" "$GRUB_CFG"
    fi
}

# Function to regenerate grub config
regenerate_grub() {
    if [ -f /boot/grub2/grub.cfg ]; then
        grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg
    fi

#!/bin/bash
set -e

# Configuration
REQUIRED_BACKLOG=8192
GRUB_CFG="/etc/default/grub"
KERNEL_PARAM="audit_backlog_limit=${REQUIRED_BACKLOG}"

# Function to check if parameter exists in GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
check_parameter() {
    grep -q "audit_backlog_limit=" "$GRUB_CFG" 2>/dev/null || return 1
}

# Function to get current value
get_current_value() {
    grep "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" "$GRUB_CFG" | grep -oP "audit_backlog_limit=\K[0-9]+" || echo "0"
}

# Function to set parameter in GRUB config
set_parameter() {
    local current_value
    current_value=$(get_current_value)
    
    if [ "$current_value" -eq "$REQUIRED_BACKLOG" ]; then
        echo "audit_backlog_limit is already set to $REQUIRED_BACKLOG"
        return 0
    fi
    
    if check_parameter; then
        # Replace existing parameter
        sed -i "s/audit_backlog_limit=[0-9]\+/audit_backlog_limit=${REQUIRED_BACKLOG}/g" "$GRUB_CFG"
    else
        # Add new parameter to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
        sed -i "s/\(^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"[^\"]*\)\"/\1 ${KERNEL_PARAM}\"/" "$GRUB_CFG"
    fi
}

# Function to regenerate grub config
regenerate_grub() {
    if [ -f /boot/grub2/grub.cfg ]; then
        grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg
    fi
    
    if [ -d /sys/firmware/efi ]; then
        if [ -f /boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg ]; then
            grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg
        fi
    fi
}

# Main script
main() {
    # Check if GRUB_CFG exists
    if [ ! -f "$GRUB_CFG" ]; then
        echo "Error: $GRUB_CFG not found"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    echo "Checking audit_backlog_limit configuration..."
    
    # Set the parameter
    set_parameter
    
    # Regenerate GRUB configuration for all installed kernels
    echo "Regenerating GRUB configuration for all kernels..."
    regenerate_grub
    
    # Verify the setting
    echo "Verifying configuration..."
    if grep -q "audit_backlog_limit=${REQUIRED_BACKLOG}" "$GRUB_CFG"; then
        echo "Successfully configured audit_backlog_limit to ${REQUIRED_BACKLOG}"
        echo "The setting will apply to all currently installed kernels and future kernels"
        echo "The configuration will persist across reboots"
    else
        echo "Error: Failed to configure audit_backlog_limit"
        exit 1
    fi
}

#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Remediation script to configure audit daemon to log extended attribute removal from symbolic links
# This script ensures the audit rules persist across reboots and are idempotent

# Determine the audit rules directory
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE=""

# Check if audit is installed and running
if ! command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Error: auditctl not found. Installing audit package..."
    dnf install -y audit audit-libs
fi

# Determine which rule file to use (audit.rules or custom rules.d files)
if [ -f "/etc/audit/audit.rules" ]; then
    AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/audit.rules"
elif [ -d "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR" ]; then
    AUDIT_RULES_FILE="$AUDIT_RULES_DIR/audit-rules.rules"
else
    echo "Error: No audit rules configuration found"
    exit 1
fi

# Ensure audit rules directory exists
mkdir -p "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR"

# Define the audit rules for extended attribute removal from symbolic links
# lremovexattr syscall (removes extended attributes from symbolic links)
# We need to monitor both 32-bit and 64-bit syscall interfaces
# Syscall number for lremovexattr: 
# - 64-bit x86_64: 373
# - 32-bit i386: 323

# Create backup of existing rules file
if [ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
    cp "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}.backup.$(date +%s)"
fi

# Function to check if a rule already exists in the audit rules file
rule_exists() {
    local rule="$1"
    if [ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
        grep -F "$rule" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null || return 1
    fi
    return 1
}

# The audit rules to add
# These rules capture lremovexattr syscalls for both unprivileged users and root
# Tagged with "perm_mod" to denote permission modifications
AUDIT_RULE_64BIT="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S lremovexattr -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k perm_mod"
AUDIT_RULE_32BIT="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S lremovexattr -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k perm_mod"
AUDIT_RULE_ROOT="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S lremovexattr -F uid=0 -k perm_mod"

# Check if we're dealing with rules.d directory structure
if [ -d "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR" ] && [ "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" = "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR/audit-rules.rules" ]; then
    # Using rules.d directory structure
    # Add rules if they don't exist
    if ! rule_exists "lremovexattr" || ! rule_exists "perm_mod"; then
        {
            echo ""
            echo "# Extended attribute removal from symbolic links monitoring"
            echo "# Captures lremovexattr syscalls for unprivileged users (auid >= 1000)"
            echo "$AUDIT_RULE_64BIT"
            echo "$AUDIT_RULE_32BIT"
            echo ""
            echo "# Capture lremovexattr for root user"
            echo "$AUDIT_RULE_ROOT"
        } >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    fi
else
    # Using single audit.rules file
    # Add rules if they don't exist
    if ! grep -q "lremovexattr" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null || ! grep -q "perm_mod" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
        {
            echo ""
            echo "# Extended attribute removal from symbolic links monitoring"
            echo "# Captures lremovexattr syscalls for unprivileged users (auid >= 1000)"
            echo "$AUDIT_RULE_64BIT"
            echo "$AUDIT_RULE_32BIT"
            echo ""
            echo "# Capture lremovexattr for root user"
            echo "$AUDIT_RULE_ROOT"
        } >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    fi
fi

# Ensure audit service is enabled and started
systemctl enable auditd 2>/dev/null || true
systemctl start auditd 2>/dev/null || true

# Load the audit rules
auditctl -R "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null || true

# Verify the rules are loaded
echo "Verifying audit rules..."
if auditctl -l | grep -q "lremovexattr"; then
    echo "SUCCESS: Audit rules for lremovexattr have been successfully configured"
    echo "Loaded rules:"
    auditctl -l | grep "lremovexattr" || true
else
    echo "WARNING: Rules may not have been loaded. Attempting alternative loading method..."
    # Try to load with augenrules if available
    if command -v augenrules &> /dev/null; then
        augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || true
        systemctl restart auditd 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
fi

# Verify final state
if auditctl -l | grep -q "lremovexattr"; then
    echo "FINAL CHECK: Rules are active and will persist across reboots"
    exit 0
else
    echo "ERROR: Failed to load audit rules. Check audit daemon configuration."
    exit 1
fi

#!/bin/bash

# RHEL 8 - Enforce certificate-name authentication for remote logging
# This script configures rsyslog to authenticate remote peers using x.509 certificates

set -e

# Ensure rsyslog is installed
if ! command -v rsyslog &> /dev/null; then
    yum install -y rsyslog
fi

# Enable and start rsyslog service
systemctl enable rsyslog
systemctl start rsyslog

# Create rsyslog configuration directory if it doesn't exist
mkdir -p /etc/rsyslog.d

# Create a configuration file for secure remote logging with certificate verification
cat > /etc/rsyslog.d/remote-logging-secure.conf << 'EOF'
# Enable TLS support and certificate verification for remote logging
# This configuration enforces x.509 certificate-based authentication

# Load the GTLS module for TLS support
module(load="imtcp" StreamDriver.Name="gtls" StreamDriver.Mode="1" StreamDriver.AuthMode="x509/name")

# Configure remote log forwarding with TLS and certificate verification
# The following settings ensure certificate name verification is enabled
$DefaultNetstreamDriver gtls
$DefaultNetstreamDriverCAFile /etc/pki/tls/certs/ca-bundle.crt
$ActionSendStreamDriver gtls
$ActionSendStreamDriverMode 1
$ActionSendStreamDriverAuthMode x509/name
$ActionSendStreamDriverPermittedPeer *.example.com

# If you have a specific remote log server, configure it like this:
# *.* @@(o)remote-server.example.com:6514
# Make sure to replace remote-server.example.com with your actual log server FQDN

# Enable high precision timestamps
$ActionFileDefaultTemplate RSYSLOG_FileFormat
EOF

# Verify rsyslog syntax
if ! rsyslogd -N1 &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Error: rsyslog configuration syntax error"
    exit 1
fi

# Create or update the main rsyslog configuration to include our settings
# Ensure that the secure remote logging configuration is loaded
if ! grep -q "remote-logging-secure.conf" /etc/rsyslog.conf; then
    # Add include directive at the end of rsyslog.conf if not already present
    echo '$IncludeConfig /etc/rsyslog.d/*.conf' >> /etc/rsyslog.conf
fi

# Ensure proper file permissions
chmod 644 /etc/rsyslog.d/remote-logging-secure.conf
chmod 644 /etc/rsyslog.conf

# Restart rsyslog to apply changes
systemctl restart rsyslog

# Verify rsyslog is running
if systemctl is-active --quiet rsyslog; then
    echo "Remediation successful: rsyslog is configured with certificate-based peer authentication"
else
    echo "Error: rsyslog failed to start"
    exit 1
fi

# Verify the configuration is persistent by checking the configuration files
if grep -q "DefaultNetstreamDriver gtls" /etc/rsyslog.d/remote-logging-secure.conf && \
   grep -q "ActionSendStreamDriverAuthMode x509/name" /etc/rsyslog.d/remote-logging-secure.conf; then
    echo "Configuration verified: Certificate-based authentication is properly configured"
else
    echo "Error: Configuration verification failed"
    exit 1
fi

# Ensure SELinux context is correct if SELinux is enabled
if command -v getenforce &> /dev/null && [ "$(getenforce)" != "Disabled" ]; then
    chcon -t syslog_etc_t /etc/rsyslog.d/remote-logging-secure.conf 2>/dev/null || true
fi

echo "Idempotent remediation completed successfully"

#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# Script to configure IPv4 ICMP echo request handling for broadcast/multicast addresses on RHEL 8
# This prevents the system from responding to ICMP echo requests sent to broadcast or multicast addresses

SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE="/etc/sysctl.d/99-icmp-broadcast-ignore.conf"

# Function to apply sysctl settings
apply_sysctl_settings() {
    # Ignore ICMP echo requests sent to broadcast addresses
    sysctl -w net.ipv4.icmp_echo_ignore_broadcasts=1
    
    # Ignore ICMP echo requests sent to multicast addresses (using icmp_echo_ignore_all as a fallback for multicast)
    # Note: icmp_echo_ignore_all=1 would ignore all ICMP echo requests
    # Instead, we rely on icmp_echo_ignore_broadcasts which also covers multicast in many kernel versions
}

# Function to create persistent configuration
create_persistent_config() {
    # Create the sysctl configuration file if it doesn't exist or update it
    if [ ! -f "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE" ]; then
        cat > "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE" << 'EOF'
# Configuration to ignore ICMP echo requests to broadcast/multicast addresses
# Prevents system from participating in network-mapping reconnaissance and ICMP amplification attacks
net.ipv4.icmp_echo_ignore_broadcasts = 1
EOF
        chmod 644 "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE"
    else
        # Check if the setting already exists in the file
        if grep -q "^net.ipv4.icmp_echo_ignore_broadcasts" "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE"; then
            # Update the existing setting to ensure it's set to 1
            sed -i 's/^net\.ipv4\.icmp_echo_ignore_broadcasts.*/net.ipv4.icmp_echo_ignore_broadcasts = 1/' "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE"
        else
            # Add the setting if it doesn't exist
            echo "net.ipv4.icmp_echo_ignore_broadcasts = 1" >> "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE"
        fi
    fi
}

# Function to reload sysctl settings from all configuration files
reload_sysctl_settings() {
    sysctl -p "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE" > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
}

# Main execution
main() {
    echo "Configuring IPv4 ICMP echo request handling for broadcast/multicast addresses..."
    
    # Create persistent configuration
    create_persistent_config
    
    # Apply settings immediately
    apply_sysctl_settings
    
    # Reload sysctl settings to ensure persistence
    reload_sysctl_settings
    
    # Verify the settings were applied correctly
    CURRENT_VALUE=$(sysctl -n net.ipv4.icmp_echo_ignore_broadcasts)
    
    if [ "$CURRENT_VALUE" = "1" ]; then
        echo "✓ Successfully configured IPv4 ICMP echo request handling"
        echo "✓ net.ipv4.icmp_echo_ignore_broadcasts is set to: $CURRENT_VALUE"
        echo "✓ Configuration has been made persistent in: $SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE"
    else
        echo "✗ Failed to configure IPv4 ICMP echo request handling"
        echo "✗ net.ipv4.icmp_echo_ignore_broadcasts is set to: $CURRENT_VALUE (expected: 1)"
        exit 1
    fi
}

main

        log_message "AIDE not installed, installing now..."
        yum install -y aide
        log_message "AIDE installed successfully"
    else
        log_message "AIDE is already installed"
    fi
    
    # Step 2: Check if AIDE database needs to be initialized
    if ! aide_database_exists; then
        log_message "AIDE database not found, initializing baseline..."
        
        # Initialize AIDE database

        aideinit

            mv /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz

#!/bin/bash

set -e

# Remediation script for AIDE baseline initialization on RHEL 8

# Function to log messages
log_message() {
    echo "[$(date +'%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')] $1"
}

# Function to check if AIDE is installed
aide_installed() {
    rpm -q aide &>/dev/null
    return $?
}

# Function to check if AIDE baseline database exists
aide_database_exists() {
    [[ -f /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz ]] || [[ -f /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz ]]
    return $?
}

# Main remediation logic
main() {
    log_message "Starting AIDE baseline initialization remediation"
    
    # Step 1: Ensure AIDE is installed
    if ! aide_installed; then
        log_message "AIDE not installed, installing now..."
        yum install -y aide
        log_message "AIDE installed successfully"
    else
        log_message "AIDE is already installed"
    fi
    
    # Step 2: Check if AIDE database needs to be initialized
    if ! aide_database_exists; then
        log_message "AIDE database not found, initializing baseline..."
        
        # Initialize AIDE database
        # This creates /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz
        aideinit
        
        if [[ -f /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz ]]; then
            log_message "AIDE baseline database created successfully"
            
            # Move the new database to the active location
            mv /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz
            log_message "AIDE baseline database installed in active location"
        else
            log_message "ERROR: Failed to create AIDE baseline database"
            exit 1
        fi
    else
        log_message "AIDE baseline database already exists"
        
        # Ensure the database is in the correct location
        if [[ -f /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz ]] && [[ ! -f /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz ]]; then
            log_message "Moving AIDE database to active location..."
            mv /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz
        fi
    fi
    
    # Step 3: Verify AIDE can perform integrity checks
    log_message "Verifying AIDE integrity check capability..."
    
    if aide --check &>/dev/null || [[ $? -eq 1 ]]; then
        log_message "AIDE integrity check verification successful"
    else
        log_message "ERROR: AIDE integrity check failed"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    log_message "AIDE remediation completed successfully"
}

main "$@"

# Set exit on error
set -e

# Check if running as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
   echo "This script must be run as root"
   exit 1

# Disable core dumps for all users by modifying /etc/security/limits.conf
if ! grep -q "^\*[[:space:]]*hard[[:space:]]*core" /etc/security/limits.conf; then
    echo "*                hard    core            0" >> /etc/security/limits.conf
fi

# Ensure the hard core limit is set to 0
sed -i 's/^\*[[:space:]]*hard[[:space:]]*core[[:space:]]*[0-9]*/*                hard    core            0/' /etc/security/limits.conf

# Disable systemd-coredump service
systemctl disable systemd-coredump.service 2>/dev/null || true
systemctl disable systemd-coredump.socket 2>/dev/null || true

# Stop the services
systemctl stop systemd-coredump.service 2>/dev/null || true
systemctl stop systemd-coredump.socket 2>/dev/null || true

# Configure kernel parameters via sysctl to disable core dumps
# Create/modify sysctl configuration file for core dumps
cat > /etc/sysctl.d/99-core-dumps-disabled.conf << 'EOF'
# Disable core dumps to prevent sensitive data retention
kernel.core_max=0
fs.suid_dumpable=0
EOF

# Apply the sysctl settings immediately
sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/99-core-dumps-disabled.conf > /dev/null

# Modify ABRT (Automatic Bug Reporting Tool) configuration if present
if [[ -f /etc/abrt/abrt.conf ]]; then
    # Comment out or set enabled to 'no'
    sed -i 's/^enabled[[:space:]]*=.*/enabled = no/' /etc/abrt/abrt.conf
    # Disable the abrtd service
    systemctl disable abrtd 2>/dev/null || true
    systemctl stop abrtd 2>/dev/null || true
fi

# Ensure dmesg cannot show core dumps
echo "kernel.printk=3 3 3 3" >> /etc/sysctl.d/99-core-dumps-disabled.conf
sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/99-core-dumps-disabled.conf > /dev/null

# Set umask to restrict core dump file creation permissions
if ! grep -q "^umask" /etc/profile; then
    echo "umask 0077" >> /etc/profile
fi

echo "Core dump handling has been configured to prevent persistent storage."
echo "Core dumps are now disabled system-wide and the configuration persists across reboots."

#!/bin/bash
set -e

# Remediation script for disabling IPv6 ICMP redirect messages on RHEL 8

echo "Disabling IPv6 ICMP redirect message acceptance..."

# Apply runtime changes
sysctl -w net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_redirects=0
sysctl -w net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_redirects=0

# Apply to all existing interfaces
for interface in $(ip -6 link show | grep "^[0-9]:" | awk '{print $2}' | sed 's/:$//'); do
    sysctl -w net.ipv6.conf."${interface}".accept_redirects=0 2>/dev/null || true
done

# Persist the changes across reboots
sysctl_config="/etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-ipv6-redirects.conf"

if [ ! -f "$sysctl_config" ]; then
    # Create new configuration file
    cat > "$sysctl_config" << 'EOF'
# Disable IPv6 ICMP redirect message acceptance
net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0
net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_redirects = 0
EOF
    chmod 644 "$sysctl_config"
    echo "Created $sysctl_config"
else
    # Update existing configuration file
    # Remove any existing accept_redirects settings for all and default
    sed -i '/^net\.ipv6\.conf\.all\.accept_redirects/d' "$sysctl_config"
    sed -i '/^net\.ipv6\.conf\.default\.accept_redirects/d' "$sysctl_config"
    
    # Append new settings
    cat >> "$sysctl_config" << 'EOF'
# Disable IPv6 ICMP redirect message acceptance
net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0
net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_redirects = 0
EOF
    echo "Updated $sysctl_config"
fi

# Apply the sysctl configuration file to ensure it's loaded
sysctl -p "$sysctl_config" > /dev/null

echo "IPv6 ICMP redirect message acceptance has been disabled."
echo "Verification of current settings:"
sysctl net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_redirects
sysctl net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_redirects

#!/bin/bash

set -e

# Configuration for password quality requirements
PWQUALITY_CONFIG="/etc/security/pwquality.conf"
MIN_LENGTH=15

# Create a backup of the original file if it doesn't exist
if [ ! -f "${PWQUALITY_CONFIG}.backup" ]; then
    if [ -f "$PWQUALITY_CONFIG" ]; then
        cp "$PWQUALITY_CONFIG" "${PWQUALITY_CONFIG}.backup"
    fi
fi

# Ensure the file exists
touch "$PWQUALITY_CONFIG"

# Check and update the minlen parameter
if grep -q "^minlen" "$PWQUALITY_CONFIG"; then
    # Update existing minlen value
    sed -i "s/^minlen[[:space:]]*=.*/minlen = $MIN_LENGTH/" "$PWQUALITY_CONFIG"
else
    # Add minlen if it doesn't exist
    echo "minlen = $MIN_LENGTH" >> "$PWQUALITY_CONFIG"
fi

# Ensure pwquality module is installed
if ! rpm -q libpwquality &>/dev/null; then
    yum install -y libpwquality
fi

# Ensure the pam_pwquality.so module is configured in PAM for password-auth
PAM_PASSWORD_AUTH="/etc/pam.d/password-auth"
PAM_SYSTEM_AUTH="/etc/pam.d/system-auth"

# Function to add pam_pwquality configuration
configure_pam_pwquality() {
    local pam_file=$1
    
    if [ ! -f "$pam_file" ]; then
        return
    fi
    
    # Check if pam_pwquality is already configured
    if grep -q "pam_pwquality.so" "$pam_file"; then
        # Update existing configuration to use pwquality
        sed -i 's/^password.*requisite.*pam_pwquality.so.*/password requisite pam_pwquality.so retry=3/' "$pam_file"
    else
        # Add pam_pwquality configuration at the beginning of password lines
        if grep -q "^password" "$pam_file"; then
            sed -i '/^password/i password requisite pam_pwquality.so retry=3' "$pam_file"
        else
            echo "password requisite pam_pwquality.so retry=3" >> "$pam_file"
        fi
    fi
}

# Configure both password-auth and system-auth
configure_pam_pwquality "$PAM_PASSWORD_AUTH"
configure_pam_pwquality "$PAM_SYSTEM_AUTH"

# Verify configuration
if grep -q "minlen = $MIN_LENGTH" "$PWQUALITY_CONFIG"; then
    echo "Password quality policy successfully enforced:"
    echo "- Minimum password length set to $MIN_LENGTH characters"
    grep "minlen" "$PWQUALITY_CONFIG"
else
    echo "Error: Failed to set minimum password length"
    exit 1
fi

# Verify pam configuration
echo "PAM configuration status:"
for pam_file in "$PAM_PASSWORD_AUTH" "$PAM_SYSTEM_AUTH"; do
    if [ -f "$pam_file" ] && grep -q "pam_pwquality.so" "$pam_file"; then
        echo "- $pam_file: pam_pwquality.so configured"
    fi
done

echo "Password policy configuration complete and will persist across reboots."

#!/bin/bash
set -e

# RHEL 8 AppArmor/SELinux Application Allowlisting Configuration
# This script configures deny-all, permit-by-exception security model

echo "Starting RHEL 8 Application Allowlisting Configuration..."

# Check if running as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
   echo "This script must be run as root"
   exit 1
fi

# Determine which MAC system to use
# RHEL 8 typically uses SELinux by default
MAC_SYSTEM="selinux"

# Check if AppArmor is available and prefer it if SELinux is not enforcing
if command -v aa-enforce &> /dev/null; then
    # Check if AppArmor is the active MAC system
    if grep -q "apparmor" /proc/cmdline 2>/dev/null || systemctl is-active apparmor &> /dev/null 2>&1; then
        MAC_SYSTEM="apparmor"
    fi
fi

echo "Detected MAC system: $MAC_SYSTEM"

if [ "$MAC_SYSTEM" = "selinux" ]; then
    echo "Configuring SELinux for application allowlisting..."
    
    # Install SELinux policy tools if not present
    if ! rpm -q selinux-policy-devel &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Installing SELinux policy development tools..."
        dnf install -y selinux-policy-devel selinux-policy-base policycoreutils-python-utils semanage
    fi
    
    # Set SELinux to enforcing mode
    echo "Setting SELinux to enforcing mode..."
    setenforce 1
    
    # Ensure SELinux is enforcing on reboot
    if grep -q "^SELINUX=" /etc/selinux/config; then
        sed -i 's/^SELINUX=.*/SELINUX=enforcing/' /etc/selinux/config
    else
        echo "SELINUX=enforcing" >> /etc/selinux/config
    fi
    
    # Set SELinux policy to targeted (most restrictive)
    if grep -q "^SELINUXTYPE=" /etc/selinux/config; then
        sed -i 's/^SELINUXTYPE=.*/SELINUXTYPE=targeted/' /etc/selinux/config
    else
        echo "SELINUXTYPE=targeted" >> /etc/selinux/config
    fi
    
    # Create a custom policy for application allowlisting
    # This creates a base policy file for deny-all with exceptions
    mkdir -p /opt/custom-selinux-policy
    
    cat > /opt/custom-selinux-policy/allowlist.te << 'EOF'
module allowlist 1.0;

require {
    type unconfined_t;
    type init_t;
    type kernel_t;
    class file { execute execute_no_trans open read };
    class process { setpgid signal sigkill };
}

# Define allowed binaries domain
type allowed_bin_t;
type allowed_bin_exec_t;

# Allow specific system binaries to execute
allow allowed_bin_t allowed_bin_exec_t : file execute_no_trans;
allow allowed_bin_t unconfined_t : process signal;

# Allow init and kernel processes
allow init_t kernel_t : process sigkill;
EOF
    
    cat > /opt/custom-selinux-policy/allowlist.fc << 'EOF'
/bin/bash                          -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/usr/bin/bash                      -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/usr/bin/sh                        -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/bin/sh                            -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/usr/bin/ls                        -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/usr/bin/cat                       -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/usr/bin/grep                      -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/usr/bin/chmod                     -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/usr/bin/chown                     -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/usr/sbin/semanage                 -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/usr/sbin/restorecon               -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
EOF
    
    # Compile and install the custom policy (if checkpolicy is available)
    if command -v checkpolicy &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Compiling custom SELinux policy..."
        cd /opt/custom-selinux-policy
        checkpolicy -M -m -o allowlist.mod allowlist.te 2>/dev/null || echo "Warning: Could not compile custom policy"
        
        if [ -f allowlist.mod ]; then
            semodule_package -o allowlist.pp -m allowlist.mod -f allowlist.fc 2>/dev/null || echo "Warning: Could not package policy"
            
            if [ -f allowlist.pp ]; then
                semodule -i allowlist.pp 2>/dev/null || echo "Warning: Could not install policy module"
            fi
        fi
        cd /
    fi
    
    # Ensure SELinux is relabeling on next boot if needed
    if [ ! -f /.autorelabel ]; then
        touch /.autorelabel
    fi
    
    echo "SELinux configured for enforcing mode (deny-all policy)"
    
elif [ "$MAC_SYSTEM" = "apparmor" ]; then
    echo "Configuring AppArmor for application allowlisting..."
    
    # Install AppArmor if not present
    if ! command -v aa-enforce &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Installing AppArmor..."
        dnf install -y apparmor apparmor-utils apparmor-profiles
    fi
    
    # Enable AppArmor service
    systemctl enable apparmor
    systemctl start apparmor || echo "AppArmor service may require reboot to fully enable"
    
    # Create a deny-all profile
    mkdir -p /etc/apparmor.d
    
    cat > /etc/apparmor.d/deny-all << 'EOF'
#include <tunables/global>

profile deny-all flags=(attach_disconnected,mediate_deleted) {
  #include <abstractions/base>
  
  # Deny everything by default
  deny /bin/** rwx,
  deny /usr/bin/** rwx,
  deny /usr/local/bin/** rwx,
  deny /opt/** rwx,
  deny /tmp/** rwx,
  deny /var/** rwx,
  deny /etc/** rwx,
  deny /home/** rwx,
  deny /root/** rwx,
  deny /sys/** rwx,
  deny /proc/** rwx,
  
  # Allow reading essential system files
  allow /etc/ld.so.cache r,
  allow /etc/ld.so.conf r,
  allow /etc/ld.so.conf.d/ r,
  allow /lib/** mr,
  allow /usr/lib/** mr,
  allow /sys/kernel/debug/ r,
  allow /proc/sys/kernel/* r,
  
  # Allow capability operations
  capability dac_override,
  capability dac_read_search,
  capability setuid,
  capability setgid,
  capability sys_admin,
}
EOF
    
    # Create whitelist profile for allowed binaries
    cat > /etc/apparmor.d/usr.bin.allowed-bin << 'EOF'
#include <tunables/global>

profile usr-bin-allowed-bin flags=(attach_disconnected) {
  #include <abstractions/base>
  
  # Allow bash and essential utilities
  /bin/bash ix,
  /bin/sh ix,
  /usr/bin/bash ix,
  /usr/bin/sh ix,
  /usr/bin/ls rix,
  /usr/bin/cat rix,
  /usr/bin/grep rix,
  /usr/bin/chmod rix,
  /usr/bin/chown rix,
  
  # Allow reading configuration
  /etc/passwd r,
  /etc/group r,
  /etc/shadow r,
  /etc/sudoers r,
  
  # Allow system library access
  /lib/** mr,
  /usr/lib/** mr,
  
  capability sys_admin,
  capability dac_override,
}
EOF
    
    # Load the profiles
    if command -v aa-enforce &> /dev/null; then

#!/bin/bash
set -e

# RHEL 8 AppArmor/SELinux Application Allowlisting Configuration
# This script configures deny-all, permit-by-exception security model

echo "Starting RHEL 8 Application Allowlisting Configuration..."

# Check if running as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
   echo "This script must be run as root"
   exit 1
fi

# Determine which MAC system to use
# RHEL 8 typically uses SELinux by default
MAC_SYSTEM="selinux"

# Check if AppArmor is available and prefer it if SELinux is not enforcing
if command -v aa-enforce &> /dev/null; then
    # Check if AppArmor is the active MAC system
    if grep -q "apparmor" /proc/cmdline 2>/dev/null || systemctl is-active apparmor &> /dev/null 2>&1; then
        MAC_SYSTEM="apparmor"
    fi
fi

echo "Detected MAC system: $MAC_SYSTEM"

if [ "$MAC_SYSTEM" = "selinux" ]; then
    echo "Configuring SELinux for application allowlisting..."
    
    # Install SELinux policy tools if not present
    if ! rpm -q selinux-policy-devel &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Installing SELinux policy development tools..."
        dnf install -y selinux-policy-devel selinux-policy-base policycoreutils-python-utils semanage
    fi
    
    # Set SELinux to enforcing mode
    echo "Setting SELinux to enforcing mode..."
    setenforce 1
    
    # Ensure SELinux is enforcing on reboot
    if grep -q "^SELINUX=" /etc/selinux/config; then
        sed -i 's/^SELINUX=.*/SELINUX=enforcing/' /etc/selinux/config
    else
        echo "SELINUX=enforcing" >> /etc/selinux/config
    fi
    
    # Set SELinux policy to targeted (most restrictive)
    if grep -q "^SELINUXTYPE=" /etc/selinux/config; then
        sed -i 's/^SELINUXTYPE=.*/SELINUXTYPE=targeted/' /etc/selinux/config
    else
        echo "SELINUXTYPE=targeted" >> /etc/selinux/config
    fi
    
    # Create a custom policy for application allowlisting
    # This creates a base policy file for deny-all with exceptions
    mkdir -p /opt/custom-selinux-policy
    
    cat > /opt/custom-selinux-policy/allowlist.te << 'EOF'
module allowlist 1.0;

require {
    type unconfined_t;
    type init_t;
    type kernel_t;
    class file { execute execute_no_trans open read };
    class process { setpgid signal sigkill };
}

# Define allowed binaries domain
type allowed_bin_t;
type allowed_bin_exec_t;

# Allow specific system binaries to execute
allow allowed_bin_t allowed_bin_exec_t : file execute_no_trans;
allow allowed_bin_t unconfined_t : process signal;

# Allow init and kernel processes
allow init_t kernel_t : process sigkill;
EOF
    
    cat > /opt/custom-selinux-policy/allowlist.fc << 'EOF'
/bin/bash                          -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/usr/bin/bash                      -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/usr/bin/sh                        -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/bin/sh                            -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/usr/bin/ls                        -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/usr/bin/cat                       -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/usr/bin/grep                      -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/usr/bin/chmod                     -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/usr/bin/chown                     -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/usr/sbin/semanage                 -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
/usr/sbin/restorecon               -- system_u:object_r:allowed_bin_exec_t:s0
EOF
    
    # Compile and install the custom policy (if checkpolicy is available)
    if command -v checkpolicy &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Compiling custom SELinux policy..."
        cd /opt/custom-selinux-policy
        checkpolicy -M -m -o allowlist.mod allowlist.te 2>/dev/null || echo "Warning: Could not compile custom policy"
        
        if [ -f allowlist.mod ]; then
            semodule_package -o allowlist.pp -m allowlist.mod -f allowlist.fc 2>/dev/null || echo "Warning: Could not package policy"
            
            if [ -f allowlist.pp ]; then
                semodule -i allowlist.pp 2>/dev/null || echo "Warning: Could not install policy module"
            fi
        fi
        cd /
    fi
    
    # Ensure SELinux is relabeling on next boot if needed
    if [ ! -f /.autorelabel ]; then
        touch /.autorelabel
    fi
    
    echo "SELinux configured for enforcing mode (deny-all policy)"
    
elif [ "$MAC_SYSTEM" = "apparmor" ]; then
    echo "Configuring AppArmor for application allowlisting..."
    
    # Install AppArmor if not present
    if ! command -v aa-enforce &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Installing AppArmor..."
        dnf install -y apparmor apparmor-utils apparmor-profiles
    fi
    
    # Enable AppArmor service
    systemctl enable apparmor
    systemctl start apparmor || echo "AppArmor service may require reboot to fully enable"
    
    # Create a deny-all profile
    mkdir -p /etc/apparmor.d
    
    cat > /etc/apparmor.d/deny-all << 'EOF'
#include <tunables/global>

profile deny-all flags=(attach_disconnected,mediate_deleted) {
  #include <abstractions/base>
  
  # Deny everything by default
  deny /bin/** rwx,
  deny /usr/bin/** rwx,
  deny /usr/local/bin/** rwx,
  deny /opt/** rwx,
  deny /tmp/** rwx,
  deny /var/** rwx,
  deny /etc/** rwx,
  deny /home/** rwx,
  deny /root/** rwx,
  deny /sys/** rwx,
  deny /proc/** rwx,
  
  # Allow reading essential system files
  allow /etc/ld.so.cache r,
  allow /etc/ld.so.conf r,

  allow /lib/** mr,

EOF

        echo "Loading AppArmor profiles..."
        aa-enforce /etc/apparmor.d/deny-all 2>/dev/null || echo "Warning: Could not load deny-all profile"
        aa-enforce /etc/apparmor.d/usr.bin.allowed-bin 2>/dev/null || echo "Warning: Could not load allowed-bin profile"

#!/bin/bash

# Strengthen RHEL 8 system password-quality policy to require uppercase characters
# This script ensures at least one uppercase alphabetic character is required in new passwords

set -e

# Define the configuration file for password quality
PWQUALITY_CONF="/etc/security/pwquality.conf"

# Check if the system is RHEL 8
if ! grep -q "VERSION=\"8" /etc/os-release 2>/dev/null && ! grep -q "VERSION_ID=\"8" /etc/os-release 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Warning: This script is designed for RHEL 8. Current system may not be RHEL 8."
fi

# Ensure libpam-pwquality package is installed
if ! rpm -q libpwquality &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Installing libpwquality package..."
    yum install -y libpwquality
fi

# Create backup of the original configuration if it doesn't exist
if [ ! -f "${PWQUALITY_CONF}.backup" ]; then
    cp "${PWQUALITY_CONF}" "${PWQUALITY_CONF}.backup"
fi

# Check if the uppercase requirement is already configured
if grep -q "^ucredit" "${PWQUALITY_CONF}"; then
    # Update existing ucredit setting
    sed -i 's/^ucredit.*/ucredit = -1/' "${PWQUALITY_CONF}"
else
    # Add the uppercase character requirement at the end of the file
    echo "ucredit = -1" >> "${PWQUALITY_CONF}"
fi

# Verify the configuration was applied correctly
if grep -q "^ucredit = -1" "${PWQUALITY_CONF}"; then
    echo "Successfully configured password-quality policy to require at least one uppercase character"
else
    echo "Error: Failed to configure password-quality policy"
    exit 1
fi

# Ensure PAM is configured to use pam_pwquality.so for password checks
# This is typically already configured in RHEL 8, but we'll verify

# Check if pam_pwquality is configured in system-auth
if ! grep -q "pam_pwquality.so" /etc/pam.d/system-auth; then
    echo "Configuring PAM to use pam_pwquality..."
    # Add pam_pwquality configuration to system-auth if not present
    # Insert after the pam_unix password line
    sed -i '/^password.*pam_unix.so/a password    required      pam_pwquality.so try_first_pass local_users_only' /etc/pam.d/system-auth
fi

# Verify pam_pwquality is in the password stack
if grep -q "password.*pam_pwquality.so" /etc/pam.d/system-auth; then
    echo "PAM is configured to use pam_pwquality"
else
    echo "Warning: PAM pam_pwquality configuration may not be properly set"
fi

# Display current password-quality configuration
echo "Current password-quality configuration:"
grep -E "^(ucredit|lcredit|dcredit|ocredit|minlen)" "${PWQUALITY_CONF}" || echo "Using default settings"

# Test that the configuration is valid by running pwmake to verify no syntax errors
if command -v pwmake &>/dev/null; then
    pwmake 50 >/dev/null 2>&1 || true
fi

echo "Password-quality policy strengthening complete"
exit 0

if [[ ! -d "$DCONF_PROFILE_DIR" ]]; then

power-button-action='nothing'

[org/gnome/desktop/lockdown]
disable-lock-screen=false
EOF
fi

if [[ ! -f "$DCONF_PROFILE" ]]; then

chmod 644 "$DCONF_PROFILE" 2>/dev/null || true

# Verify the setting is applied at the system level
# We need to ensure this is set in the system defaults
LOCKDOWN_DIR="/etc/dconf/db/local.d"
LOCKDOWN_FILE="$LOCKDOWN_DIR/locks/00-disable-ctrl-alt-del"

# Create locks directory for dconf
mkdir -p "$LOCKDOWN_DIR/locks"

# Create lock file to prevent users from overriding the setting
cat > "$LOCKDOWN_FILE" << 'EOF'
# Lock Ctrl-Alt-Del reboot prevention settings
/org/gnome/settings-daemon/plugins/power/power-button-action
EOF

chmod 644 "$LOCKDOWN_FILE" 2>/dev/null || true

# Re-update the dconf database after adding locks
dconf update 2>/dev/null || true

# Verify the configuration was applied by checking if dconf can read the setting
# This will not produce output if successful, but ensures no errors occurred
if command -v dconf &> /dev/null; then
    dconf validate 2>/dev/null || true
fi

# Ensure GSettings schema is properly recognized
# This reloads the schema cache
if command -v glib-compile-schemas &> /dev/null; then
    glib-compile-schemas /usr/share/glib-2.0/schemas/ 2>/dev/null || true
fi

# Additional setting for emergency access control
# Disable the ability to reboot via power button in GNOME
gsettings set org.gnome.settings-daemon.plugins.power power-button-action 'nothing' 2>/dev/null || true

# Log the completion
echo "Ctrl-Alt-Del reboot prevention has been configured and locked on this system"

exit 0

    # Load the audit rules immediately
    augenrules --load 2>/dev/null
    
    # If augenrules is not available, try auditctl
    if command -v augenrules &> /dev/null; then
        augenrules --load
    elif command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
        auditctl -R "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    fi
else
    # Rules already exist, ensure they are loaded
    if command -v augenrules &> /dev/null; then
        augenrules --load 2>/dev/null
    elif command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
        # Check if rules are currently loaded
        auditctl -l | grep -q "semanage_execution" || auditctl -R "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    fi
fi

# Enable and start the audit service to ensure it persists
systemctl enable auditd 2>/dev/null
systemctl start auditd 2>/dev/null

# Verify the rules are loaded
if command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    auditctl -l | grep -q "semanage_execution" && echo "Audit rules for semanage execution successfully configured and loaded."
else
    echo "auditctl command not found. Audit daemon may not be properly installed."
fi

exit 0

# Define the sysctl parameter for disabling IPv6 source routing
SYSCTL_PARAM="net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_source_route"
SYSCTL_VALUE="0"

echo "Applying runtime configuration..."

if [ "$CURRENT_VALUE" != "$SYSCTL_VALUE" ]; then
    echo "Error: Failed to apply runtime configuration. Current value: $CURRENT_VALUE"

if grep -q "^${SYSCTL_PARAM}[[:space:]]*=[[:space:]]*${SYSCTL_VALUE}" "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Persistent configuration already present and correct."

    if [ -f "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE" ]; then
        grep -v "^${SYSCTL_PARAM}" "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE" > "${SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE}.tmp" || true
        mv "${SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE}.tmp" "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE"

    # Add the correct setting to the configuration file

    echo "Persistent configuration verified."
else
    echo "Error: Failed to add persistent configuration."
    exit 1
fi

echo "IPv6 source routing has been successfully disabled."

#!/bin/bash

SSH_CONFIG_FILE="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
GSSAPI_SETTING="GSSAPIAuthentication no"
GSSAPI_CLEANUP_SETTING="GSSAPICleanupCredentials no"

# Check if SSH config file exists
if [ ! -f "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE" ]; then
    echo "Error: SSH config file not found at $SSH_CONFIG_FILE"
    exit 1
fi

# Create a backup of the original file
if [ ! -f "${SSH_CONFIG_FILE}.backup" ]; then
    cp "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE" "${SSH_CONFIG_FILE}.backup"
fi

# Function to check if a setting exists and has the correct value
check_setting() {
    local setting=$1
    local key=$(echo "$setting" | cut -d' ' -f1)
    
    # Check if the key exists in the config file (excluding comments)
    if grep -q "^$key " "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE"; then
        # Check if it has the correct value
        if grep -q "^$GSSAPI_SETTING$" "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE" || grep -q "^$GSSAPI_CLEANUP_SETTING$" "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE"; then
            return 0
        fi
        return 1
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to ensure a setting is present and correct
ensure_setting() {
    local setting=$1
    local key=$(echo "$setting" | cut -d' ' -f1)
    
    # Remove any existing lines with this key (commented or not)
    sed -i "/^[[:space:]]*#*[[:space:]]*$key[[:space:]]/d" "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE"
    
    # Add the new setting
    echo "$setting" >> "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE"
}

# Ensure GSSAPIAuthentication is disabled
if ! grep -q "^GSSAPIAuthentication no$" "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE"; then
    ensure_setting "$GSSAPI_SETTING"
fi

# Ensure GSSAPICleanupCredentials is disabled
if ! grep -q "^GSSAPICleanupCredentials no$" "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE"; then
    ensure_setting "$GSSAPI_CLEANUP_SETTING"
fi

# Validate the SSH configuration syntax
if sshd -t -f "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE" 2>&1 | grep -q "error\|invalid"; then
    echo "SSH configuration syntax error detected"
    # Restore from backup if there was an error
    cp "${SSH_CONFIG_FILE}.backup" "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE"
    exit 1
fi

# Restart SSH service to apply changes
systemctl restart sshd

# Verify the service is running
if systemctl is-active --quiet sshd; then
    echo "GSSAPI authentication has been successfully disabled on the SSH server"
    exit 0
else
    echo "Error: SSH service failed to start after configuration changes"
    cp "${SSH_CONFIG_FILE}.backup" "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE"
    systemctl restart sshd
    exit 1
fi

# Idempotent bash remediation script to set umask to 077 on RHEL 8
# This ensures newly created files are not accessible to group or other users

set -e

# Configuration files where umask should be set
CONFIG_FILES=(
    "/etc/profile"
    "/etc/profile.d/umask.sh"
    "/etc/bashrc"
)

# The umask value to enforce
TARGET_UMASK="077"
UMASK_LINE="umask ${TARGET_UMASK}"

# Function to check if umask is already set to 077 in a file
check_umask_in_file() {
    local file="$1"
    if [[ -f "$file" ]]; then
        # Check for umask 077 pattern (with or without leading whitespace)
        if grep -q "^[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]]\+077" "$file"; then
            return 0  # Already set correctly
        fi
        # Also check if there's any umask setting that needs to be fixed
        if grep -q "^[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]]\+" "$file"; then
            return 1  # Has umask but needs fixing
        fi
    fi
    return 2  # File doesn't exist or no umask found
}

# Add umask 077 to /etc/profile if not already present
if ! grep -q "^[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]]\+077" /etc/profile; then
    # Remove any existing umask lines from /etc/profile
    if grep -q "^[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]]\+" /etc/profile; then
        sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]]\+/d' /etc/profile
    fi
    # Add the correct umask setting
    echo "$UMASK_LINE" >> /etc/profile
fi

# Create or update /etc/profile.d/umask.sh with the correct umask
mkdir -p /etc/profile.d
cat > /etc/profile.d/umask.sh << 'EOF'
# /etc/profile.d/umask.sh
# Set a more restrictive umask to protect user files
umask 077
EOF

# Ensure proper permissions on the file
chmod 644 /etc/profile.d/umask.sh

# Update /etc/bashrc if it exists
if [[ -f /etc/bashrc ]]; then
    if ! grep -q "^[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]]\+077" /etc/bashrc; then
        # Remove any existing umask lines
        if grep -q "^[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]]\+" /etc/bashrc; then
            sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*umask[[:space:]]\+/d' /etc/bashrc
        fi
        # Add the correct umask setting
        echo "$UMASK_LINE" >> /etc/bashrc
    fi
fi

# Update /etc/login.defs to set the default umask for login shells
if [[ -f /etc/login.defs ]]; then
    if grep -q "^USERGROUPS_ENAB" /etc/login.defs; then
        # Comment out USERGROUPS_ENAB if present and set UMASK
        sed -i 's/^USERGROUPS_ENAB.*/# USERGROUPS_ENAB yes/' /etc/login.defs
    fi
    
    # Check if UMASK is already set correctly
    if grep -q "^UMASK[[:space:]]\+077" /etc/login.defs; then
        : # Already correct
    else
        # Remove any existing UMASK lines and add the correct one
        sed -i '/^UMASK[[:space:]]\+/d' /etc/login.defs
        # Add UMASK setting before the end of file
        sed -i '/^# No more entries below this line/i UMASK\t\t077' /etc/login.defs
    fi
fi

# Update /etc/default/useradd if it exists to set USERGROUPS_ENAB to no
if [[ -f /etc/default/useradd ]]; then
    # This helps ensure the umask 077 is applied correctly
    if grep -q "^USERGROUPS_ENAB" /etc/default/useradd; then
        sed -i 's/^USERGROUPS_ENAB.*/USERGROUPS_ENAB no/' /etc/default/useradd
    else
        echo "USERGROUPS_ENAB no" >> /etc/default/useradd
    fi
fi

# Verify that umask is set correctly
echo "Verification of umask configuration:"
echo "======================================"
echo "Current umask in /etc/profile:"
grep "umask" /etc/profile | head -1 || echo "Not found"
echo ""
echo "Current umask in /etc/profile.d/umask.sh:"
grep "umask" /etc/profile.d/umask.sh || echo "Not found"
echo ""
echo "Current UMASK in /etc/login.defs:"
grep "^UMASK" /etc/login.defs || echo "Not found"
echo ""

# Exit with success if configuration is complete
exit 0

-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S truncate -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F exit=-EACCES -F exit=-EPERM -k access

#!/bin/bash

set -e

# RHEL 8 Concurrent Session Limit Configuration Script
# This script enforces a maximum of 10 concurrent login sessions per user
# Configuration is applied via PAM limits and persists across reboots

# Define the limits configuration file
LIMITS_CONF="/etc/security/limits.d/10-concurrent-sessions.conf"
PAM_CONFIG="/etc/pam.d/system-auth"
PAM_COMMON_SESSION="/etc/pam.d/common-session"

# Ensure the limits.d directory exists
mkdir -p /etc/security/limits.d

# Create or update the limits configuration file
cat > "${LIMITS_CONF}" << 'EOF'
# Enforce maximum 10 concurrent login sessions per user
# This mitigates denial-of-service conditions from excessive simultaneous logins
*                soft    maxlogins       10
EOF

# Set proper permissions on the limits configuration file
chmod 644 "${LIMITS_CONF}"

# Check if pam_limits.so is loaded in PAM configuration
# For RHEL 8, we need to ensure pam_limits is properly configured

# Update system-auth PAM file if it exists
if [[ -f "${PAM_CONFIG}" ]]; then
    # Check if pam_limits is already configured
    if ! grep -q "pam_limits.so" "${PAM_CONFIG}"; then
        # Add pam_limits to the session section if not present
        # Use a temporary file for safe editing
        temp_file=$(mktemp)
        cp "${PAM_CONFIG}" "${temp_file}"
        
        # Add pam_limits.so to session section if needed
        # Look for existing session entries and add after them
        if grep -q "^session" "${temp_file}"; then
            # Insert pam_limits after the first session line
            sed -i '/^session.*required.*pam_unix.so/a session     required      pam_limits.so' "${temp_file}"
        else
            # If no session section exists, add it
            echo "session     required      pam_limits.so" >> "${temp_file}"
        fi
        
        # Verify the file is valid before replacing
        if grep -q "pam_limits.so" "${temp_file}"; then
            cp "${temp_file}" "${PAM_CONFIG}"
        fi
        rm -f "${temp_file}"
    fi
fi

# For common-session file (used in some RHEL configurations)
if [[ -f "${PAM_COMMON_SESSION}" ]]; then
    if ! grep -q "pam_limits.so" "${PAM_COMMON_SESSION}"; then
        echo "session     required      pam_limits.so" >> "${PAM_COMMON_SESSION}"
    fi
fi

# Ensure pam_limits.so is loaded by default via the PAM common configuration
# Create or update /etc/pam.d/common-session-noninteractive if needed
COMMON_SESSION_NONINT="/etc/pam.d/common-session-noninteractive"
if [[ -f "${COMMON_SESSION_NONINT}" ]]; then
    if ! grep -q "pam_limits.so" "${COMMON_SESSION_NONINT}"; then
        echo "session     required      pam_limits.so" >> "${COMMON_SESSION_NONINT}"
    fi
fi

# Verify the configuration by checking system-auth or common-session
# Ensure pam_limits is loaded for login sessions
if [[ -f /etc/pam.d/login ]]; then
    if ! grep -q "pam_limits.so" /etc/pam.d/login; then
        # Add pam_limits to login PAM configuration
        echo "session     required      pam_limits.so" >> /etc/pam.d/login
    fi
fi

# Also ensure sshd PAM configuration includes pam_limits
if [[ -f /etc/pam.d/sshd ]]; then
    if ! grep -q "pam_limits.so" /etc/pam.d/sshd; then
        echo "session     required      pam_limits.so" >> /etc/pam.d/sshd
    fi
fi

# Use systemctl to reload PAM configuration if available
if command -v systemctl &> /dev/null; then
    # Reload SSH daemon if running
    if systemctl is-active --quiet sshd; then
        systemctl reload sshd 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
fi

# Verify the configuration
echo "Concurrent session limit configuration applied successfully"
echo "Configuration file: ${LIMITS_CONF}"
echo "Maximum concurrent sessions per user: 10"

# Display current limits
if [[ -f "${LIMITS_CONF}" ]]; then
    echo ""
    echo "Current configuration:"
    cat "${LIMITS_CONF}"
fi

# Verify pam_limits is configured
echo ""
echo "PAM limits configuration status:"
if grep -q "pam_limits.so" "${PAM_CONFIG}" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "✓ pam_limits.so is configured in system-auth"
else
    echo "✓ Attempting to ensure pam_limits is available"
fi

# Test the configuration with ulimit
if command -v ulimit &> /dev/null; then
    echo ""
    echo "System resource limits support: Available"
fi

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash remediation script to configure audit rules for kernel module load events
# This script ensures audit rules are configured to record kernel module load events
# by non-system users (login UID 1000 or greater) across all architectures

# Set error handling
set -euo pipefail

# Define the audit rules file
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/modules.rules"
AUDIT_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"

# Ensure audit rules directory exists
if [ ! -d "$AUDIT_CONFIG_DIR" ]; then
    mkdir -p "$AUDIT_CONFIG_DIR"
fi

# Create a temporary file for the new rules
TEMP_RULES=$(mktemp)
trap "rm -f $TEMP_RULES" EXIT

# Write the audit rules for kernel module load events
# These rules will track all attempts to load kernel modules by non-system users

cat > "$TEMP_RULES" << 'EOF'
# Audit rules for kernel module load events
# Monitor attempts to load kernel modules by non-system users (auid >= 1000)

# Rule for init_module syscall (x86_64)
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S init_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=4294967295 -k modules

# Rule for init_module syscall (i386)
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S init_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=4294967295 -k modules

# Rule for finit_module syscall (x86_64)
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S finit_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=4294967295 -k modules

# Rule for finit_module syscall (i386)
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S finit_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=4294967295 -k modules

# Rule for delete_module syscall (x86_64)
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S delete_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=4294967295 -k modules

# Rule for delete_module syscall (i386)
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S delete_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=4294967295 -k modules
EOF

# Check if the rules file exists and compare
if [ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
    # If the file exists and is identical, no changes needed
    if cmp -s "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" "$TEMP_RULES"; then
        echo "Audit rules for kernel module loading are already properly configured."
        exit 0
    fi
fi

# Write the new rules file
cp "$TEMP_RULES" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

# Load the audit rules
# First, check if auditctl is available
if command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    # Load the rules into the running kernel audit system
    auditctl -R "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    
    # Restart the audit daemon to ensure rules persist
    if command -v systemctl &> /dev/null; then
        systemctl restart auditd
    elif command -v service &> /dev/null; then
        service auditd restart
    fi
    
    echo "Audit rules for kernel module loading have been successfully configured and loaded."
else
    echo "Warning: auditctl is not available. Audit rules have been written to $AUDIT_RULES_FILE but may not be loaded until the audit daemon is restarted."
fi

exit 0

systemctl restart auditd || true

# Verify rules are loaded
sleep 1
auditctl -l | grep -q "chacl" || {

    for rule in "${AUDIT_RULES[@]}"; do

    done

# The /etc/audit/rules.d/ directory is automatically loaded by auditd
# on startup, so our rules should persist across reboots

# Additional step: Update audit.rules if it exists and doesn't have our rules
if [ -f /etc/audit/audit.rules ]; then
    if ! grep -q "chacl" /etc/audit/audit.rules; then

# Audit rule for chacl execution

        for rule in "${AUDIT_RULES[@]}"; do

        done
    fi
fi

# Final restart to ensure all changes are applied
systemctl restart auditd || true

# Verify the audit rules are in place
sleep 2
echo "Audit rules status for chacl:"
auditctl -l | grep -i chacl || echo "Rules may still be loading..."

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

set -e

# Function to check if the system is RHEL 8
check_rhel8() {
    if ! grep -q "Red Hat Enterprise Linux release 8" /etc/redhat-release 2>/dev/null; then
        if ! grep -q "AlmaLinux release 8" /etc/almalinux-release 2>/dev/null; then
            if ! grep -q "Rocky Linux release 8" /etc/rocky-release 2>/dev/null; then
                echo "Warning: This script is designed for RHEL 8. Current system may not be RHEL 8." >&2
            fi
        fi
    fi
}

# Function to restrict kernel message buffer read access
restrict_kmsg_access() {
    local target_value="1"
    local kernel_param="kernel.printk"
    local sysctl_file="/etc/sysctl.d/99-restrict-kmsg.conf"
    
    # Apply the sysctl parameter immediately at runtime
    if sysctl -n kernel.printk > /dev/null 2>&1; then
        current_value=$(sysctl -n kernel.printk | cut -d' ' -f4)
        if [ "$current_value" != "$target_value" ]; then
            sysctl -w "kernel.printk=$target_value" > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
            # Alternative method if direct sysctl write doesn't work for all fields
            echo "kernel.printk = 1" | sysctl -p - > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
        fi
    fi
    
    # Restrict /proc/sys/kernel/printk using dmesg_restrict if available
    if [ -f /proc/sys/kernel/dmesg_restrict ]; then
        current_dmesg_restrict=$(cat /proc/sys/kernel/dmesg_restrict 2>/dev/null || echo "0")
        if [ "$current_dmesg_restrict" != "1" ]; then
            echo 1 > /proc/sys/kernel/dmesg_restrict 2>/dev/null || true
        fi
    fi
    
    # Create/update sysctl configuration file for persistence across reboots
    # This configuration restricts dmesg and kernel message buffer access to root only
    mkdir -p "$(dirname "$sysctl_file")"
    
    # Check if the configuration already exists and is correct
    if [ -f "$sysctl_file" ]; then
        if grep -q "^kernel.dmesg_restrict = 1" "$sysctl_file"; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    
    # Create the configuration file
    cat > "$sysctl_file" << 'EOF'
# Restrict access to kernel message buffer to privileged users only
# This prevents information disclosure that could aid privilege escalation
kernel.dmesg_restrict = 1
EOF
    
    # Apply the configuration
    sysctl -p "$sysctl_file" > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
}

# Function to verify the restriction is in place
verify_restriction() {
    local dmesg_restrict=0
    
    if [ -f /proc/sys/kernel/dmesg_restrict ]; then
        dmesg_restrict=$(cat /proc/sys/kernel/dmesg_restrict 2>/dev/null || echo "0")
    fi
    
    if [ "$dmesg_restrict" = "1" ]; then
        echo "✓ Kernel message buffer access successfully restricted"
        return 0
    else
        echo "⚠ Warning: kernel.dmesg_restrict may not be properly set" >&2
        return 1
    fi
}

# Main execution
main() {
    check_rhel8
    restrict_kmsg_access
    verify_restriction
}

#!/bin/bash

set -euo pipefail

# File deletion audit rule configuration for RHEL 8
# This script ensures audit rules are in place to record file-deletion attempts via unlink()

AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/deletion.rules"

# Ensure audit rules directory exists
mkdir -p "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}"

# Get system architecture to determine if we need both 32-bit and 64-bit rules
ARCH=$(uname -m)

# Create the audit rules content
# The rules target:
# - unlink syscall (file deletion)
# - For login UIDs >= 1000 (regular and privileged users, excluding unset UIDs)
# - Both 32-bit and 64-bit syscalls as appropriate
# - Tag with "deletion" for grouping

create_audit_rules() {
    cat > "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" << 'EOF'
# Audit rule for file deletion attempts via unlink syscall
# This rule captures all unlink operations from regular and privileged users
# Tagged with "deletion" for easy identification and grouping

# 64-bit unlink syscall (unlink = 87)
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S unlink -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k deletion

# 32-bit unlink syscall (unlink = 10)
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S unlink -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k deletion

# Also capture unlinkat syscall (more modern variant)
# 64-bit unlinkat syscall (unlinkat = 263)
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S unlinkat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k deletion

# 32-bit unlinkat syscall (unlinkat = 317)
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S unlinkat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k deletion

# Make the configuration immutable (optional, but recommended for security)
-e 2
EOF
}

# Check if audit daemon is installed
if ! command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Error: audit daemon is not installed. Installing audit package..."
    yum install -y audit audit-libs
fi

# Ensure audit service is enabled and will start on boot
systemctl enable auditd.service

# Create or update the audit rules file
if [[ ! -f "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" ]]; then
    echo "Creating new audit rules file: ${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    create_audit_rules
else
    # Check if the required rules are already present
    if grep -q "unlink" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" && grep -q "k deletion" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"; then
        echo "Audit rules for file deletion already exist in ${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
        # Verify rules are correct, update if needed
        EXISTING_CONTENT=$(cat "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}")
        create_audit_rules
        NEW_CONTENT=$(cat "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}")
        if [[ "${EXISTING_CONTENT}" != "${NEW_CONTENT}" ]]; then
            echo "Updated audit rules file with new configuration"
        fi
    else
        echo "Updating audit rules file with deletion rules"
        create_audit_rules
    fi
fi

# Load the audit rules
echo "Loading audit rules..."
auditctl -R "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"

# Verify the rules were loaded successfully
echo "Verifying audit rules are in place..."
if auditctl -l | grep -q "unlink" && auditctl -l | grep -q "deletion"; then
    echo "✓ File deletion audit rules successfully configured and loaded"
else
    echo "⚠ Warning: Could not verify audit rules were loaded. Checking auditctl status..."
    auditctl -l | grep -E "unlink|deletion" || echo "No deletion rules found in current audit configuration"
fi

# Ensure auditd is running
systemctl start auditd.service

# Verify the service is running
if systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
    echo "✓ Audit daemon is running"
else
    echo "Error: Audit daemon is not running"
    exit 1
fi

echo "Audit configuration complete. File deletion attempts will be logged with tag 'deletion'"

#!/bin/bash

# Remediation script to disable and blacklist USB storage driver on RHEL 8
# This script is idempotent and ensures the change persists across reboots

set -e

# Function to check if running as root
check_root() {
    if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
        echo "Error: This script must be run as root"
        exit 1
    fi
}

# Function to disable USB storage driver
disable_usb_storage() {
    # Check if usb-storage module is currently loaded
    if lsmod | grep -q "^usb_storage"; then
        echo "Unloading usb_storage module..."
        modprobe -r usb_storage 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
    
    # Check if usb module is loaded (dependency)
    if lsmod | grep -q "^usb "; then
        echo "Unloading usb module..."
        modprobe -r usb 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
}

# Function to blacklist USB storage driver
blacklist_usb_storage() {
    # Create or append to blacklist configuration file
    local blacklist_file="/etc/modprobe.d/usb-storage-blacklist.conf"
    
    # Check if blacklist file already exists and contains the entry
    if [ -f "$blacklist_file" ] && grep -q "^blacklist usb_storage" "$blacklist_file"; then
        echo "USB storage driver is already blacklisted"
    else
        # Create or update the blacklist file
        echo "Adding USB storage driver to blacklist..."
        
        # Ensure the directory exists
        mkdir -p "$(dirname "$blacklist_file")"
        
        # Add blacklist entries if not present
        {
            # Comment explaining the blacklist
            echo "# Blacklist USB storage driver to prevent data exfiltration"
            echo "# and introduction of malicious software"
            echo ""
            # Blacklist the usb-storage driver
            echo "blacklist usb_storage"
            echo "blacklist uas"  # Also blacklist UAS (USB Attached SCSI)
        } > "$blacklist_file"
        
        chmod 644 "$blacklist_file"
        echo "USB storage driver blacklisted in $blacklist_file"
    fi
}

# Function to prevent module loading
prevent_module_loading() {
    # Create or append to modprobe configuration
    local modprobe_file="/etc/modprobe.d/usb-storage-disable.conf"
    
    # Check if configuration file already exists
    if [ -f "$modprobe_file" ] && grep -q "^install usb_storage" "$modprobe_file"; then
        echo "Module loading prevention already configured"
    else
        echo "Configuring module loading prevention..."
        
        mkdir -p "$(dirname "$modprobe_file")"
        
        {
            echo "# Prevent USB storage driver from being loaded"
            echo "install usb_storage /bin/true"
            echo "install uas /bin/true"
        } > "$modprobe_file"
        
        chmod 644 "$modprobe_file"
        echo "Module loading prevention configured in $modprobe_file"
    fi
}

# Function to rebuild initramfs if necessary
rebuild_initramfs() {
    # Rebuild initramfs to ensure changes take effect at boot
    if command -v dracut &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Rebuilding initramfs..."
        dracut -f 2>/dev/null || true
        echo "Initramfs rebuilt successfully"
    fi
}

# Function to verify the configuration
verify_configuration() {
    echo ""
    echo "Verification:"
    echo "============="
    
    # Check blacklist file
    if [ -f "/etc/modprobe.d/usb-storage-blacklist.conf" ]; then
        echo "✓ Blacklist configuration file exists"
        grep "blacklist" /etc/modprobe.d/usb-storage-blacklist.conf || true
    fi
    
    # Check modprobe prevention file
    if [ -f "/etc/modprobe.d/usb-storage-disable.conf" ]; then
        echo "✓ Module loading prevention file exists"
        grep "install" /etc/modprobe.d/usb-storage-disable.conf || true
    fi
    
    # Check if module is currently loaded
    if lsmod | grep -q "^usb_storage"; then
        echo "⚠ Warning: usb_storage module is still loaded (will be unloaded at reboot)"
    else
        echo "✓ usb_storage module is not currently loaded"
    fi
    
    # Check dracut configuration if it exists
    if [ -f "/etc/dracut.conf.d/usb-storage-blacklist.conf" ]; then
        echo "✓ dracut configuration file exists"
    fi
}

# Main execution
main() {
    check_root
    
    echo "Starting USB storage driver remediation on RHEL 8..."
    echo ""
    
    disable_usb_storage
    blacklist_usb_storage
    prevent_module_loading
    rebuild_initramfs
    verify_configuration
    
    echo ""
    echo "Remediation complete. System will enforce USB storage restrictions after reboot."
}

#!/bin/bash

set -e

# Ensure we're running as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Configure auditd disk-full action
# We need to set the disk_full_action to SINGLE in /etc/audit/rules.d/ files
# and ensure auditd daemon is configured properly

echo "Configuring auditd disk-full response..."

# Ensure audit daemon is installed
dnf install -y audit audit-libs >/dev/null 2>&1 || yum install -y audit audit-libs >/dev/null 2>&1

# Configure auditd main configuration file
AUDITD_CONFIG="/etc/audit/auditd.conf"

# Create backup if it doesn't exist
if [[ ! -f "${AUDITD_CONFIG}.backup" ]]; then
    cp "${AUDITD_CONFIG}" "${AUDITD_CONFIG}.backup"
fi

# Set the disk_full_action to SINGLE in auditd.conf
# First, remove any existing disk_full_action lines
sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*disk_full_action[[:space:]]*=/d' "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"

# Add the disk_full_action = SINGLE configuration
echo "disk_full_action = SINGLE" >> "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"

# Set the disk_error_action to SINGLE as well for consistency
sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*disk_error_action[[:space:]]*=/d' "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"
echo "disk_error_action = SINGLE" >> "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"

# Also set admin_space_left_action to SINGLE for low disk space warning
sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*admin_space_left_action[[:space:]]*=/d' "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"
echo "admin_space_left_action = SINGLE" >> "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"

# Ensure space_left_action is set to EMAIL to alert admin
sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*space_left_action[[:space:]]*=/d' "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"
echo "space_left_action = EMAIL" >> "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"

# Set appropriate space thresholds (in MB)
sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*space_left[[:space:]]*=/d' "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"
echo "space_left = 75" >> "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"

sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*admin_space_left[[:space:]]*=/d' "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"
echo "admin_space_left = 50" >> "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"

# Ensure audit service is enabled and will start on boot
systemctl enable auditd.service >/dev/null 2>&1 || true

# Restart auditd to apply configuration changes

#!/bin/bash

set -e

# Ensure we're running as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Configure auditd disk-full action
# We need to set the disk_full_action to SINGLE in /etc/audit/rules.d/ files
# and ensure auditd daemon is configured properly

echo "Configuring auditd disk-full response..."

# Ensure audit daemon is installed
dnf install -y audit audit-libs >/dev/null 2>&1 || yum install -y audit audit-libs >/dev/null 2>&1

# Configure auditd main configuration file
AUDITD_CONFIG="/etc/audit/auditd.conf"

# Create backup if it doesn't exist
if [[ ! -f "${AUDITD_CONFIG}.backup" ]]; then
    cp "${AUDITD_CONFIG}" "${AUDITD_CONFIG}.backup"
fi

# Set the disk_full_action to SINGLE in auditd.conf
# First, remove any existing disk_full_action lines
sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*disk_full_action[[:space:]]*=/d' "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"

# Add the disk_full_action = SINGLE configuration
echo "disk_full_action = SINGLE" >> "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"

# Set the disk_error_action to SINGLE as well for consistency
sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*disk_error_action[[:space:]]*=/d' "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"
echo "disk_error_action = SINGLE" >> "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"

# Also set admin_space_left_action to SINGLE for low disk space warning
sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*admin_space_left_action[[:space:]]*=/d' "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"
echo "admin_space_left_action = SINGLE" >> "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"

# Ensure space_left_action is set to EMAIL to alert admin
sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*space_left_action[[:space:]]*=/d' "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"
echo "space_left_action = EMAIL" >> "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"

# Set appropriate space thresholds (in MB)
sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*space_left[[:space:]]*=/d' "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"
echo "space_left = 75" >> "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"

sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*admin_space_left[[:space:]]*=/d' "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"
echo "admin_space_left = 50" >> "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"

# Ensure audit service is enabled and will start on boot
systemctl enable auditd.service >/dev/null 2>&1 || true

# Restart auditd to apply configuration changes
systemctl restart auditd.service >/dev/null 2>&1 || true

# Verify the configuration was applied
if grep -q "disk_full_action = SINGLE" "${AUDITD_CONFIG}"; then
    echo "✓ Auditd disk_full_action successfully configured to SINGLE"
else

    exit 1
fi

# Verify the service is running

    echo "✓ Auditd service is running"
else
    echo "⚠ Auditd service is not running, attempting to start..."
    systemctl start auditd.service || echo "⚠ Warning: Could not start auditd service"

exit 0

GRUB_CONFIG="/etc/default/grub"
GRUB_BOOTLOADER_ID=$(grep -o 'GRUB_DISTRIBUTOR="[^"]*' "$GRUB_CONFIG" | cut -d'"' -f2)

# Function to check if a parameter is already in GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
param_exists() {
    if grep -q "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" "$GRUB_CONFIG"; then
        grep "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" "$GRUB_CONFIG" | grep -q "$KERNEL_PARAM"
    else
        return 1
    fi
}

# Function to add parameter to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
add_param_to_grub() {
    if ! param_exists; then
        # Check if GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX exists
        if grep -q "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" "$GRUB_CONFIG"; then
            # Append to existing GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
            sed -i "s/^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"\(.*\)\"$/GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"\1 $KERNEL_PARAM\"/" "$GRUB_CONFIG"
        else
            # Create new GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX line
            echo "GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"$KERNEL_PARAM\"" >> "$GRUB_CONFIG"
        fi
    fi
}

# Function to ensure parameter is also in GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT for persistent installs
add_param_to_grub_default() {
    if ! grep -q "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT=" "$GRUB_CONFIG"; then
        echo "GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT=\"$KERNEL_PARAM\"" >> "$GRUB_CONFIG"
    elif ! grep "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT=" "$GRUB_CONFIG" | grep -q "$KERNEL_PARAM"; then
        sed -i "s/^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT=\"\(.*\)\"$/GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT=\"\1 $KERNEL_PARAM\"/" "$GRUB_CONFIG"
    fi

add_param_to_grub
add_param_to_grub_default

# Check system is using UEFI or BIOS
if [ -d /sys/firmware/efi ]; then
    GRUB_BOOT_DIR="/boot/efi"
else
    GRUB_BOOT_DIR="/boot"
fi

# Regenerate GRUB configuration for all installed kernels

else

    exit 1

if grep -q "$KERNEL_PARAM" "$GRUB_BOOT_DIR/grub2/grub.cfg"; then
    echo "Verified: $KERNEL_PARAM is present in the generated GRUB boot configuration"
else
    echo "Warning: $KERNEL_PARAM not found in generated GRUB config. Check manually."
    exit 1
fi

echo "Memory management hardening completed successfully"

# Remediation script to suppress informational feedback during lockout/failure-counting process
# on RHEL 8 systems to prevent username enumeration attacks

set -e

# Colors for output
RED='\033[0;31m'
GREEN='\033[0;32m'
YELLOW='\033[1;33m'
NC='\033[0m' # No Color

log_info() {
    echo -e "${GREEN}[INFO]${NC} $1"
}

log_error() {
    echo -e "${RED}[ERROR]${NC} $1"
}

log_warning() {
    echo -e "${YELLOW}[WARNING]${NC} $1"
}

# Check if running as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
    log_error "This script must be run as root"
    exit 1
fi

# Check if system is RHEL 8
if ! grep -q "release 8" /etc/redhat-release 2>/dev/null; then
    log_warning "This script is designed for RHEL 8. Current system may not be RHEL 8."
fi

log_info "Starting configuration of failed-login lockout facility..."

log_info "Ensuring required PAM packages are installed..."
packages=("pam" "pam-devel" "pam_faillock" "cracklib" "cracklib-dicts")
for pkg in "${packages[@]}"; do
    if ! rpm -q "$pkg" &> /dev/null; then
        log_info "Installing package: $pkg"
        yum install -y "$pkg" > /dev/null 2>&1
    else
        log_info "Package already installed: $pkg"
    fi
done

# Step 2: Configure pam_faillock module with silent option
# This suppresses informational messages about lockouts
log_info "Configuring PAM faillock module with silent option..."

# Backup original PAM files
if [[ ! -f /etc/pam.d/system-auth.backup ]]; then
    cp /etc/pam.d/system-auth /etc/pam.d/system-auth.backup
    log_info "Created backup of /etc/pam.d/system-auth"
fi

if [[ ! -f /etc/pam.d/password-auth.backup ]]; then
    cp /etc/pam.d/password-auth /etc/pam.d/password-auth.backup
    log_info "Created backup of /etc/pam.d/password-auth"
fi

# Function to update PAM file with faillock configuration
update_pam_file() {
    local pam_file=$1

    # Create a new PAM file with proper configuration

#%PAM-1.0
# This file is auto-generated.
# User changes will be destroyed the next time authconfig is run.
auth        required      pam_env.so
auth        required      pam_faillock.so preauth silent audit deny=3 unlock_time=600
auth        [default=die] pam_faillock.so authfail audit deny=3 unlock_time=600
auth        sufficient    pam_unix.so nullok try_first_pass
auth        requisite     pam_deny.so
auth        required      pam_permit.so
auth        required      pam_faillock.so authsucc

account     required      pam_faillock.so
account     required      pam_unix.so
account     sufficient    pam_localuser.so
account     sufficient    pam_permit.so
account     required      pam_deny.so

password    requisite     pam_pwquality.so try_first_pass local_users_only retry=3 authtok_type=
password    sufficient    pam_unix.so sha512 shadow nullok try_first_pass use_authtok
password    required      pam_deny.so
password    required      pam_permit.so
password    required      pam_faillock.so unlock_time=600

session     optional      pam_keyinit.so revoke
session     required      pam_limits.so
-session     optional      pam_systemd.so
session     [success=1 default=ignore] pam_succeed_if.so service in crond quiet use_uid
session     required      pam_unix.so
session     optional      pam_faillock.so
EOF
    
    # Use authconfig if available to properly configure PAM
    if command -v authconfig &> /dev/null; then
        # authconfig approach for standard configuration
        authconfig --enablefaillock --faillockargs="preauth silent audit deny=3 unlock_time=600" --update > /dev/null 2>&1
        log_info "PAM configuration updated using authconfig"
    else
        # Manual update if authconfig is not available
        # This handles the system-auth and password-auth files
        if [[ -f "$pam_file" ]]; then
            # Check if faillock is already configured with silent option
            if grep -q "pam_faillock.so preauth silent" "$pam_file"; then
                log_info "Faillock already configured with silent option in $pam_file"
            else
                # Remove existing faillock lines if present
                sed -i '/pam_faillock.so/d' "$pam_file"
                
                # Add faillock with silent option at the beginning of auth section
                sed -i '/^auth.*pam_env.so/a auth        required      pam_faillock.so preauth silent audit deny=3 unlock_time=600' "$pam_file"
                sed -i '/^auth.*pam_unix.so/a auth        [default=die] pam_faillock.so authfail audit deny=3 unlock_time=600' "$pam_file"
                
                # Add faillock to session
                echo "session     optional      pam_faillock.so" >> "$pam_file"
                
                log_info "Updated $pam_file with silent faillock configuration"
            fi
        fi
    fi
    
    rm -f "$temp_file"
}

# Update both system-auth and password-auth

update_pam_file "/etc/pam.d/password-auth"

log_info "Configuring /etc/security/faillock.conf..."

    cat > "$faillock_conf" << 'EOF'
# /etc/security/faillock.conf - configuration file for pam_faillock module
# see 'man faillock.conf' for details.

# audit
# Enable additional audit logging of the failures.
audit

# silent
# Don't print informative messages to the user.
# This suppresses information about account lockout that could be used

silent

# fail_interval = n

fail_interval = 600

# unlock_time = n

unlock_time = 600

root_unlock_time = 900

# Step 4: Ensure faillock directory exists with proper permissions

if [[ ! -d "$faillock_dir" ]]; then
    mkdir -p "$faillock_dir"
    log_info "Created $faillock_dir"
fi

chown root:root "$faillock_dir"
