# audit
# Enable additional audit logging of the failures.
audit

# silent
# Don't print informative messages to the user.
# This suppresses information about account lockout that could be used
# for username enumeration attacks.
silent

# deny = n
# Deny access after n login failures.
deny = 3

# fail_interval = n
# The access will be reenabled after n seconds of inactivity after the last failed attempt.
fail_interval = 600

# unlock_time = n
# The access will be reenabled after n seconds after the lock was set.
unlock_time = 600

# root_unlock_time = n
# The access will be reenabled after n seconds after the lock was set if the user is root.
root_unlock_time = 900
EOF
    log_info "Created /etc/security/faillock.conf"
else
    log_info "faillock.conf already exists, updating configuration..."
    
    # Backup the original
    if [[ ! -f "${faillock_conf}.backup" ]]; then
        cp "$faillock_conf" "${faillock_conf}.backup"
    fi
    
    # Update or add configuration lines
    update_or_add_conf() {
        local key=$1
        local value=$2
        local file=$3
        
        if grep -q "^${key}" "$file"; then
            sed -i "s/^${key}.*/${key} = ${value}/" "$file"
        else
            if grep -q "^# ${key}" "$file"; then
                sed -i "s/^# ${key}.*/${key} = ${value}/" "$file"
            else
                echo "${key} = ${value}" >> "$file"
            fi
        fi
    }
    
    update_or_add_conf "audit" "" "$faillock_conf"
    update_or_add_conf "silent" "" "$faillock_conf"
    update_or_add_conf "deny" "3" "$faillock_conf"
    update_or_add_conf "fail_interval" "600" "$faillock_conf"
    update_or_add_conf "unlock_time" "600" "$faillock_conf"
fi

# Step 4: Ensure faillock directory exists with proper permissions
log_info "Ensuring faillock directory structure..."

faillock_dir="/var/run/faillock"
if [[ ! -d "$faillock_dir" ]]; then
    mkdir -p "$faillock_dir"
    log_info "Created $faillock_dir"
fi

# Ensure proper permissions
chmod 0770 "$faillock_dir"
chown root:root "$faillock_dir"

# Also ensure /var/log/faillock exists (for persistent storage)
faillog_dir="/var/log/faillock"
if [[ ! -d "$faillog_dir" ]]; then
    mkdir -p "$faillog_dir"
    log_info "Created $faillog_dir"
fi

chmod 0700 "$faillog_dir"
chown root:root "$faillog_dir"

# Step 5: Configure system limits for PAM
log_info "Configuring system limits..."

limits_file="/etc/security/limits.d/95-faillock-

#!/bin/bash

set -euo pipefail

# Disable and mask the systemd-coredump.socket unit to prevent core dump collection
systemctl disable systemd-coredump.socket 2>/dev/null || true
systemctl mask systemd-coredump.socket

# Disable and mask the systemd-coredump service if it exists
systemctl disable systemd-coredump.service 2>/dev/null || true
systemctl mask systemd-coredump.service

# Disable and mask the systemd-coredump.target if it exists  
systemctl disable systemd-coredump.target 2>/dev/null || true
systemctl mask systemd-coredump.target

# Stop any running core dump services
systemctl stop systemd-coredump.socket 2>/dev/null || true
systemctl stop systemd-coredump.service 2>/dev/null || true

# Verify the units are masked
systemctl status systemd-coredump.socket 2>&1 | grep -q "masked" || echo "Warning: systemd-coredump.socket may not be properly masked"
systemctl status systemd-coredump.service 2>&1 | grep -q "masked" || echo "Warning: systemd-coredump.service may not be properly masked"

# Configure kernel to disable core dumps
echo "kernel.core_pattern=|/bin/true" | tee /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-coredumps.conf > /dev/null
sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-coredumps.conf > /dev/null 2>&1 || true

# Disable core dumps for all users via limits.conf
echo "* soft core 0" | tee -a /etc/security/limits.conf > /dev/null
echo "* hard core 0" | tee -a /etc/security/limits.conf > /dev/null

# Verify core dump handling is disabled
if ! systemctl is-active --quiet systemd-coredump.socket && \
   ! systemctl is-active --quiet systemd-coredump.service; then
    echo "Core dump handling has been successfully disabled"
else
    echo "Warning: Some core dump services may still be active"
    exit 1
fi

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# SSH Client Responsiveness Timeout Configuration Script
# This script configures SSH server to probe connected clients for network responsiveness
# and automatically disconnects unresponsive clients after 600 seconds (10 minutes)

set -e

# Configuration parameters
SSH_CONFIG_FILE="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
BACKUP_SUFFIX=".backup.$(date +%s)"

# Function to check if a line exists and has the correct value
check_ssh_config_value() {
    local key="$1"
    local expected_value="$2"
    
    # Check if the key exists in the config file (not commented out)
    if grep -q "^${key} " "${SSH_CONFIG_FILE}"; then
        # Get the current value
        local current_value=$(grep "^${key} " "${SSH_CONFIG_FILE}" | awk '{print $NF}')
        if [ "${current_value}" = "${expected_value}" ]; then
            return 0  # Already configured correctly
        else
            return 1  # Needs to be updated
        fi
    else
        return 1  # Key not found, needs to be added
    fi
}

# Function to set SSH configuration value
set_ssh_config_value() {
    local key="$1"
    local value="$2"
    local description="$3"
    
    echo "Configuring SSH: ${description}"
    
    # Create backup of sshd_config if not already backed up in this session
    if [ ! -f "${SSH_CONFIG_FILE}${BACKUP_SUFFIX}" ]; then
        cp "${SSH_CONFIG_FILE}" "${SSH_CONFIG_FILE}${BACKUP_SUFFIX}"
        echo "Backed up original SSH config to ${SSH_CONFIG_FILE}${BACKUP_SUFFIX}"
    fi
    
    # Check if the key already exists
    if grep -q "^${key} " "${SSH_CONFIG_FILE}"; then
        # Key exists, update it
        sed -i "s/^${key} .*/${key} ${value}/" "${SSH_CONFIG_FILE}"
        echo "  Updated: ${key} ${value}"
    else
        # Key doesn't exist, add it
        echo "${key} ${value}" >> "${SSH_CONFIG_FILE}"
        echo "  Added: ${key} ${value}"
    fi
}

# Main execution
echo "Starting SSH client responsiveness timeout configuration..."

# ClientAliveInterval: Specifies the interval in seconds for sending keep-alive probes
# Setting to 300 seconds (5 minutes) - probes sent every 5 minutes
set_ssh_config_value "ClientAliveInterval" "300" "Client alive interval to 300 seconds (5 minutes)"

# ClientAliveCountMax: Number of client alive messages which may be sent without 
# receiving any messages back from the client. Setting to 2 means:
# After no response for (300 * 2) = 600 seconds = 10 minutes, connection is closed
set_ssh_config_value "ClientAliveCountMax" "2" "Client alive count max to 2 (total timeout: 10 minutes)"

# TCPKeepAlive: Use system TCP keep alives to check if connection is alive
# This provides additional layer of protection at TCP level
set_ssh_config_value "TCPKeepAlive" "yes" "TCP keep alive to yes"

# Verify the configuration syntax
echo ""
echo "Verifying SSH configuration syntax..."
if sshd -t -f "${SSH_CONFIG_FILE}" 2>&1; then
    echo "SSH configuration syntax is valid"
else
    echo "ERROR: SSH configuration syntax is invalid. Restoring backup..."
    cp "${SSH_CONFIG_FILE}${BACKUP_SUFFIX}" "${SSH_CONFIG_FILE}"
    exit 1
fi

# Display current configuration
echo ""
echo "Current SSH client responsiveness configuration:"
echo "=================================================="
grep -E "^(ClientAliveInterval|ClientAliveCountMax|TCPKeepAlive)" "${SSH_CONFIG_FILE}" || true

# Calculate and display total timeout
echo ""
echo "Timeout calculation:"
echo "===================="
echo "ClientAliveInterval: 300 seconds (5 minutes)"
echo "ClientAliveCountMax: 2"
echo "Total unresponsiveness timeout: 300 × 2 = 600 seconds (10 minutes)"

# Restart SSH service to apply changes
echo ""
echo "Applying configuration changes..."

# Check if we're in a container or systemd environment
if command -v systemctl &> /dev/null; then
    # Use systemctl to restart SSH service
    if systemctl is-enabled sshd &> /dev/null; then
        systemctl restart sshd
        echo "SSH service restarted successfully via systemctl"
    else
        echo "WARNING: sshd service is not enabled. Configuration saved but service not restarted."
        echo "To enable and start the service, run: systemctl enable --now sshd"
    fi
else
    # Fallback to service command if systemctl is not available
    if command -v service &> /dev/null; then
        service sshd restart
        echo "SSH service restarted successfully via service command"
    else
        echo "WARNING: Could not restart SSH service. Please restart manually."
    fi
fi

# Verify service is running
echo ""
echo "SSH Service Status:"
echo "==================="
if command -v systemctl &> /dev/null; then
    systemctl status sshd --no-pager | head -n 3 || true
else
    service sshd status || echo "Service status check skipped"
fi

echo ""
echo "Configuration completed successfully!"
echo "SSH will now disconnect unresponsive clients after 10 minutes of inactivity."
echo ""

# Ensure persistence across reboots by verifying systemd configuration
if command -v systemctl &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Verifying SSH service is enabled for auto-start..."
    if systemctl is-enabled sshd &> /dev/null; then
        echo "SSH service is enabled for auto-start"
    else
        echo "WARNING: SSH service is not enabled for auto-start."
        echo "Enable it with: systemctl enable sshd"
    fi
fi

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Bash script to configure chrony in client-only mode on RHEL 8
# This script ensures chrony operates strictly as a client and never opens listening ports

set -e

# Function to check if chrony is installed
check_chrony_installed() {
    if ! command -v chronyc &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Chrony is not installed. Installing chrony..."
        dnf install -y chrony
    fi
}

# Function to backup the original chrony configuration
backup_chrony_config() {
    local config_file="/etc/chrony.conf"
    local backup_file="${config_file}.backup.$(date +%s)"
    
    if [ -f "$config_file" ] && [ ! -f "${config_file}.backup" ]; then
        cp "$config_file" "$backup_file"
        echo "Backed up original configuration to $backup_file"
    fi
}

# Function to configure chrony for client-only mode
configure_client_only_mode() {
    local config_file="/etc/chrony.conf"
    local temp_file="/tmp/chrony_conf_temp_$$"
    
    # Create a new configuration file with client-only settings
    cat > "$temp_file" << 'EOF'
# Use NTP servers to synchronize time
# Default servers (can be modified as needed)
pool 0.rhel.pool.ntp.org iburst
pool 1.rhel.pool.ntp.org iburst
pool 2.rhel.pool.ntp.org iburst
pool 3.rhel.pool.ntp.org iburst

# Record the rate at which the system clock is gaining/losing time
driftfile /var/lib/chrony/drift

# Allow the system clock to be stepped in the beginning if adjustment is larger than 1 second
makestep 1.0 3

# Reduce the minimum number of selectable sources required to set the system clock from 3 to 1
minsources 1

# Disable server functionality - comment out or remove any port/allow directives
# This ensures chrony operates in client-only mode

# Disable the reference clock driver
# noclientlog

# Enhance security - restrict access to local system only
cmdallow 127.0.0.1
cmdallow ::1

# Log files location
logdir /var/log/chrony

# Specify the directory for the keyfile
keyfile /etc/chrony.keys

# Specify the directory for the leapsecond file
leapsecfile /usr/share/zoneinfo/leap-seconds.list

# Save NTS keys and cookies
ntsdumpdir /var/lib/chrony
EOF
    
    # Check if the file needs to be updated
    if ! diff -q "$config_file" "$temp_file" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
        cp "$temp_file" "$config_file"
        echo "Updated chrony configuration to client-only mode"
        return 0  # Changes were made
    else
        echo "Chrony is already configured in client-only mode"
        return 1  # No changes needed
    fi
    
    rm -f "$temp_file"
}

# Function to verify no server ports are open
verify_no_listening_ports() {
    # Check if chrony service is running
    if systemctl is-active --quiet chronyd; then
        # Check for listening ports on NTP (123) and any other potential chrony ports
        if ss -tulnp 2>/dev/null | grep -q "chronyd.*LISTEN"; then
            echo "Warning: Chrony appears to be listening on some ports"
            return 1
        else
            echo "Verified: Chrony is not listening on any ports"
            return 0
        fi
    fi
}

# Function to enable and start chrony service
enable_chrony_service() {
    systemctl daemon-reload
    
    if ! systemctl is-enabled chronyd &> /dev/null; then
        systemctl enable chronyd
        echo "Enabled chronyd service to start on boot"
    fi
    
    if ! systemctl is-active --quiet chronyd; then
        systemctl start chronyd
        echo "Started chronyd service"
    else
        # Restart to apply new configuration
        systemctl restart chronyd
        echo "Restarted chronyd service with new configuration"
    fi
}

# Function to verify the configuration
verify_configuration() {
    echo "Verifying chrony client-only configuration..."
    
    # Check that no server directives are active
    if grep -q "^[[:space:]]*port" /etc/chrony.conf 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "Warning: Found port directive in chrony configuration"
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Check that no allow directives are present (except for local)
    if grep -q "^[[:space:]]*allow[[:space:]]*[^:]" /etc/chrony.conf 2>/dev/null | grep -v "127.0.0.1\|::1"; then
        echo "Warning: Found non-local allow directive in chrony configuration"
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Verify chrony is running
    if systemctl is-active --quiet chronyd; then
        echo "Chrony daemon is running"
    else
        echo "Error: Chrony daemon is not running"
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Wait for chrony to sync if it's just started
    sleep 2
    
    # Check synchronization status
    if command -v chronyc &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Chrony synchronization status:"
        chronyc tracking 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
    
    echo "Configuration verification complete"
    return 0
}

# Main script execution
main() {
    echo "Starting chrony client-only mode configuration for RHEL 8..."
    
    # Step 1: Check if chrony is installed
    check_chrony_installed
    
    # Step 2: Backup original configuration
    backup_chrony_config
    
    # Step 3: Configure chrony for client-only mode
    if configure_client_only_mode; then
        # Step 4: Enable and start the service
        enable_chrony_service
        
        # Step 5: Wait for service to stabilize
        sleep 3
    else
        echo "No configuration changes needed, but ensuring service is enabled..."
        enable_chrony_service
    fi
    
    # Step 6: Verify no listening ports
    verify_no_listening_ports
    
    # Step 7: Final verification
    verify_configuration
    
    echo "Chrony client-only mode configuration complete!"
    echo "The configuration will persist across reboots."
}

# Execute main function
main

    # Check that no allow directives are present (except for local)
    if grep -q "^[[:space:]]*allow[[:space:]]*[^:]" /etc/chrony.conf 2>/dev/null | grep -v "127.0.0.1\|::1"; then
        echo "Warning: Found non-local allow directive in chrony configuration"
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Verify chrony is running
    if systemctl is-active --quiet chronyd; then
        echo "Chrony daemon is running"
    else
        echo "Error: Chrony daemon is not running"
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Wait for chrony to sync if it's just started
    sleep 2
    
    # Check synchronization status
    if command -v chronyc &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Chrony synchronization status:"
        chronyc tracking 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
    
    echo "Configuration verification complete"
    return 0
}

# Main script execution
main() {
    echo "Starting chrony client-only mode configuration for RHEL 8..."
    
    # Step 1: Check if chrony is installed
    check_chrony_installed
    
    # Step 2: Backup original configuration
    backup_chrony_config
    
    # Step 3: Configure chrony for client-only mode
    if configure_client_only_mode; then
        # Step 4: Enable and start the service
        enable_chrony_service
        
        # Step 5: Wait for service to stabilize
        sleep 3
    else
        echo "No configuration changes needed, but ensuring service is enabled..."
        enable_chrony_service
    fi
    
    # Step 6: Verify no listening ports
    verify_no_listening_ports
    
    # Step 7: Final verification
    verify_configuration
    
    echo "Chrony client-only mode configuration complete!"
    echo "The configuration will persist across reboots."
}

# Execute main function
main

#!/bin/bash

# Script to ensure time synchronization on RHEL 8 with bounded polling intervals
# This script configures chrony (the default time daemon on RHEL 8) to ensure
# all remote time sources have explicit maximum polling intervals defined

set -e

# Check if running on RHEL 8
if ! grep -q "release 8" /etc/redhat-release 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Warning: This script is designed for RHEL 8" >&2
fi

# Ensure chrony is installed
if ! command -v chronyc &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Installing chrony..."
    yum install -y chrony
fi

# Ensure chrony is enabled and started
systemctl enable chronyd
systemctl start chronyd

# Path to chrony configuration
CHRONY_CONF="/etc/chrony.conf"

# Create a backup of the original configuration
if [ ! -f "${CHRONY_CONF}.backup" ]; then
    cp "${CHRONY_CONF}" "${CHRONY_CONF}.backup"
fi

# Read the current configuration
CONFIG_CONTENT=$(cat "${CHRONY_CONF}")

# Function to check if a line already has maxpoll defined
has_maxpoll() {
    echo "$1" | grep -q "maxpoll"
}

# Function to add maxpoll to a server/pool line if not already present
add_maxpoll_if_needed() {
    local line="$1"
    
    # Skip lines that are comments or don't have server/pool directives
    if [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*# ]] || ! [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*(server|pool)[[:space:]] ]]; then
        echo "$line"
        return
    fi
    
    # If maxpoll is already defined, return as-is
    if has_maxpoll "$line"; then
        echo "$line"
        return
    fi
    
    # Add maxpoll 10 (1024 seconds = ~17 minutes maximum polling interval)
    # This ensures bounded polling interval as required
    echo "$line maxpoll 10"
}

# Process the configuration file
NEW_CONFIG=""
while IFS= read -r line || [ -n "$line" ]; do
    NEW_CONFIG+="$(add_maxpoll_if_needed "$line")"$'\n'
done <<< "$CONFIG_CONTENT"

# Remove the trailing newline added by the while loop
NEW_CONFIG="${NEW_CONFIG%$'\n'}"

# Write the updated configuration back
echo "$NEW_CONFIG" > "${CHRONY_CONF}"

# Verify the syntax of the configuration
if ! chronyc -c 'quit' &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Error: Invalid chrony configuration" >&2
    # Restore from backup
    cp "${CHRONY_CONF}.backup" "${CHRONY_CONF}"
    exit 1
fi

# Reload chrony configuration
systemctl reload chronyd

# Wait a moment for the service to process the reload
sleep 2

# Verify that sources are configured and polling
echo "Current NTP sources configuration:"
chronyc sources || true

# Verify that maxpoll settings are applied
echo "Verifying maxpoll settings in configuration:"
grep -E "^(server|pool)" "${CHRONY_CONF}" | grep -E "maxpoll|iburst" || echo "Checking for maxpoll settings..."

# Additional configuration to ensure bounded intervals
# Set minimum and maximum polling intervals at the daemon level
if ! grep -q "minsources" "${CHRONY_CONF}"; then
    echo "minsources 1" >> "${CHRONY_CONF}"
fi

if ! grep -q "maxdrift" "${CHRONY_CONF}"; then
    echo "maxdrift 1000" >> "${CHRONY_CONF}"
fi

# Ensure the configuration includes leapsectz for timezone/leap second handling
if ! grep -q "leapsectz" "${CHRONY_CONF}"; then
    echo "leapsectz right/UTC" >> "${CHRONY_CONF}"
fi

# Final reload to apply any additional settings
systemctl reload chronyd

echo "NTP time synchronization configuration completed successfully"
echo "All configured time sources now have bounded maximum polling intervals"
echo "Configuration persists across system reboots"

#!/bin/bash
set -e

# Ensure audit daemon is installed and running
if ! command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    dnf install -y audit
fi

# Start and enable audit daemon
systemctl enable auditd
systemctl start auditd

# Find the postdrop binary path
POSTDROP_PATH=$(which postdrop 2>/dev/null || find /usr -name postdrop -type f 2>/dev/null | head -1)

if [ -z "$POSTDROP_PATH" ]; then
    echo "Warning: postdrop binary not found. Installing postfix..."
    dnf install -y postfix
    POSTDROP_PATH=$(which postdrop)
fi

# Get the inode number of postdrop
POSTDROP_INODE=$(stat -c %i "$POSTDROP_PATH")

# Path to audit rules file
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/privileged.rules"

# Ensure the directory exists
mkdir -p "$(dirname "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE")"

# Check if rule already exists with correct parameters
RULE_PATTERN="path=$POSTDROP_PATH.*uid>=1000.*key=privileged-postdrop"

if ! auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -q "postdrop"; then
    # Remove any existing postdrop rules to avoid duplicates
    if grep -q "postdrop" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
        sed -i '/postdrop/d' "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    fi
    
    # Add the audit rule to the rules file for persistence
    echo "# Audit postdrop executions by non-system users" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    echo "-a always,exit -F path=$POSTDROP_PATH -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k privileged-postdrop" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    
    # Load the rule immediately
    auditctl -a always,exit -F path="$POSTDROP_PATH" -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k privileged-postdrop
fi

# Verify rule was loaded
if auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -q "postdrop"; then
    echo "Audit rule for postdrop successfully configured"
else
    echo "Error: Failed to load audit rule for postdrop"
    exit 1
fi

# Make audit rules immutable to prevent tampering (optional but recommended)
if ! grep -q "^-e 2" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
    echo "-e 2" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
fi

# Reload audit rules to apply any changes
auditctl -R "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null || true

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent script to enforce sudo authentication with user's own password on RHEL 8

set -e

# Define the sudoers configuration directory
SUDOERS_DIR="/etc/sudoers.d"
CONFIG_FILE="${SUDOERS_DIR}/sudo_auth_policy"

# Ensure the sudoers directory exists
if [[ ! -d "$SUDOERS_DIR" ]]; then
    mkdir -p "$SUDOERS_DIR"
    chmod 755 "$SUDOERS_DIR"
fi

# Create the desired sudoers configuration
# This configuration ensures:
# - Disable root password authentication (NOPASSWD, PASSWD defaults)
# - Disable target user password authentication
# - Disable run-as user password authentication
# - Enable only invoking user password authentication

cat > "${CONFIG_FILE}.tmp" << 'EOF'
# Sudo authentication policy - require user's own password
# This configuration enforces that sudo requires the invoking user's password

# Disable authenticate as root user
Defaults authenticate, !rootpw

# Disable authenticate as target/run-as user
Defaults !targetpw, !runaspw

# Ensure password timeout is set appropriately (5 minutes default)
Defaults passwd_timeout=5

# Log authentication attempts
Defaults use_pty, log_host, log_year, logfile="/var/log/sudo.log"

# Require password for all users in sudoers
Defaults env_reset, secure_path="/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin"
EOF

# Check if the file already exists and has the same content
if [[ -f "$CONFIG_FILE" ]]; then
    if diff -q "${CONFIG_FILE}.tmp" "$CONFIG_FILE" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
        rm -f "${CONFIG_FILE}.tmp"
        echo "Sudo authentication policy is already correctly configured."
        exit 0
    fi
fi

# Validate the sudoers file syntax
visudo -c -f "${CONFIG_FILE}.tmp" > /dev/null 2>&1
if [[ $? -ne 0 ]]; then
    echo "ERROR: Invalid sudoers configuration syntax"
    rm -f "${CONFIG_FILE}.tmp"
    exit 1
fi

# Install the new configuration
mv "${CONFIG_FILE}.tmp" "$CONFIG_FILE"
chmod 440 "$CONFIG_FILE"

# Verify the configuration was applied
visudo -c -f "$CONFIG_FILE" > /dev/null 2>&1
if [[ $? -eq 0 ]]; then
    echo "Sudo authentication policy has been successfully configured."
    echo "Users must now authenticate with their own password for sudo commands."
else
    echo "ERROR: Failed to apply sudo authentication policy"
    exit 1
fi

# Test that the configuration is working by checking sudo defaults
sudo -l -n 2>/dev/null | grep -q "authenticate" || true

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

set -euo pipefail

# Define the maximum password age value
MAX_DAYS=60
LOGIN_DEFS_FILE="/etc/login.defs"

# Function to update or add a parameter in login.defs
update_login_defs() {
    local param="$1"
    local value="$2"
    local file="$3"
    
    # Create a temporary file
    local temp_file="${file}.tmp.$$"
    
    # Check if the parameter exists
    if grep -q "^${param}" "$file"; then
        # Update existing parameter
        sed "s/^${param}[[:space:]].*/^${param}\t${value}/" "$file" > "$temp_file"
    else
        # Add new parameter if it doesn't exist
        echo "${param}	${value}" >> "$temp_file"
        cat "$file" >> "$temp_file"
    fi
    
    # Move the temporary file to the original location
    mv "$temp_file" "$file"
}

# Check if running as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Backup the original login.defs file
if [[ ! -f "${LOGIN_DEFS_FILE}.backup" ]]; then
    cp "$LOGIN_DEFS_FILE" "${LOGIN_DEFS_FILE}.backup"
fi

# Create a temporary file for modifications
temp_file="${LOGIN_DEFS_FILE}.tmp.$$"
cp "$LOGIN_DEFS_FILE" "$temp_file"

# Update or add PASS_MAX_DAYS
if grep -q "^PASS_MAX_DAYS" "$temp_file"; then
    sed -i "s/^PASS_MAX_DAYS.*/PASS_MAX_DAYS   ${MAX_DAYS}/" "$temp_file"
else
    # Add after PASS_MIN_DAYS if it exists, otherwise add at the end
    if grep -q "^PASS_MIN_DAYS" "$temp_file"; then
        sed -i "/^PASS_MIN_DAYS/a PASS_MAX_DAYS   ${MAX_DAYS}" "$temp_file"
    else
        echo "PASS_MAX_DAYS   ${MAX_DAYS}" >> "$temp_file"
    fi
fi

# Move the temporary file to replace the original
mv "$temp_file" "$LOGIN_DEFS_FILE"

# Verify the change was applied
if grep -q "^PASS_MAX_DAYS.*${MAX_DAYS}" "$LOGIN_DEFS_FILE"; then
    echo "Successfully configured PASS_MAX_DAYS to ${MAX_DAYS} days in ${LOGIN_DEFS_FILE}"
else
    echo "Failed to configure PASS_MAX_DAYS" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Apply the setting to existing users using chage command
# Get all users with UIDs >= 1000 (regular users, excluding system accounts)
for username in $(awk -F: '$3 >= 1000 {print $1}' /etc/passwd); do
    if [[ -n "$username" ]]; then
        chage -M "$MAX_DAYS" "$username" 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
done

echo "System-wide password policy configured successfully"
echo "New user accounts will have a maximum password age of ${MAX_DAYS} days"

# Variables
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/chcon_audit.rules"
CHCON_PATH="/usr/bin/chcon"
RULE_DESCRIPTION="Monitor chcon command execution"

# Function to create the audit rule content
create_audit_rule_content() {
    cat << 'EOF'
# Monitor chcon command execution for regular users (UID >= 1000) and logged-in accounts
# This rule captures SELinux file-context changing utility usage as privileged-command usage

-a always,exit -F path=/usr/bin/chcon -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k chcon_execution
-a always,exit -F path=/usr/bin/chcon -F perm=x -F auid!=-1 -F uid!=0 -k chcon_execution_logged_in
EOF
}

# Function to verify chcon path and update rule if necessary
verify_and_update_chcon_path() {
    local actual_path=$(which chcon 2>/dev/null || echo "/usr/bin/chcon")
    if [ "$actual_path" != "/usr/bin/chcon" ]; then

if [ ! -d "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR" ]; then

    chmod 755 "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR"

NEEDS_UPDATE=false

if [ ! -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
    NEEDS_UPDATE=true
else
    # Check if the rule file contains both required rules
    if ! grep -q "F path=/usr/bin/chcon" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
        NEEDS_UPDATE=true
    fi
    if ! grep -q "F auid>=1000" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then

    fi

if [ "$NEEDS_UPDATE" = true ]; then

    chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    verify_and_update_chcon_path
fi

if command -v augenrules &> /dev/null; then
    # Using augenrules
    augenrules --load > /dev/null 2>&1 || true

        auditctl -a always,exit -F path=/usr/bin/chcon -F perm=x -F auid!=-1 -F uid!=0 -k chcon_execution_logged_in 2>/dev/null || true
    fi

# Ensure audit daemon is enabled and running

#!/bin/bash

set -euo pipefail

# RHEL 8 Audit System Configuration Script
# Makes login UIDs immutable to prevent tampering with audit logs

AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules"
AUDIT_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
IMMUTABLE_RULE="--loginuid-immutable"

# Ensure audit rules directory exists
if [ ! -d "$AUDIT_CONFIG_DIR" ]; then
    mkdir -p "$AUDIT_CONFIG_DIR"
fi

# Check if immutable loginuid rule already exists
if grep -q "loginuid-immutable" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Login UID immutability rule already configured."
    exit 0
fi

# Create a backup of the current audit rules if it exists
if [ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
    cp "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}.bak.$(date +%s)"
fi

# Ensure the audit rules file exists
touch "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

# Add the loginuid-immutable rule to the audit rules file
# This rule must be added at the beginning of the rules file or right after initial rules
{
    # Read existing content if file is not empty
    if [ -s "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
        cat "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    fi
} > "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}.tmp"

# Check if we need to add the rule
if ! grep -q "loginuid-immutable" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}.tmp" 2>/dev/null; then
    # Add the immutable loginuid rule
    {
        echo "# Make login UIDs immutable to prevent tampering with audit logs"
        echo "--loginuid-immutable"
        cat "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}.tmp"
    } > "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
fi

# Clean up temporary file
rm -f "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}.tmp"

# Set proper permissions on the audit rules file
chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
chown root:root "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

# Check if auditd service is installed
if command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    # Load the rules immediately if auditd is running
    if systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
        # Apply the loginuid-immutable rule immediately
        auditctl --loginuid-immutable 2>/dev/null || true
        
        # Restart auditd to load all rules from the configuration file
        systemctl restart auditd
        echo "Audit daemon restarted with new rules loaded."
    fi
fi

# Enable auditd service to start on boot
systemctl enable auditd 2>/dev/null || true

# Verify the setting
if auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -q "loginuid-immutable"; then
    echo "Successfully configured login UID immutability."
    exit 0
else
    echo "Attempting to load rules from configuration..."
    # Try to load rules from the configuration file
    auditctl -R "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" 2>/dev/null || true
    
    # Final verification
    if auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -q "loginuid-immutable"; then
        echo "Successfully configured login UID immutability."
        exit 0
    else
        echo "Warning: Rules may require auditd restart to take effect."
        exit 0
    fi
fi

#!/bin/bash

# SSH Server Configuration Script for RHEL 8
# Ensures root cannot authenticate directly over SSH

set -e

# Define SSH configuration file
SSH_CONFIG_FILE="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
SSH_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"
SSH_DROP_IN_FILE="${SSH_CONFIG_DIR}/01-disable-root-login.conf"

# Function to check if SSH daemon is running
check_ssh_service() {
    systemctl is-active --quiet sshd
    return $?
}

# Function to validate SSH configuration syntax
validate_ssh_config() {
    sshd -t -f "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE" 2>/dev/null
    return $?
}

# Main configuration
main() {
    echo "Starting SSH root login restriction configuration..."
    
    # Create the drop-in configuration directory if it doesn't exist
    if [ ! -d "$SSH_CONFIG_DIR" ]; then
        echo "Creating SSH configuration drop-in directory..."
        mkdir -p "$SSH_CONFIG_DIR"
        chmod 755 "$SSH_CONFIG_DIR"
    fi
    
    # Create or update the drop-in configuration file
    echo "Creating drop-in SSH configuration file..."
    cat > "$SSH_DROP_IN_FILE" << 'EOF'
# This file is managed by system configuration
# It disables direct root login over SSH for security

PermitRootLogin no
EOF
    
    # Set proper permissions on the drop-in file
    chmod 644 "$SSH_DROP_IN_FILE"
    
    # Validate SSH configuration
    echo "Validating SSH configuration syntax..."
    if ! validate_ssh_config; then
        echo "ERROR: SSH configuration validation failed!"
        rm -f "$SSH_DROP_IN_FILE"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    # Check if SSH service is running and restart it
    if check_ssh_service; then
        echo "Restarting SSH service to apply changes..."
        systemctl restart sshd
        
        # Verify SSH service is still running after restart
        if check_ssh_service; then
            echo "SSH service restarted successfully"
        else
            echo "ERROR: SSH service failed to restart!"
            exit 1
        fi
    else
        echo "SSH service is not running, enabling it for boot..."
        systemctl enable sshd
        systemctl start sshd
    fi
    
    # Verify the configuration was applied
    echo "Verifying configuration..."
    if grep -q "^PermitRootLogin no" "$SSH_DROP_IN_FILE"; then
        echo "SUCCESS: Root SSH login has been disabled"
        echo "Configuration file: $SSH_DROP_IN_FILE"
        echo "Change will persist across reboots"
    else
        echo "ERROR: Failed to apply configuration"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    # Display current root login status
    echo ""
    echo "Current root login SSH status:"
    sshd -T 2>/dev/null | grep -i "permitrootlogin" || echo "PermitRootLogin: no (default)"
}

# Run main function
main

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Bash script to configure audit rules for file permission modification attempts
# by regular users on RHEL 8 systems

set -e

# Determine the audit rules directory
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/permissions_modification.rules"

# Ensure the audit rules directory exists
if [ ! -d "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR" ]; then
    mkdir -p "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR"
fi

# Define the audit rules for fchmodat system calls (both 32-bit and 64-bit)
# These rules capture attempts by regular users (UID >= 1000) to change file permissions
AUDIT_RULES="
# Audit file permission modifications via fchmodat for regular users
# This captures attempts to alter file access control permissions using fchmodat
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S fchmodat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F key=file_perm_modifications
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fchmodat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F key=file_perm_modifications
"

# Check if audit rules file exists and has the correct content
if [ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
    # Check if the rules are already present
    if grep -q "file_perm_modifications" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
        echo "Audit rules for file permission modifications already configured."
    else
        # Append the rules to the existing file
        echo "$AUDIT_RULES" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
        echo "Added audit rules for file permission modifications to $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    fi
else
    # Create the audit rules file with the rules
    cat > "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" << 'EOF'
# Audit file permission modifications via fchmodat for regular users
# This captures attempts to alter file access control permissions using fchmodat
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S fchmodat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F key=file_perm_modifications
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fchmodat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F key=file_perm_modifications
EOF
    echo "Created new audit rules file at $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
fi

# Check if auditctl is available and load the rules
if command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Loading audit rules via auditctl..."
    
    # Load the rules directly into the kernel
    auditctl -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S fchmodat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F key=file_perm_modifications 2>/dev/null || true
    auditctl -a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fchmodat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F key=file_perm_modifications 2>/dev/null || true
    
    echo "Audit rules loaded via auditctl"
fi

# Check if augenrules is available and compile the rules
if command -v augenrules &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Compiling audit rules via augenrules..."
    
    # Compile the rules
    augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || true
    
    echo "Audit rules compiled via augenrules"
fi

# Enable and start the audit daemon to ensure rules persist across reboots
if command -v systemctl &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Enabling and starting audit daemon..."
    
    # Enable the audit daemon to start on boot
    systemctl enable auditd 2>/dev/null || true
    
    # Start the audit daemon if not already running
    systemctl start auditd 2>/dev/null || true
    
    echo "Audit daemon configured to start on boot"
fi

# Verify the audit rules are loaded
if command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Verifying audit rules..."
    auditctl -l | grep -i "file_perm_modifications" || echo "Warning: Rules may not be fully loaded yet"
fi

echo "Audit configuration for file permission modifications completed successfully."

-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fchmodat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F key=file_perm_modifications
EOF
    echo "Created new audit rules file at $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
fi

# Check if auditctl is available and load the rules
if command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Loading audit rules via auditctl..."
    
    # Load the rules directly into the kernel
    auditctl -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S fchmodat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F key=file_perm_modifications 2>/dev/null || true
    auditctl -a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fchmodat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F key=file_perm_modifications 2>/dev/null || true
    
    echo "Audit rules loaded via auditctl"
fi

# Check if augenrules is available and compile the rules
if command -v augenrules &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Compiling audit rules via augenrules..."
    
    # Compile the rules
    augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || true
    
    echo "Audit rules compiled via augenrules"
fi

# Enable and start the audit daemon to ensure rules persist across reboots
if command -v systemctl &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Enabling and starting audit daemon..."
    
    # Enable the audit daemon to start on boot
    systemctl enable auditd 2>/dev/null || true
    
    # Start the audit daemon if not already running
    systemctl start auditd 2>/dev/null || true
    
    echo "Audit daemon configured to start on boot"
fi

# Verify the audit rules are loaded
if command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Verifying audit rules..."
    auditctl -l | grep -i "file_perm_modifications" || echo "Warning: Rules may not be fully loaded yet"
fi

echo "Audit configuration for file permission modifications completed successfully."

#!/bin/bash

set -euo pipefail

# RHEL 8 SSH X11 Forwarding Loopback-Only Configuration
# This script ensures X11 forwarding proxy display binds only to loopback interface

SSHD_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
SSHD_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"
SSHD_X11_CONFIG="${SSHD_CONFIG_DIR}/x11-loopback.conf"

# Function to check if a line exists and is not commented
check_config_exists() {
    local file=$1
    local pattern=$2
    
    if [[ ! -f "$file" ]]; then
        return 1
    fi
    
    grep -q "^[[:space:]]*${pattern}[[:space:]]*" "$file" && return 0 || return 1
}

# Function to check if X11UseLocalhost is properly set
check_x11_localhost_setting() {
    local file=$1
    
    # Check if X11UseLocalhost yes is set (this is what we want)
    if grep -q "^[[:space:]]*X11UseLocalhost[[:space:]]\+yes" "$file"; then
        return 0
    fi
    
    return 1
}

# Function to enable SSH X11 forwarding with loopback binding
configure_x11_loopback() {
    # Ensure the SSH config directory exists
    if [[ ! -d "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR" ]]; then
        mkdir -p "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR"
        chmod 755 "$SSHD_CONFIG_DIR"
    fi
    
    # Create or update the X11 loopback configuration file
    cat > "$SSHD_X11_CONFIG" << 'EOF'
# X11 Forwarding Configuration - Loopback-Only Binding
# This configuration ensures X11 forwarding proxy display
# binds only to the loopback interface (127.0.0.1)

# Enable X11 forwarding
X11Forwarding yes

# Bind X11 forwarding proxy to localhost only
# This prevents remote hosts from accessing forwarded X11 sessions
X11UseLocalhost yes

# Use X11 display number offset to prevent conflicts
X11DisplayOffset 10
EOF

    chmod 644 "$SSHD_X11_CONFIG"
    
    # Verify the main sshd_config doesn't have conflicting settings
    # Create a temporary backup
    cp "$SSHD_CONFIG" "${SSHD_CONFIG}.backup.$(date +%s)"
    
    # Remove any commented or uncommented X11Forwarding lines from main config
    # to avoid conflicts with included configuration
    sed -i.bak '/^[[:space:]]*#*[[:space:]]*X11Forwarding/d' "$SSHD_CONFIG"
    sed -i.bak '/^[[:space:]]*#*[[:space:]]*X11UseLocalhost/d' "$SSHD_CONFIG"
    sed -i.bak '/^[[:space:]]*#*[[:space:]]*X11DisplayOffset/d' "$SSHD_CONFIG"
    
    # Ensure Include directive is present if not already
    if ! grep -q "^Include[[:space:]]\+${SSHD_CONFIG_DIR}/\*" "$SSHD_CONFIG"; then
        # Add Include directive at the end of the file
        echo "" >> "$SSHD_CONFIG"
        echo "# Include custom SSH configurations" >> "$SSHD_CONFIG"
        echo "Include ${SSHD_CONFIG_DIR}/*.conf" >> "$SSHD_CONFIG"
    fi
}

# Function to verify SSH configuration
verify_ssh_config() {
    # Test SSH configuration syntax
    if ! sshd -t 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "ERROR: SSH configuration syntax error detected"
        # Restore from backup if available
        for backup in "${SSHD_CONFIG}.backup."*; do
            if [[ -f "$backup" ]]; then
                echo "Restoring from backup: $backup"
                cp "$backup" "$SSHD_CONFIG"
                break
            fi
        done
        return 1
    fi
    
    return 0
}

# Function to restart SSH service
restart_ssh_service() {
    if systemctl is-active --quiet sshd; then
        systemctl restart sshd
        echo "SSH service restarted successfully"
    else
        echo "SSH service is not running, starting it..."
        systemctl start sshd
    fi
}

# Function to verify the configuration is in effect
verify_configuration() {
    local config_file=$1
    
    if [[ ! -f "$config_file" ]]; then
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Check that X11Forwarding is enabled
    if ! grep -q "^[[:space:]]*X11Forwarding[[:space:]]\+yes" "$config_file"; then
        echo "WARNING: X11Forwarding not properly configured"
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Check that X11UseLocalhost is set to yes
    if ! grep -q "^[[:space:]]*X11UseLocalhost[[:space:]]\+yes" "$config_file"; then
        echo "WARNING: X11UseLocalhost not set to yes"
        return 1
    fi
    
    return 0
}

# Main execution
main() {
    echo "Configuring SSH X11 forwarding for loopback-only binding..."
    
    # Configure X11 loopback settings
    configure_x11_loopback
    
    # Verify SSH configuration syntax
    if ! verify_ssh_config; then
        echo "ERROR: SSH configuration verification failed"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    # Verify the configuration file exists and is correct
    if ! verify_configuration "$SSHD_X11_CONFIG"; then
        echo "ERROR: X11 configuration verification failed"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    # Restart SSH service to apply changes
    restart_ssh_service
    
    # Final verification
    echo "Configuration complete. Verifying..."
    if verify_configuration "$SSHD_X11_CONFIG"; then
        echo "SUCCESS: SSH X11 forwarding is configured to use loopback-only binding"
        echo "X11 forwarding proxy display will bind only to 127.0.0.1"
        exit 0
    else
        echo "ERROR: Configuration verification failed"
        exit 1
    fi
}

# Run main function
main

# Script to configure auditd rules for privilege escalation detection on RHEL 8

# Configuration file for audit rules
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/privilege_escalation.rules"
AUDITD_CONF="/etc/audit/audit.rules"

# Ensure auditd package is installed
if ! rpm -q audit > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    dnf install -y audit audit-libs
fi

# Create the audit rules file for privilege escalation detection
mkdir -p "$(dirname "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE")"

# Define the rules for detecting privilege escalation
# These rules monitor for execve syscalls where euid != uid or egid != gid
cat > "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" << 'EOF'
# Audit privilege escalation via setuid/setgid execution
# Monitor execve syscalls where effective UID differs from real UID (32-bit)
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S execve -F uid!=euid -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k privilege_escalation_uid_32

# Monitor execve syscalls where effective UID differs from real UID (64-bit)
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S execve -F uid!=euid -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k privilege_escalation_uid_64

# Monitor execve syscalls where effective GID differs from real GID (32-bit)
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S execve -F gid!=egid -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k privilege_escalation_gid_32

# Monitor execve syscalls where effective GID differs from real GID (64-bit)
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S execve -F gid!=egid -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k privilege_escalation_gid_64

# Make configuration immutable
-e 2
EOF

# Check if the audit rules file content is already correctly configured
# This ensures idempotency
if grep -q "privilege_escalation_uid_32" "$AUDITD_CONF" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Audit rules for privilege escalation already configured"
else
    echo "Configuring audit rules for privilege escalation detection"
    
    # Load the new rules
    augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || true
    
    # Restart auditd to apply the rules
    systemctl restart auditd || systemctl start auditd
fi

# Enable auditd service to start on boot
systemctl enable auditd

# Verify the rules are loaded
if auditctl -l 2>/dev/null | grep -q "privilege_escalation"; then
    echo "Audit rules for privilege escalation have been successfully configured"
    exit 0
else
    echo "Warning: Audit rules may not be fully loaded, but configuration file has been created"
    echo "Rules will be applied after system restart"
    exit 0
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent remediation script to enable extended attribute checking in AIDE on RHEL 8

set -e

# Configuration
AIDE_CONFIG_FILE="/etc/aide.conf"
AIDE_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/aide.conf.d"
AIDE_XA_FLAG="xattrs"

# Backup original configuration
if [ -f "$AIDE_CONFIG_FILE" ]; then
    if [ ! -f "${AIDE_CONFIG_FILE}.bak" ]; then
        cp "$AIDE_CONFIG_FILE" "${AIDE_CONFIG_FILE}.bak"
    fi
fi

# Function to check if extended attributes flag is already present in a line
has_xa_flag() {
    local line="$1"
    # Check if the line contains xattrs flag
    [[ "$line" =~ x(attrs)?[^a-zA-Z0-9]* ]] || [[ "$line" =~ [^a-zA-Z0-9]*x(attrs)? ]]
}

# Function to add extended attributes flag to a ruleset definition
add_xa_to_ruleset() {
    local line="$1"
    
    # Check if line is a ruleset definition (contains + or -)
    if [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*(NORMAL|STRICT|LSPP|CUSTOM|[A-Z_]+)[[:space:]]*= ]] || [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*\+ ]]; then
        # Check if xattrs is already present
        if ! has_xa_flag "$line"; then
            # Add xattrs flag after the equals sign or at the end
            if [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*(NORMAL|STRICT|LSPP|CUSTOM|[A-Z_]+)[[:space:]]*= ]]; then
                # For variable definitions, append +xattrs
                echo "${line} +xattrs"
            else
                # For rules starting with +, append xattrs
                echo "${line} +xattrs"
            fi
        else
            echo "$line"
        fi
    else
        echo "$line"
    fi
}

# Process main AIDE configuration file
if [ -f "$AIDE_CONFIG_FILE" ]; then
    # Create a temporary file
    TEMP_FILE=$(mktemp)
    
    # Read the configuration file and process it
    while IFS= read -r line || [ -n "$line" ]; do
        # Skip empty lines and comments
        if [ -z "$line" ] || [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*# ]]; then
            echo "$line" >> "$TEMP_FILE"
        else
            # Check if this is a ruleset definition or rule
            if [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*(NORMAL|STRICT|LSPP|CUSTOM|[A-Z_]+)[[:space:]]*= ]] || [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*(\+|-)[^/] ]]; then
                # This is a variable definition or rule
                if ! has_xa_flag "$line"; then
                    # Need to add xattrs
                    # Append +xattrs to the line
                    echo "${line} +xattrs" >> "$TEMP_FILE"
                else
                    echo "$line" >> "$TEMP_FILE"
                fi
            else
                echo "$line" >> "$TEMP_FILE"
            fi
        fi
    done < "$AIDE_CONFIG_FILE"
    
    # Replace original file with modified version
    mv "$TEMP_FILE" "$AIDE_CONFIG_FILE"
fi

if [ -d "$AIDE_CONFIG_DIR" ]; then
    for config_file in "$AIDE_CONFIG_DIR"/*.conf; do

            if [ ! -f "${config_file}.bak" ]; then

            # Create a temporary file

    fi

    # Check if the line contains xattrs flag

#!/bin/bash

set -e

# Disable the debug-shell service to prevent unauthenticated root access
systemctl disable debug-shell.service 2>/dev/null || true
systemctl stop debug-shell.service 2>/dev/null || true

# Mask the service to prevent it from being started even by other means
systemctl mask debug-shell.service

# Verify the service is masked and disabled
if systemctl is-enabled debug-shell.service &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Error: Failed to disable debug-shell service"
    exit 1
fi

if ! systemctl is-active --quiet debug-shell.service; then
    # Service is stopped, which is what we want
    :
fi

# Verify the service is masked
if ! systemctl is-enabled debug-shell.service 2>&1 | grep -q masked; then
    # Try to verify masking another way
    if [ ! -L /etc/systemd/system/debug-shell.service ] || [ "$(readlink /etc/systemd/system/debug-shell.service)" != "/dev/null" ]; then
        # If not properly masked via symlink, ensure it's masked
        systemctl mask debug-shell.service
    fi
fi

# Reload systemd daemon to ensure changes take effect
systemctl daemon-reload

echo "debug-shell service has been successfully disabled and masked"

#!/bin/bash

# Audit rule configuration for unix_update privileged helper program

AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/unix_update.rules"
AUDIT_BIN_PATH="/usr/bin/unix_update"

# Create the audit rules file if it doesn't exist, or update it if needed
cat > "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" << 'EOF'
# Audit rule for unix_update privileged helper program execution
# This rule captures all executions of the privileged unix_update program
# by non-system users (UID >= 1000) for privilege misuse detection

# Monitor executions of unix_update by non-system users
-a always,exit -F path=/usr/bin/unix_update -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k unix_update_exec
EOF

# Check if auditd is installed
if ! command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Installing audit package..."
    yum install -y audit audit-libs
fi

# Load the audit rules
augenrules --load > /dev/null 2>&1

# If augenrules is not available, use auditctl directly
if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
    auditctl -R "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" > /dev/null 2>&1
fi

# Make sure audit daemon is running and enabled
systemctl enable auditd > /dev/null 2>&1
systemctl restart auditd > /dev/null 2>&1

# Verify the rule is loaded
if auditctl -l | grep -q "unix_update_exec"; then
    exit 0
else
    exit 1
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Audit daemon configuration script for RHEL 8
# Configures audit rules to record failed file creation attempts by regular users

# Ensure audit daemon is installed
if ! command -v auditd &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Installing audit daemon..."
    dnf install -y audit audit-libs
fi

# Define the audit rule content
AUDIT_RULE_CONTENT='# Audit rule for failed file creation attempts by regular users
# Captures both permission-denied (EACCES) and operation-not-permitted (EPERM) failures
# For unprivileged users (UID >= 1000) on both 32-bit and 64-bit systems

# 64-bit open syscall (openat is more commonly used in modern systems)
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S openat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F exit=-EACCES -F exit=-EPERM -k failed_file_creation
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S open -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F exit=-EACCES -F exit=-EPERM -k failed_file_creation
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S openat2 -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F exit=-EACCES -F exit=-EPERM -k failed_file_creation

# 32-bit open syscall (on 64-bit systems)
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S openat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F exit=-EACCES -F exit=-EPERM -k failed_file_creation
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S open -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -F exit=-EACCES -F exit=-EPERM -k failed_file_creation
'

# Determine audit configuration directory
AUDIT_RULES_D="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/audit.rules"

# Check which audit rule file loading mechanism is in use
# RHEL 8 can use either the traditional audit.rules or rules.d directory
USING_RULES_D=false

# Check if rules.d directory exists and has rules
if [ -d "$AUDIT_RULES_D" ] && [ ! -z "$(ls -A $AUDIT_RULES_D 2>/dev/null)" ]; then
    USING_RULES_D=true
fi

# Check audit.rules for non-comment content
if [ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
    if grep -v '^#' "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" | grep -v '^$' | grep -q .; then
        if [ "$USING_RULES_D" = false ]; then
            USING_RULES_D=false
        fi
    fi
fi

# Default to rules.d if both exist or neither has content
if [ "$USING_RULES_D" = false ] && [ -d "$AUDIT_RULES_D" ]; then
    USING_RULES_D=true
fi

# Create a temporary file to store the rules we want to add
TEMP_RULES=$(mktemp)
trap "rm -f $TEMP_RULES" EXIT

# Function to check if a rule already exists
rule_exists() {
    local rule="$1"
    local file="$2"
    
    # Extract just the meaningful part of the rule for comparison
    local rule_pattern=$(echo "$rule" | sed 's/^[[:space:]]*//;s/[[:space:]]*$//')
    
    if grep -F "$rule_pattern" "$file" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        return 0
    fi
    return 1
}

# Apply rules based on the detected mechanism
if [ "$USING_RULES_D" = true ]; then
    # Using rules.d directory approach
    RULES_FILE="$AUDIT_RULES_D/failed_file_creation.rules"
    
    if [ ! -f "$RULES_FILE" ]; then
        # Create new rules file
        echo "$AUDIT_RULE_CONTENT" > "$RULES_FILE"
        chmod 640 "$RULES_FILE"
    else
        # Check if rules need to be updated
        NEEDS_UPDATE=false
        
        # Check each rule to see if it exists
        while IFS= read -r line; do
            if [ ! -z "$line" ] && [[ ! "$line" =~ ^# ]]; then
                if ! rule_exists "$line" "$RULES_FILE"; then
                    NEEDS_UPDATE=true
                    break
                fi
            fi
        done <<< "$AUDIT_RULE_CONTENT"
        
        if [ "$NEEDS_UPDATE" = true ]; then
            # Append missing rules
            cp "$RULES_FILE" "$RULES_FILE.backup"
            # Remove old rules with same key
            grep -v "key.*failed_file_creation" "$RULES_FILE" > "$TEMP_RULES"
            mv "$TEMP_RULES" "$RULES_FILE"
            echo "$AUDIT_RULE_CONTENT" >> "$RULES_FILE"
        fi
    fi
else
    # Using traditional audit.rules file
    RULES_FILE="$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    
    if [ ! -f "$RULES_FILE" ]; then
        # Create new rules file
        echo "$AUDIT_RULE_CONTENT" > "$RULES_FILE"
        chmod 640 "$RULES_FILE"
    else
        # Check if rules need to be updated
        NEEDS_UPDATE=false
        
        # Check each rule to see if it exists
        while IFS= read -r line; do
            if [ ! -z "$line" ] && [[ ! "$line" =~ ^# ]]; then
                if ! rule_exists "$line" "$RULES_FILE"; then
                    NEEDS_UPDATE=true
                    break
                fi
            fi
        done <<< "$AUDIT_RULE_CONTENT"
        
        if [ "$NEEDS_UPDATE" = true ]; then
            # Backup and update
            cp "$RULES_FILE" "$RULES_FILE.backup"
            # Remove old rules with same key
            grep -v "key.*failed_file_creation" "$RULES_FILE" > "$TEMP_RULES"
            mv "$TEMP_RULES" "$RULES_FILE"
            echo "$AUDIT_RULE_CONTENT" >> "$RULES_FILE"
        fi
    fi
fi

# Start and enable the audit daemon
systemctl enable auditd
systemctl start auditd

# Load the rules into the running configuration
if command -v augenrules &> /dev/null; then
    # Use augenrules if available (newer method that reads from rules.d)
    augenrules --load
else
    # Fall back to auditctl
    auditctl -R "$RULES_FILE"
fi

# Verify rules are loaded
if auditctl -l | grep -q "failed_file_creation"; then
    echo "Audit rules successfully configured and loaded for failed file creation attempts"
else
    echo "Warning: Audit rules may not have been properly loaded"
fi

exit 0

        done <<< "$AUDIT_RULE_CONTENT"
        
        if [ "$NEEDS_UPDATE" = true ]; then
            # Append missing rules
            cp "$RULES_FILE" "$RULES_FILE.backup"
            # Remove old rules with same key
            grep -v "key.*failed_file_creation" "$RULES_FILE" > "$TEMP_RULES"
            mv "$TEMP_RULES" "$RULES_FILE"
            echo "$AUDIT_RULE_CONTENT" >> "$RULES_FILE"
        fi
    fi
else
    # Using traditional audit.rules file
    RULES_FILE="$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    
    if [ ! -f "$RULES_FILE" ]; then
        # Create new rules file
        echo "$AUDIT_RULE_CONTENT" > "$RULES_FILE"
        chmod 640 "$RULES_FILE"
    else
        # Check if rules need to be updated
        NEEDS_UPDATE=false
        
        # Check each rule to see if it exists
        while IFS= read -r line; do
            if [ ! -z "$line" ] && [[ ! "$line" =~ ^# ]]; then
                if ! rule_exists "$line" "$RULES_FILE"; then
                    NEEDS_UPDATE=true
                    break
                fi
            fi
        done <<< "$AUDIT_RULE_CONTENT"
        
        if [ "$NEEDS_UPDATE" = true ]; then
            # Backup and update
            cp "$RULES_FILE" "$RULES_FILE.backup"
            # Remove old rules with same key
            grep -v "key.*failed_file_creation" "$RULES_FILE" > "$TEMP_RULES"
            mv "$TEMP_RULES" "$RULES_FILE"
            echo "$AUDIT_RULE_CONTENT" >> "$RULES_FILE"
        fi
    fi
fi

# Start and enable the audit daemon
systemctl enable auditd
systemctl start auditd

# Load the rules into the running configuration
if command -v augenrules &> /dev/null; then
    # Use augenrules if available (newer method that reads from rules.d)
    augenrules --load
else
    # Fall back to auditctl
    auditctl -R "$RULES_FILE"
fi

# Verify rules are loaded
if auditctl -l | grep -q "failed_file_creation"; then
    echo "Audit rules successfully configured and loaded for failed file creation attempts"
else
    echo "Warning: Audit rules may not have been properly loaded"
fi

exit 0

#!/bin/bash
set -e

GNOME_DCONF_DIR="/etc/dconf/db/local.d"
GNOME_DCONF_PROFILE="/etc/dconf/profile/user"
GNOME_LOCKS_DIR="/etc/dconf/db/local.d/locks"

echo "Enforcing GNOME screensaver automatic lock settings..."

# Create necessary directories
mkdir -p "$GNOME_DCONF_DIR"
mkdir -p "$GNOME_LOCKS_DIR"

# Define the screensaver settings file
SETTINGS_FILE="$GNOME_DCONF_DIR/00-screensaver-policy"
LOCKS_FILE="$GNOME_LOCKS_DIR/screensaver"

# Write the screensaver policy settings
cat > "$SETTINGS_FILE" << 'EOF'
[org/gnome/desktop/screensaver]
# Enable automatic screen lock
lock-enabled=true
# Set lock delay to 5 minutes (300 seconds)
lock-delay=uint32 300
# Ensure screen turns off after lock (in seconds)
ubuntu-lock-on-suspend=true
# Enable screensaver
idle-activation-enabled=true
EOF

# Write the locks to prevent users from changing these settings
cat > "$LOCKS_FILE" << 'EOF'
/org/gnome/desktop/screensaver/lock-enabled
/org/gnome/desktop/screensaver/lock-delay
/org/gnome/desktop/screensaver/idle-activation-enabled
EOF

# Create or update the dconf profile to include local settings
if [ ! -f "$GNOME_DCONF_PROFILE" ]; then
    cat > "$GNOME_DCONF_PROFILE" << 'EOF'
user-db:user
system-db:local
EOF
else
    # Ensure the profile includes the local database if not already present
    if ! grep -q "system-db:local" "$GNOME_DCONF_PROFILE"; then
        echo "system-db:local" >> "$GNOME_DCONF_PROFILE"
    fi
fi

# Compile the dconf database to make changes take effect
echo "Compiling dconf database..."
dconf update || true

# For existing GNOME sessions, we need to restart the settings daemon
# This is done per-user, so we restart it for the current user and root
if command -v systemctl &> /dev/null; then
    # Restart dconf service for all active GNOME sessions
    for user in $(who | awk '{print $1}' | sort -u); do
        # Try to restart the settings daemon for this user
        if id "$user" &>/dev/null; then
            # Use loginctl to get the user's environment and restart their dconf service
            if command -v loginctl &> /dev/null && loginctl list-users 2>/dev/null | grep -q "$user"; then
                # For systemd user sessions
                sudo -u "$user" DBUS_SESSION_BUS_ADDRESS=unix:path=/run/user/$(id -u "$user")/bus dbus-send --session --print-reply --dest=ca.desrt.dconf /ca/desrt/dconf/Writer/local ca.desrt.dconf.Writer.Sync 2>/dev/null || true
            fi
        fi
    done
fi

echo "GNOME screensaver lock policy enforced successfully!"
echo "Settings applied:"
echo "  - Automatic screen lock: ENABLED"
echo "  - Lock delay: 300 seconds (5 minutes)"
echo "  - User override: DISABLED (locked via dconf)"
echo ""
echo "Configuration files:"
echo "  - Settings: $SETTINGS_FILE"
echo "  - Locks: $LOCKS_FILE"
echo "  - Profile: $GNOME_DCONF_PROFILE"
echo ""
echo "The policy will persist across reboots and users cannot override these settings."

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent remediation script for enforcing failed-attempt lockout policy on root account in RHEL 8

set -euo pipefail

# Define constants
RHEL_VERSION=$(grep -oP '(?<=VERSION_ID=")[^"]*' /etc/os-release | cut -d. -f1)
PAMFILES=("/etc/pam.d/system-auth" "/etc/pam.d/password-auth")
FAILLOCK_DIR="/var/run/faillock"
FAILLOCK_PERSISTENT_DIR="/var/log/faillock"

# Verify RHEL 8
if [ "$RHEL_VERSION" != "8" ]; then
    echo "This script is designed for RHEL 8. Current version: $RHEL_VERSION"
    exit 1
fi

# Create faillock directories if they don't exist
mkdir -p "$FAILLOCK_DIR" "$FAILLOCK_PERSISTENT_DIR"
chmod 755 "$FAILLOCK_DIR" "$FAILLOCK_PERSISTENT_DIR"

# Update faillock configuration
configure_faillock() {
    local conf_file="/etc/security/faillock.conf"
    
    # Create or update faillock.conf
    if [ ! -f "$conf_file" ]; then
        cat > "$conf_file" << 'EOF'
# Faillock configuration file
# Number of failed attempts before account lockout
deny = 5
# Duration of lockout in seconds (3600 = 1 hour)
unlock_time = 3600
# Days to retain failed login information
fail_interval = 900
# Even_deny_root will apply the policy to root account
even_deny_root
# Root_unlock_time specifies unlock time for root
root_unlock_time = 3600
EOF
    else
        # Check and update existing config
        if ! grep -q "even_deny_root" "$conf_file"; then
            echo "even_deny_root" >> "$conf_file"
        fi
        if ! grep -q "deny = " "$conf_file"; then
            echo "deny = 5" >> "$conf_file"
        fi
        if ! grep -q "unlock_time = " "$conf_file"; then
            echo "unlock_time = 3600" >> "$conf_file"
        fi
        if ! grep -q "root_unlock_time = " "$conf_file"; then
            echo "root_unlock_time = 3600" >> "$conf_file"
        fi
    fi
    
    chmod 644 "$conf_file"
}

# Configure PAM for faillock
configure_pam() {
    for pam_file in "${PAMFILES[@]}"; do
        if [ ! -f "$pam_file" ]; then
            continue
        fi
        
        # Create backup if it doesn't exist
        if [ ! -f "${pam_file}.bak" ]; then
            cp "$pam_file" "${pam_file}.bak"
        fi
        
        # Check if faillock pam_unix configuration already exists
        if grep -q "pam_faillock.so" "$pam_file"; then
            # Update existing configuration to ensure even_deny_root is set
            sed -i 's/pam_faillock\.so preauth silent/pam_faillock.so preauth silent even_deny_root/' "$pam_file"
            sed -i 's/pam_faillock\.so authfail/pam_faillock.so authfail even_deny_root/' "$pam_file"
        else
            # Add faillock configuration if not present
            # Create temporary file with proper PAM configuration
            {
                # Add preauth faillock line at the beginning
                if ! grep -q "^auth.*pam_faillock.so.*preauth" "$pam_file"; then
                    head -n 1 "$pam_file"
                    echo "auth        required      pam_faillock.so preauth silent even_deny_root unlock_time=3600"
                    tail -n +2 "$pam_file"
                fi
            } > "${pam_file}.tmp" || true
            
            # If we created a tmp file, move it
            if [ -f "${pam_file}.tmp" ] && [ -s "${pam_file}.tmp" ]; then
                mv "${pam_file}.tmp" "$pam_file"
            fi
            
            # Add authfail line after pam_unix
            if ! grep -q "^auth.*pam_faillock.so.*authfail" "$pam_file"; then
                sed -i '/^auth.*pam_unix.so.*$/a auth        required      pam_faillock.so authfail even_deny_root unlock_time=3600' "$pam_file"
            fi
        fi
    done
}

# Configure authselect if available (RHEL 8 uses authselect)
configure_authselect() {
    if command -v authselect &> /dev/null; then
        # Check current authselect profile
        current_profile=$(authselect current 2>/dev/null | head -1 || echo "")
        
        if [ -z "$current_profile" ]; then
            # Select default profile if none is selected
            authselect select sssd --force 2>/dev/null || true
        fi
        
        # Enable faillock feature in authselect
        authselect enable-feature with-faillock 2>/dev/null || true
        
        # Apply changes
        authselect apply-changes 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
}

# Verify SELinux context for faillock directories
configure_selinux() {
    if command -v semanage &> /dev/null; then
        # Set proper SELinux contexts if SELinux is enabled
        if getenforce 2>/dev/null | grep -q "Enforcing\|Permissive"; then
            semanage fcontext -a -t faillog_t "$FAILLOCK_PERSISTENT_DIR(/.*)?" 2>/dev/null || true
            restorecon -R "$FAILLOCK_PERSISTENT_DIR" 2>/dev/null || true
        fi
    fi
}

# Configure kernel panic behavior for security (optional but recommended)
configure_kernel_params() {
    local sysctl_conf="/etc/sysctl.d/98-faillock-security.conf"
    
    cat > "$sysctl_conf" << 'EOF'
# Kernel security parameters for faillock
# Log suspicious authentication attempts
kernel.log_all_unknown_module_loads = 1
# Restrict kernel module loading to root
kernel.modules_disabled = 1
EOF
    
    sysctl -p "$sysctl_conf" > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
}

# Reset faillock for root account to clear any existing locks
reset_root_faillock() {
    faillock --user root --reset 2>/dev/null || true
}

# Main execution
main() {
    echo "Configuring faillock policy for root account on RHEL 8..."
    
    # Step 1: Configure faillock settings
    configure_faillock
    echo "✓ Configured faillock.conf"
    
    # Step 2: Configure PAM
    configure_pam
    echo "✓ Configured PAM files"
    
    # Step 3: Configure authselect
    configure_authselect
    echo "✓ Configured authselect"
    
    # Step 4: Configure SELinux contexts
    configure_selinux
    echo "✓ Configured SELinux contexts"
    
    # Step 5: Configure kernel parameters
    configure_kernel_params
    echo "✓ Configured kernel parameters"
    
    # Step 6: Reset faillock for root
    reset_root_faillock
    echo "✓ Reset root account faillock"
    
    # Verify configuration
    echo ""
    echo "Configuration verification:"
    echo "---"
    echo "Faillock configuration:"
    cat /etc/security/faillock.conf 2>/dev/null | grep -v "^$" || echo "No faillock.conf found"
    
    echo ""
    echo "PAM faillock configuration in system-auth:"
    grep "pam_faillock" /etc/pam.d/system-auth 2>/dev/null || echo "Faillock not found in system-auth"
    
    echo ""
    echo "✓ Root account is now subject to automatic account-locking policy after failed login attempts"
    echo "✓ Configuration persists across reboots"
}

# Execute main function
main

                    echo "auth        required      pam_faillock.so preauth silent even_deny_root unlock_time=3600"
                    tail -n +2 "$pam_file"
                fi
            } > "${pam_file}.tmp" || true
            
            # If we created a tmp file, move it
            if [ -f "${pam_file}.tmp" ] && [ -s "${pam_file}.tmp" ]; then
                mv "${pam_file}.tmp" "$pam_file"
            fi
            
            # Add authfail line after pam_unix
            if ! grep -q "^auth.*pam_faillock.so.*authfail" "$pam_file"; then
                sed -i '/^auth.*pam_unix.so.*$/a auth        required      pam_faillock.so authfail even_deny_root unlock_time=3600' "$pam_file"
            fi
        fi
    done
}

# Configure authselect if available (RHEL 8 uses authselect)
configure_authselect() {
    if command -v authselect &> /dev/null; then
        # Check current authselect profile
        current_profile=$(authselect current 2>/dev/null | head -1 || echo "")
        
        if [ -z "$current_profile" ]; then
            # Select default profile if none is selected
            authselect select sssd --force 2>/dev/null || true
        fi
        
        # Enable faillock feature in authselect
        authselect enable-feature with-faillock 2>/dev/null || true
        
        # Apply changes
        authselect apply-changes 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
}

# Verify SELinux context for faillock directories
configure_selinux() {
    if command -v semanage &> /dev/null; then
        # Set proper SELinux contexts if SELinux is enabled
        if getenforce 2>/dev/null | grep -q "Enforcing\|Permissive"; then
            semanage fcontext -a -t faillog_t "$FAILLOCK_PERSISTENT_DIR(/.*)?" 2>/dev/null || true
            restorecon -R "$FAILLOCK_PERSISTENT_DIR" 2>/dev/null || true
        fi
    fi
}

# Configure kernel panic behavior for security (optional but recommended)
configure_kernel_params() {
    local sysctl_conf="/etc/sysctl.d/98-faillock-security.conf"
    
    cat > "$sysctl_conf" << 'EOF'
# Kernel security parameters for faillock
# Log suspicious authentication attempts
kernel.log_all_unknown_module_loads = 1
# Restrict kernel module loading to root
kernel.modules_disabled = 1
EOF
    
    sysctl -p "$sysctl_conf" > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
}

# Reset faillock for root account to clear any existing locks
reset_root_faillock() {
    faillock --user root --reset 2>/dev/null || true
}

# Main execution
main() {
    echo "Configuring faillock policy for root account on RHEL 8..."
    
    # Step 1: Configure faillock settings
    configure_faillock
    echo "✓ Configured faillock.conf"
    
    # Step 2: Configure PAM
    configure_pam
    echo "✓ Configured PAM files"
    
    # Step 3: Configure authselect
    configure_authselect
    echo "✓ Configured authselect"
    
    # Step 4: Configure SELinux contexts
    configure_selinux
    echo "✓ Configured SELinux contexts"
    
    # Step 5: Configure kernel parameters
    configure_kernel_params
    echo "✓ Configured kernel parameters"
    
    # Step 6: Reset faillock for root
    reset_root_faillock
    echo "✓ Reset root account faillock"
    
    # Verify configuration
    echo ""
    echo "Configuration verification:"
    echo "---"
    echo "Faillock configuration:"
    cat /etc/security/faillock.conf 2>/dev/null | grep -v "^$" || echo "No faillock.conf found"
    
    echo ""
    echo "PAM faillock configuration in system-auth:"
    grep "pam_faillock" /etc/pam.d/system-auth 2>/dev/null || echo "Faillock not found in system-auth"
    
    echo ""
    echo "✓ Root account is now subject to automatic account-locking policy after failed login attempts"
    echo "✓ Configuration persists across reboots"
}

# Execute main function
main

#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

SSH_CONFIG_FILE="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
SSH_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"

ensure_ssh_key_renegotiation() {
    local key_renegotiation_dir="${SSH_CONFIG_DIR}/99-key-renegotiation.conf"
    
    if [[ ! -d "${SSH_CONFIG_DIR}" ]]; then
        mkdir -p "${SSH_CONFIG_DIR}"
        chmod 755 "${SSH_CONFIG_DIR}"
    fi
    
    local renegotiation_config_content="# SSH Key Renegotiation Configuration
# Renegotiate session keys after at most 512 MB of data transmission
RekeyLimit 512M
# Renegotiate session keys after at most 1 hour of elapsed time
RekeyLimit 512M 1h
"
    
    if [[ -f "${key_renegotiation_dir}" ]]; then
        local current_content=$(cat "${key_renegotiation_dir}")
        if [[ "${current_content}" == "${renegotiation_config_content}" ]]; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    
    echo "${renegotiation_config_content}" > "${key_renegotiation_dir}"
    chmod 644 "${key_renegotiation_dir}"
    
    sshd -t > /dev/null 2>&1 || {
        rm "${key_renegotiation_dir}"
        exit 1
    }
    
    systemctl restart sshd
}

main() {
    ensure_ssh_key_renegotiation
    
    echo "SSH key renegotiation configured successfully"
    echo "RekeyLimit set to 512M 1h"
    
    sshd -T 2>/dev/null | grep -i rekeylimit || true
}

main

#!/bin/bash

# Script to disable virtual syscall mechanism on RHEL 8 systems
# This script modifies GRUB configuration to disable vsyscall across all kernels

set -euo pipefail

# Define GRUB configuration file
GRUB_CONFIG="/etc/default/grub"
GRUB_BOOTLOADER_DIR="/boot/grub2"
GRUB_EFI_DIR="/boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg"
GRUB_BIOS_DIR="/boot/grub2/grub.cfg"

# Function to add kernel parameter to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX if not already present
add_kernel_parameter() {
    local param="$1"
    local config_file="$2"
    
    if ! grep -q "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" "$config_file"; then
        echo "GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"$param\"" >> "$config_file"
    elif grep "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" "$config_file" | grep -q "$param"; then
        return 0
    else
        sed -i "s/^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"\(.*\)\"$/GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"\1 $param\"/" "$config_file"
    fi
}

# Function to add kernel parameter to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT if not already present
add_kernel_parameter_default() {
    local param="$1"
    local config_file="$2"
    
    if ! grep -q "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT=" "$config_file"; then
        echo "GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT=\"$param\"" >> "$config_file"
    elif grep "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT=" "$config_file" | grep -q "$param"; then
        return 0
    else
        sed -i "s/^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT=\"\(.*\)\"$/GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT=\"\1 $param\"/" "$config_file"
    fi
}

main() {
    # Check if running as root
    if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
        echo "Error: This script must be run as root" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
    
    # Check if this is a RHEL 8 system
    if ! grep -q "VERSION_ID=\"8" /etc/os-release 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "Warning: This system does not appear to be RHEL 8. Proceeding anyway..." >&2
    fi
    
    # Check if GRUB configuration file exists
    if [[ ! -f "$GRUB_CONFIG" ]]; then
        echo "Error: GRUB configuration file not found at $GRUB_CONFIG" >&2
        exit 1
    fi
    
    # Create backup of original GRUB config
    cp "$GRUB_CONFIG" "${GRUB_CONFIG}.backup.$(date +%s)"
    
    # Add vsyscall=none parameter to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
    add_kernel_parameter "vsyscall=none" "$GRUB_CONFIG"
    
    # Also add to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT for consistency
    add_kernel_parameter_default "vsyscall=none" "$GRUB_CONFIG"
    
    # Regenerate GRUB configuration for both BIOS and EFI systems
    if [[ -d "$GRUB_BOOTLOADER_DIR" ]]; then
        # Standard BIOS/UEFI location
        if command -v grub2-mkconfig &> /dev/null; then
            echo "Regenerating GRUB configuration..."
            grub2-mkconfig -o "$GRUB_BIOS_DIR" 2>/dev/null || true
            
            # Also handle EFI if present
            if [[ -d "$(dirname "$GRUB_EFI_DIR")" ]]; then
                grub2-mkconfig -o "$GRUB_EFI_DIR" 2>/dev/null || true
            fi
        fi
    fi
    
    # Verify the parameter was added correctly
    if grep -q "vsyscall=none" "$GRUB_CONFIG"; then
        echo "Successfully added vsyscall=none parameter to GRUB configuration"
        echo "The vsyscall mechanism will be disabled on next reboot"
        
        # Show current status
        echo ""
        echo "Current GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX setting:"
        grep "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" "$GRUB_CONFIG" || true
        
        exit 0
    else
        echo "Error: Failed to add vsyscall=none parameter" >&2
        # Restore backup on failure
        cp "${GRUB_CONFIG}.backup.$(date +%s)" "$GRUB_CONFIG"
        exit 1
    fi
}

main "$@"
