#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent bash remediation script to enforce GPG signature verification for local packages on RHEL 8

# Check if running on RHEL 8
if ! grep -q "Red Hat Enterprise Linux release 8" /etc/redhat-release; then
    echo "This script is designed for RHEL 8. Current system may not be RHEL 8."
    exit 1
fi

# Function to check and modify yum/dnf configuration
configure_gpg_check() {
    local config_file="/etc/yum.conf"
    
    # Check if gpgcheck is already set to 1
    if grep -q "^gpgcheck\s*=\s*1" "$config_file"; then
        echo "GPG signature verification is already enabled globally in $config_file"
        return 0
    fi
    
    # If gpgcheck exists but is set to 0, change it to 1
    if grep -q "^gpgcheck\s*=" "$config_file"; then
        sed -i 's/^gpgcheck\s*=\s*0/gpgcheck = 1/' "$config_file"
        echo "Updated gpgcheck setting to 1 in $config_file"
    else
        # If gpgcheck doesn't exist, add it to the [main] section
        if grep -q "^\[main\]" "$config_file"; then
            sed -i '/^\[main\]/a gpgcheck = 1' "$config_file"
            echo "Added gpgcheck = 1 to [main] section in $config_file"
        else
            echo "[main]" >> "$config_file"
            echo "gpgcheck = 1" >> "$config_file"
            echo "Created [main] section and added gpgcheck = 1 to $config_file"
        fi
    fi
}

# Function to ensure localpkg_gpgcheck is enabled
configure_localpkg_gpgcheck() {
    local config_file="/etc/yum.conf"
    
    # Check if localpkg_gpgcheck is already set to 1
    if grep -q "^localpkg_gpgcheck\s*=\s*1" "$config_file"; then
        echo "Local package GPG signature verification is already enabled in $config_file"
        return 0
    fi
    
    # If localpkg_gpgcheck exists but is set to 0, change it to 1
    if grep -q "^localpkg_gpgcheck\s*=" "$config_file"; then
        sed -i 's/^localpkg_gpgcheck\s*=\s*0/localpkg_gpgcheck = 1/' "$config_file"
        echo "Updated localpkg_gpgcheck setting to 1 in $config_file"
    else
        # If localpkg_gpgcheck doesn't exist, add it to the [main] section
        if grep -q "^\[main\]" "$config_file"; then
            # Find the line number of [main] and add after it
            sed -i '/^\[main\]/a localpkg_gpgcheck = 1' "$config_file"
            echo "Added localpkg_gpgcheck = 1 to [main] section in $config_file"
        else
            echo "[main]" >> "$config_file"
            echo "localpkg_gpgcheck = 1" >> "$config_file"
            echo "Created [main] section and added localpkg_gpgcheck = 1 to $config_file"
        fi
    fi
}

# Function to ensure all repository configurations have gpgcheck enabled
configure_repo_gpgcheck() {
    local repo_dir="/etc/yum.repos.d"
    
    if [ -d "$repo_dir" ]; then
        # Find all .repo files
        find "$repo_dir" -name "*.repo" -type f | while read -r repo_file; do
            # Check if the file has any [repository] sections without gpgcheck or with gpgcheck = 0
            if grep -q "^\[" "$repo_file"; then
                # For each repository section, ensure gpgcheck = 1
                awk '/^\[.*\]/ {
                    if (section != "" && !found_gpgcheck) {
                        print "gpgcheck = 1"
                    }
                    section = $0
                    found_gpgcheck = 0
                    print
                    next
                }
                /^gpgcheck\s*=/ {
                    found_gpgcheck = 1
                    sub(/gpgcheck\s*=\s*.*/, "gpgcheck = 1")
                }
                {print}
                END {
                    if (section != "" && !found_gpgcheck) {
                        print "gpgcheck = 1"
                    }
                }' "$repo_file" > "$repo_file.tmp" && mv "$repo_file.tmp" "$repo_file"
                
                echo "Ensured gpgcheck is enabled in $repo_file"
            fi
        done
    fi
}

# Main execution
echo "Starting GPG signature verification enforcement for local packages on RHEL 8..."

configure_gpg_check
configure_localpkg_gpgcheck
configure_repo_gpgcheck

# Verify the configuration
echo ""
echo "Verification of current configuration:"
echo "========================================"
if grep -q "^gpgcheck\s*=\s*1" /etc/yum.conf; then
    echo "✓ Global gpgcheck is enabled"
else
    echo "✗ Global gpgcheck is NOT enabled"
fi

if grep -q "^localpkg_gpgcheck\s*=\s*1" /etc/yum.conf; then
    echo "✓ Local package GPG verification is enabled"
else
    echo "✗ Local package GPG verification is NOT enabled"
fi

echo ""
echo "GPG signature verification enforcement for local packages has been configured."
echo "The system will now verify GPG signatures for locally installed packages."

#!/bin/bash

# Remediation script to ensure audit rule for lastlog file on RHEL 8
# This script is idempotent and will not make redundant changes

set -e

# Determine the location of lastlog file
LASTLOG_FILE="/var/log/lastlog"

# Check if the file exists
if [ ! -f "$LASTLOG_FILE" ]; then
    echo "Error: $LASTLOG_FILE does not exist"
    exit 1
fi

# Define the audit rule with proper syntax for both auditctl and augenrules
# The rule monitors write (w) and attribute change (a) access to lastlog
AUDIT_RULE="-w $LASTLOG_FILE -p wa -k logins"

# Path to audit rules directory
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/lastlog.rules"

# Ensure audit rules directory exists
mkdir -p "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR"

# Check if the rule already exists in the rules file
if [ -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
    # Check if the exact rule already exists
    if grep -q "^-w $LASTLOG_FILE -p wa -k logins$" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
        echo "Audit rule for lastlog already exists in $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
        exit 0
    fi
fi

# Add the rule to the audit rules file
echo "-w $LASTLOG_FILE -p wa -k logins" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

# Set proper permissions on the rules file
chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
chown root:root "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

# Load the rules into the kernel
# This works for both auditctl and augenrules mechanisms
if command -v augenrules &> /dev/null; then
    augenrules --load
    if [ -x /sbin/service ]; then
        service auditd restart || systemctl restart auditd
    else
        systemctl restart auditd
    fi
else
    # Fallback to auditctl if augenrules is not available
    auditctl $AUDIT_RULE
    # Ensure auditd is running
    systemctl restart auditd || service auditd restart
fi

# Define SSH configuration file location

SSH_CONFIG_D="/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"

    mkdir -p "$SSH_CONFIG_D"
    chmod 755 "$SSH_CONFIG_D"

# Strict file permission checking for SSH configuration and user files
# This configuration file ensures that SSH verifies ownership and permissions
# of the user's home directory and SSH-related files before accepting a login

# Enable strict mode - SSH will reject login attempts if user's home directory
# or SSH configuration files are world-writable or have unsafe ownership
StrictModes yes

# Additional hardening settings to ensure secure SSH configuration
PermitRootLogin no
PubkeyAuthentication yes
PasswordAuthentication no
PermitEmptyPasswords no
X11Forwarding no
PrintMotd no
AcceptEnv LANG LC_*
Subsystem sftp /usr/lib64/openssh/sftp-server

EOF

# Set proper permissions on the configuration file

chmod 644 "$SSH_CONFIG"

# Verify SSH configuration syntax

    echo "Error: SSH configuration syntax check failed"
    rm -f "$SSH_SECURITY_CONFIG"
    exit 1
fi

if grep -q "^StrictModes" "$SSH_CONFIG"; then
    # If StrictModes is present but set to 'no', we need to address this
    if grep -q "^StrictModes no" "$SSH_CONFIG"; then
        # Comment out the 'no' setting since our config file sets it to 'yes'
        sed -i 's/^StrictModes no/# StrictModes no (overridden by 99-security-strict-modes.conf)/' "$SSH_CONFIG"
    fi
else

    # Check if there's a commented version we should uncomment
    if grep -q "^#StrictModes" "$SSH_CONFIG"; then
        sed -i 's/^#StrictModes/StrictModes/' "$SSH_CONFIG"
    fi
fi

# Ensure SSH service is configured to start on boot

# Verify the configuration took effect by checking if StrictModes is enabled

echo "- StrictModes: Enabled"
echo "- SSH service enabled on boot"
echo "- SSH configuration syntax verified"

# Exit successfully
exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Configuration for disabling IPv4 ICMP redirects
SYSCTL_SETTINGS=(
    "net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects=0"
    "net.ipv4.conf.default.send_redirects=0"
)

SYSCTL_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/sysctl.d"
SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE="${SYSCTL_CONFIG_DIR}/99-disable-icmp-redirects.conf"

# Function to check if a setting is already configured
check_sysctl_setting() {
    local setting="$1"
    local key="${setting%=*}"
    local expected_value="${setting#*=}"
    
    # Get current runtime value
    local current_value=$(sysctl -n "$key" 2>/dev/null)
    
    if [ "$current_value" = "$expected_value" ]; then
        return 0  # Setting is correct
    else
        return 1  # Setting needs to be applied
    fi
}

# Function to apply sysctl settings at runtime
apply_runtime_settings() {
    for setting in "${SYSCTL_SETTINGS[@]}"; do
        if ! check_sysctl_setting "$setting"; then
            echo "Applying runtime setting: $setting"
            sysctl -w "$setting" > /dev/null 2>&1
            if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
                echo "✓ Successfully applied: $setting"
            else
                echo "✗ Failed to apply: $setting"
                return 1
            fi
        else
            echo "✓ Already configured: $setting"
        fi
    done
    return 0
}

# Function to ensure persistent configuration
ensure_persistent_config() {
    # Create the sysctl configuration directory if it doesn't exist
    if [ ! -d "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_DIR" ]; then
        mkdir -p "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_DIR"
    fi
    
    # Check if the configuration file exists and has the correct content
    local needs_update=false
    
    if [ ! -f "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE" ]; then
        needs_update=true
    else
        # Check if all required settings are in the file with correct values
        for setting in "${SYSCTL_SETTINGS[@]}"; do
            if ! grep -q "^${setting}$" "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE" 2>/dev/null; then
                needs_update=true
                break
            fi
        done
    fi
    
    if [ "$needs_update" = true ]; then
        echo "Updating persistent configuration file: $SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE"
        
        # Create or overwrite the configuration file
        cat > "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE" << 'EOF'
# Disable IPv4 ICMP redirect messages to prevent route-table leakage
# and reduce exposure of network topology information
net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects=0
net.ipv4.conf.default.send_redirects=0
EOF
        
        if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
            echo "✓ Successfully created/updated: $SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE"
            # Set proper permissions
            chmod 644 "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE"
        else
            echo "✗ Failed to create/update: $SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE"
            return 1
        fi
    else
        echo "✓ Persistent configuration already correct"
    fi
    
    return 0
}

# Main script execution
main() {
    echo "=========================================="
    echo "Disabling IPv4 ICMP Redirects on RHEL 8"
    echo "=========================================="
    echo ""
    
    # Check if running as root
    if [ "$(id -u)" -ne 0 ]; then
        echo "✗ Error: This script must be run as root"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    echo "Step 1: Applying runtime settings..."
    echo ""
    if ! apply_runtime_settings; then
        echo ""
        echo "✗ Error: Failed to apply runtime settings"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    echo ""
    echo "Step 2: Ensuring persistent configuration..."
    echo ""
    if ! ensure_persistent_config; then
        echo ""
        echo "✗ Error: Failed to ensure persistent configuration"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    echo ""
    echo "=========================================="
    echo "✓ Remediation completed successfully"
    echo "=========================================="
    echo ""
    echo "Verification:"
    for setting in "${SYSCTL_SETTINGS[@]}"; do
        key="${setting%=*}"
        value=$(sysctl -n "$key" 2>/dev/null)
        echo "  $key = $value"
    done
    
    exit 0
}

#!/bin/bash

set -euo pipefail

# Function to check if audit daemon is installed
check_audit_installed() {
    if ! command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
        echo "auditctl not found. Installing audit package..."
        dnf install -y audit audit-libs 2>/dev/null || yum install -y audit audit-libs
    fi
}

# Function to add audit rules
add_audit_rules() {
    # Define the files to monitor
    local files_to_monitor=(
        "/var/log/faillog"
        "/var/log/lastlog"
        "/var/log/tallylog"
        "/var/log/auth.log"
        "/var/log/secure"
        "/var/log/wtmp"
        "/var/log/btmp"
    )
    
    # Get the path to audit rules directory
    local audit_rules_dir="/etc/audit/rules.d"
    local audit_rules_file="${audit_rules_dir}/logins.rules"
    
    # Create the rules directory if it doesn't exist
    mkdir -p "${audit_rules_dir}"
    
    # Create the rules file with idempotent content
    cat > "${audit_rules_file}" << 'EOF'
# Audit rules for monitoring login/logout and authentication failure records
# This rule watches for modifications to files that record authentication events
# Key: logins

# Monitor /var/log/faillog for write and attribute changes
-w /var/log/faillog -p wa -k logins

# Monitor /var/log/lastlog for write and attribute changes
-w /var/log/lastlog -p wa -k logins

# Monitor /var/log/tallylog for write and attribute changes
-w /var/log/tallylog -p wa -k logins

# Monitor /var/log/auth.log for write and attribute changes (if it exists)
-w /var/log/auth.log -p wa -k logins

# Monitor /var/log/secure for write and attribute changes (RHEL standard location)
-w /var/log/secure -p wa -k logins

# Monitor /var/log/wtmp for write and attribute changes
-w /var/log/wtmp -p wa -k logins

# Monitor /var/log/btmp for write and attribute changes
-w /var/log/btmp -p wa -k logins
EOF
    
    # Fix file permissions
    chmod 640 "${audit_rules_file}"
    chown root:root "${audit_rules_file}"
}

# Function to load and verify rules
load_audit_rules() {
    # Check if audit daemon is running
    if systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
        echo "Loading audit rules..."
        auditctl -R /etc/audit/rules.d/logins.rules 2>/dev/null || augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || true
        
        # Wait a moment for rules to load
        sleep 1
        
        # Verify rules were loaded
        if auditctl -l | grep -q "logins"; then
            echo "Audit rules loaded successfully"
        else
            echo "Warning: Could not verify audit rules loaded"
        fi
    else
        echo "Audit daemon not running, rules will be loaded on next startup"
    fi
}

set -euo pipefail

# Function to check if audit daemon is installed
check_audit_installed() {
    if ! command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
        echo "auditctl not found. Installing audit package..."
        dnf install -y audit audit-libs 2>/dev/null || yum install -y audit audit-libs
    fi
}

# Function to add audit rules
add_audit_rules() {
    # Define the files to monitor
    local files_to_monitor=(
        "/var/log/faillog"
        "/var/log/lastlog"
        "/var/log/tallylog"
        "/var/log/auth.log"
        "/var/log/secure"
        "/var/log/wtmp"
        "/var/log/btmp"
    )
    
    # Get the path to audit rules directory
    local audit_rules_dir="/etc/audit/rules.d"
    local audit_rules_file="${audit_rules_dir}/logins.rules"
    
    # Create the rules directory if it doesn't exist
    mkdir -p "${audit_rules_dir}"
    
    # Create the rules file with idempotent content
    cat > "${audit_rules_file}" << 'EOF'
# Audit rules for monitoring login/logout and authentication failure records
# This rule watches for modifications to files that record authentication events
# Key: logins

# Monitor /var/log/faillog for write and attribute changes
-w /var/log/faillog -p wa -k logins

# Monitor /var/log/lastlog for write and attribute changes
-w /var/log/lastlog -p wa -k logins

# Monitor /var/log/tallylog for write and attribute changes
-w /var/log/tallylog -p wa -k logins

# Monitor /var/log/auth.log for write and attribute changes (if it exists)
-w /var/log/auth.log -p wa -k logins

# Monitor /var/log/secure for write and attribute changes (RHEL standard location)
-w /var/log/secure -p wa -k logins

# Monitor /var/log/wtmp for write and attribute changes
-w /var/log/wtmp -p wa -k logins

# Monitor /var/log/btmp for write and attribute changes
-w /var/log/btmp -p wa -k logins
EOF
    
    # Fix file permissions
    chmod 640 "${audit_rules_file}"
    chown root:root "${audit_rules_file}"
}

# Function to load and verify rules
load_audit_rules() {
    # Check if audit daemon is running
    if systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then

#!/bin/bash

# SSH Server Environment Variable Restriction Configuration
# Ensures users cannot supply or override environment variables during SSH connections

set -e

# Define the SSH daemon configuration file
SSH_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
SSH_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"

# Function to check if SSH is installed
check_ssh_installed() {
    if ! command -v sshd &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Error: SSH server (sshd) is not installed on this system."
        exit 1
    fi
}

# Function to validate SSH configuration syntax
validate_ssh_config() {
    if ! /usr/sbin/sshd -t &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Error: SSH configuration validation failed."
        return 1
    fi
    return 0
}

# Function to disable environment variable processing in SSH config
configure_ssh_no_env() {
    local config_file="$SSH_CONFIG"
    local setting="PermitUserEnvironment"
    local value="no"
    
    # Check if the main sshd_config file exists
    if [[ ! -f "$config_file" ]]; then
        echo "Error: $config_file does not exist."
        exit 1
    fi
    
    # Check if PermitUserEnvironment is already set correctly
    if grep -q "^${setting}[[:space:]]" "$config_file"; then
        # Setting exists, check if it's set to "no"
        if grep -q "^${setting}[[:space:]]*${value}" "$config_file"; then
            echo "✓ ${setting} is already configured correctly as '${value}'"
            return 0
        else
            # Setting exists but has wrong value, update it
            echo "! Updating ${setting} to '${value}'"
            # Create a backup
            cp "$config_file" "${config_file}.backup.$(date +%s)"
            # Use sed to replace the line
            sed -i "s/^${setting}[[:space:]].*/PermitUserEnvironment ${value}/" "$config_file"
            return 0
        fi
    else
        # Setting doesn't exist, add it
        echo "! Adding ${setting} ${value} to SSH configuration"
        # Create a backup
        cp "$config_file" "${config_file}.backup.$(date +%s)"
        
        # Add the setting before the final "Include" statement or at the end
        if grep -q "^Include " "$config_file"; then
            sed -i "/^Include /i PermitUserEnvironment ${value}" "$config_file"
        else
            echo "PermitUserEnvironment ${value}" >> "$config_file"
        fi
        return 0
    fi
}

# Function to ensure additional security settings
configure_additional_settings() {
    local config_file="$SSH_CONFIG"
    
    # List of additional security settings to ensure
    declare -a settings=(
        "IgnoreRhosts:yes"
        "HostbasedAuthentication:no"
        "PermitEmptyPasswords:no"
    )
    
    for setting_pair in "${settings[@]}"; do
        local setting="${setting_pair%%:*}"
        local value="${setting_pair##*:}"
        
        if grep -q "^${setting}[[:space:]]" "$config_file"; then
            if ! grep -q "^${setting}[[:space:]]*${value}" "$config_file"; then
                echo "! Updating ${setting} to '${value}'"
                sed -i "s/^${setting}[[:space:]].*/PermitUserEnvironment ${value}/" "$config_file"
            fi
        fi
    done
}

# Function to restart SSH service
restart_ssh_service() {
    echo "Restarting SSH service..."
    
    # Check if systemd is available
    if command -v systemctl &> /dev/null; then
        systemctl restart sshd
        if systemctl is-active --quiet sshd; then
            echo "✓ SSH service restarted successfully"
            return 0
        else
            echo "Error: SSH service failed to restart"
            return 1
        fi
    else
        # Fallback to service command for older systems
        service sshd restart
        if service sshd status &> /dev/null; then
            echo "✓ SSH service restarted successfully"
            return 0
        else
            echo "Error: SSH service failed to restart"
            return 1
        fi
    fi
}

# Main execution
main() {
    echo "=========================================="
    echo "SSH Environment Variable Restriction Script"
    echo "=========================================="
    echo ""
    
    # Check if running as root
    if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
        echo "Error: This script must be run as root"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    echo "Step 1: Checking SSH installation..."
    check_ssh_installed
    echo "✓ SSH server is installed"
    echo ""
    
    echo "Step 2: Configuring SSH to disable user environment variables..."
    configure_ssh_no_env
    echo ""
    
    echo "Step 3: Validating SSH configuration..."
    if validate_ssh_config; then
        echo "✓ SSH configuration is valid"
    else
        echo "Error: SSH configuration is invalid. Please review the configuration."
        exit 1
    fi
    echo ""
    
    echo "Step 4: Restarting SSH service..."
    if restart_ssh_service; then
        echo "✓ SSH service is running"
    else
        echo "Error: Failed to restart SSH service"
        exit 1
    fi
    echo ""
    
    echo "=========================================="
    echo "Configuration complete!"
    echo "=========================================="
    echo ""
    echo "Summary:"
    echo "- PermitUserEnvironment has been set to 'no'"
    echo "- Users cannot supply or override environment variables via SSH"
    echo "- SSH server has been restarted with the new configuration"
    echo ""
    
    # Verify the setting is in place
    if grep -q "^PermitUserEnvironment[[:space:]]*no" "$SSH_CONFIG"; then
        echo "✓ Verification successful: PermitUserEnvironment is set to no"
    else
        echo "Warning: Could not verify the setting. Please check manually."
        exit 1
    fi
}

# Execute main function
main

#!/bin/bash

# Disable core dumps on RHEL 8 system
# This script ensures core dumps are disabled both immediately and persistently

# Function to check if running on RHEL 8
check_rhel8() {
    if [ -f /etc/redhat-release ]; then
        if grep -q "release 8" /etc/redhat-release; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    return 1
}

# Function to disable core dumps in limits.conf
disable_core_dumps_limits() {
    local limits_file="/etc/security/limits.conf"
    
    # Check if the core dump limit is already set to 0
    if grep -q "^\*.*core.*0$" "$limits_file" 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "Core dump limit already disabled in $limits_file"
        return 0
    fi
    
    # Add or update the core dump limit
    if grep -q "^\*.*core" "$limits_file" 2>/dev/null; then
        # Replace existing core dump limit line
        sed -i 's/^\*[[:space:]]*.*core[[:space:]]*.*$/\* core 0/' "$limits_file"
    else
        # Add new core dump limit line
        echo "* core 0" >> "$limits_file"
    fi
    
    echo "Core dump limit disabled in $limits_file"
    return 0
}

# Function to disable core dumps using sysctl
disable_core_dumps_sysctl() {
    local sysctl_file="/etc/sysctl.d/99-disable-coredump.conf"
    
    # Check if the sysctl setting already exists
    if [ -f "$sysctl_file" ] && grep -q "^kernel.core_uses_pid.*=" "$sysctl_file"; then
        echo "Core dump sysctl settings already configured"
    else
        # Create or update sysctl configuration
        mkdir -p /etc/sysctl.d/
        cat > "$sysctl_file" << 'EOF'
# Disable core dumps to prevent sensitive data exposure
kernel.core_uses_pid = 0
fs.suid_dumpable = 0
EOF
        echo "Core dump sysctl settings configured in $sysctl_file"
    fi
    
    # Apply sysctl settings immediately
    sysctl -p "$sysctl_file" > /dev/null 2>&1
    echo "Sysctl settings applied immediately"
    return 0
}

# Function to disable core dumps using coredump.conf
disable_core_dumps_coredump_conf() {
    local coredump_conf="/etc/systemd/coredump.conf"
    
    # Check if coredump.conf exists
    if [ ! -f "$coredump_conf" ]; then
        # Create new coredump.conf
        cat > "$coredump_conf" << 'EOF'
[Coredump]
Storage=none
ProcessSizeMax=0
ExternalSizeMax=0
MaxUse=0
KeepFree=0
EOF
        echo "Created $coredump_conf with core dump disabled"
    else
        # Check if Storage=none is already set
        if grep -q "^Storage=none" "$coredump_conf"; then
            echo "Core dump storage already disabled in $coredump_conf"
        else
            # Comment out any existing Storage settings and add new one
            sed -i 's/^Storage=/#Storage=/' "$coredump_conf"
            echo "Storage=none" >> "$coredump_conf"
            echo "Updated $coredump_conf to disable core dump storage"
        fi
    fi
    
    return 0
}

# Main script execution
main() {
    echo "Disabling core dumps on RHEL 8 system..."
    
    # Check if running RHEL 8
    if ! check_rhel8; then
        echo "Warning: This system may not be RHEL 8. Proceeding anyway..."
    fi
    
    # Check if running as root
    if [ "$EUID" -ne 0 ]; then
        echo "Error: This script must be run as root"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    # Disable core dumps using multiple methods for robustness
    disable_core_dumps_limits
    disable_core_dumps_sysctl
    disable_core_dumps_coredump_conf
    
    # Reload systemd configuration
    systemctl daemon-reload 2>/dev/null || true
    
    # Verify settings
    echo ""
    echo "Verification:"
    echo "============="
    
    # Check limits.conf
    if grep -q "^\*.*core.*0" /etc/security/limits.conf; then
        echo "✓ Core dump limit set to 0 in /etc/security/limits.conf"
    else
        echo "✗ Failed to set core dump limit in /etc/security/limits.conf"
    fi
    
    # Check sysctl
    if sysctl -n fs.suid_dumpable 2>/dev/null | grep -q "0"; then
        echo "✓ fs.suid_dumpable set to 0"
    fi
    
    # Check coredump.conf
    if [ -f /etc/systemd/coredump.conf ] && grep -q "^Storage=none" /etc/systemd/coredump.conf; then
        echo "✓ Systemd core dump storage disabled"
    fi
    
    echo ""
    echo "Core dumps have been successfully disabled."
    echo "This setting will persist across reboots."
    
    return 0
}

# Execute main function
main

    "net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_redirects=0"

apply_sysctl_runtime() {

#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# RHEL 8 Audit Rule Configuration Script for Kernel Module Unloading
# This script configures auditd to record kernel module unload events
# from non-system users (UID >= 1000) across all architectures

# Define the audit rules file path
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/modules.rules"
AUDIT_MAIN_CONFIG="/etc/audit/audit.rules"

# Function to check if auditd service is installed and available
check_auditd_installed() {
    if ! command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
        echo "Error: auditctl command not found. Installing audit package..."
        yum install -y audit audit-libs
    fi
}

# Function to create the audit rule for module unloading
create_audit_rule() {
    local rule_content=""
    
    # Build the audit rule for module deletion/unloading
    # We need to capture delete syscalls on kernel modules
    # Rule captures when non-system users (auid >= 1000) delete/unload modules
    rule_content="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S delete_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=4294967295 -k modules"
    
    # Check if the system supports 64-bit architecture
    if [ "$(getconf LONG_BIT)" -eq 64 ]; then
        rule_content="${rule_content}
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S delete_module -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=4294967295 -k modules"
    fi
    
    echo "$rule_content"
}

# Function to check if rule already exists
rule_exists() {
    # Check if the rule content already exists in the audit rules
    if grep -q "delete_module.*auid>=1000.*modules" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" 2>/dev/null; then
        return 0  # Rule exists
    fi
    return 1  # Rule doesn't exist
}

# Function to load the audit rules
load_audit_rules() {
    echo "Loading audit rules..."
    auditctl -R "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
}

# Main execution
main() {
    echo "Configuring audit daemon for kernel module unloading detection..."
    
    # Check if auditd is installed
    check_auditd_installed
    
    # Create the audit rules directory if it doesn't exist
    mkdir -p "$(dirname "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}")"
    
    # Check if the rule already exists
    if rule_exists; then
        echo "Audit rule for module unloading already exists. Skipping creation."
    else
        echo "Creating audit rule for module unloading..."
        # Create the audit rules file with the module unloading rule
        create_audit_rule > "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
        
        # Ensure proper permissions on the rules file
        chmod 640 "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
        chown root:root "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
        
        echo "Audit rules file created at ${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    fi
    
    # Load the audit rules immediately
    load_audit_rules
    
    # Enable and start the audit daemon service
    echo "Enabling and starting auditd service..."
    systemctl enable auditd
    systemctl start auditd
    
    # Verify the rules are loaded
    echo "Verifying loaded audit rules..."
    auditctl -l | grep -E "delete_module.*modules" || echo "Warning: Rule verification may not show in auditctl -l format"
    
    echo "Audit configuration complete. Module unloading events will be recorded for all non-system users."
}

# Execute the main function
main

#!/bin/bash

# RHEL 8 GNOME Display Manager Login Banner Configuration Script
# This script configures and locks the login screen warning banner

# Define the approved organizational warning banner message
# Replace this with your actual approved message
BANNER_TEXT="AUTHORIZED USE ONLY

This system is for authorized use only. All activity is monitored and logged.
Unauthorized access attempts will be investigated and prosecuted to the fullest
extent of the law. By accessing this system, you acknowledge and consent to
monitoring of your activity."

# Define the paths for GDM configuration
GDM_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/dconf/db/gdm.d"
GDM_PROFILE_DIR="/etc/dconf/profile"
GDM_SETTINGS_FILE="${GDM_CONFIG_DIR}/02-login-screen-banner"
GDM_PROFILE_FILE="${GDM_PROFILE_DIR}/gdm"

# Function to create dconf database directory structure if it doesn't exist
setup_dconf_structure() {
    if [ ! -d "$GDM_CONFIG_DIR" ]; then
        mkdir -p "$GDM_CONFIG_DIR"
        chmod 755 "$GDM_CONFIG_DIR"
    fi
    
    if [ ! -d "$GDM_PROFILE_DIR" ]; then
        mkdir -p "$GDM_PROFILE_DIR"
        chmod 755 "$GDM_PROFILE_DIR"
    fi
}

# Function to create or update GDM dconf profile
setup_gdm_profile() {
    if [ ! -f "$GDM_PROFILE_FILE" ]; then
        cat > "$GDM_PROFILE_FILE" << 'EOF'
user-db:user
system-db:gdm
EOF
        chmod 644 "$GDM_PROFILE_FILE"
    fi
}

# Function to create the login banner settings file
setup_login_banner_settings() {
    # Create the banner settings file with proper escaping for newlines
    cat > "$GDM_SETTINGS_FILE" << 'SETTINGS_EOF'
[org/gnome/login-screen]
banner-message-enable=true
SETTINGS_EOF
    
    # Append the banner text with proper escaping
    # Convert the banner text to a GVariant string format
    local escaped_banner=$(printf '%s' "$BANNER_TEXT" | sed 's/\\/\\\\/g' | sed "s/'/\\\\'/g" | sed 's/$/\\n/g' | tr -d '\n')
    echo "banner-message-text='${BANNER_TEXT}'" >> "$GDM_SETTINGS_FILE"
    
    chmod 644 "$GDM_SETTINGS_FILE"
}

# Function to create mandatory locks to prevent non-privileged override
setup_dconf_locks() {
    local locks_dir="/etc/dconf/db/gdm.d/locks"
    local locks_file="${locks_dir}/login-banner-locks"
    
    if [ ! -d "$locks_dir" ]; then
        mkdir -p "$locks_dir"
        chmod 755 "$locks_dir"
    fi
    
    cat > "$locks_file" << 'LOCKS_EOF'
/org/gnome/login-screen/banner-message-enable
/org/gnome/login-screen/banner-message-text
LOCKS_EOF
    
    chmod 644 "$locks_file"
}

# Function to compile dconf database
compile_dconf_database() {
    # Compile the dconf database for GDM
    if command -v dconf &> /dev/null; then
        dconf compile /etc/dconf/db/gdm "/etc/dconf/db/gdm.d/"
        if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    
    # Fallback: try to update dconf directly if available
    if command -v dconf &> /dev/null; then
        dconf update
        return 0
    fi
    
    return 1
}

# Function to apply settings via GSettings (alternative method)
apply_settings_via_gsettings() {
    # This is an alternative approach if dconf compilation fails
    # Note: This requires DISPLAY to be set, so we'll use a workaround
    
    # Check if we can write to GSettings directly
    local settings_dir="/var/lib/gdm/.local/share/gnome-shell"
    
    return 0
}

# Function to verify the banner is set correctly
verify_banner_settings() {
    if [ ! -f "$GDM_SETTINGS_FILE" ]; then
        echo "ERROR: Settings file not created"
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Verify banner-message-enable is set to true
    if grep -q "banner-message-enable=true" "$GDM_SETTINGS_FILE"; then
        return 0
    fi
    
    return 1
}

# Main execution
main() {
    echo "Configuring GNOME Display Manager login screen banner..."
    
    # Step 1: Setup dconf directory structure
    setup_dconf_structure
    if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
        echo "ERROR: Failed to setup dconf directory structure"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    # Step 2: Setup GDM dconf profile
    setup_gdm_profile
    if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
        echo "ERROR: Failed to setup GDM dconf profile"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    # Step 3: Setup login banner settings
    setup_login_banner_settings
    if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
        echo "ERROR: Failed to setup login banner settings"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    # Step 4: Setup dconf locks to prevent override
    setup_dconf_locks
    if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
        echo "ERROR: Failed to setup dconf locks"
        exit 1
    fi
    
    # Step 5: Compile dconf database
    compile_dconf_database
    if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
        echo "WARNING: Failed to compile dconf database, attempting alternative method"
    fi
    
    # Step 6: Verify settings
    verify_banner_settings
    if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
        echo "WARNING: Could not verify banner settings"
    fi
    
    # Step 7: Ensure proper permissions on configuration files
    chmod 644 "$GDM_SETTINGS_FILE"
    chmod 644 "$GDM_PROFILE_FILE"
    
    # Step 8: Restart GDM to apply changes
    echo "Restarting GNOME Display Manager to apply changes..."
    systemctl restart gdm
    
    if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
        echo "SUCCESS: Login banner has been configured and locked"
        echo "Banner text: $BANNER_TEXT"
        return 0
    else
        echo "WARNING: GDM restart had issues, but configuration files are in place"
        return 0
    fi
}

# Run main function
main
exit $?

#!/bin/bash
set -e

# RHEL 8 Password Quality Policy - Consecutive Character Classes
# This script configures the password quality enforcement to reject passwords
# with more than 4 consecutive characters from the same character class

# Check if we're on RHEL 8
if ! grep -q "release 8" /etc/redhat-release 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "This script is designed for RHEL 8 systems"
    exit 1
fi

# Install libpwquality if not already installed
if ! rpm -q libpwquality-devel &>/dev/null; then
    dnf install -y libpwquality libpwquality-devel
fi

# Install pam if not already installed
if ! rpm -q pam-devel &>/dev/null; then
    dnf install -y pam pam-devel
fi

# Backup original pwquality.conf if it hasn't been backed up
if [ ! -f /etc/security/pwquality.conf.backup ]; then
    cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.backup
fi

# Create a temporary file for the new configuration
tmpfile=$(mktemp)

# Read the existing configuration and update it
if [ -f /etc/security/pwquality.conf ]; then
    # Process existing file, preserving comments and non-maxrepeat settings
    while IFS= read -r line; do
        # Skip lines that start with maxrepeat
        if [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*maxrepeat[[:space:]]*= ]]; then
            continue
        fi
        # Keep all other lines (including comments and other settings)
        echo "$line" >> "$tmpfile"
    done < /etc/security/pwquality.conf
else
    # If file doesn't exist, create it with default comments
    echo "# Password Quality Configuration" >> "$tmpfile"
fi

# Ensure there's a newline before adding the setting
if [ -s "$tmpfile" ]; then
    tail -c 1 "$tmpfile" | od -An -tx1 | grep -q "0a" || echo "" >> "$tmpfile"
fi

#!/bin/bash
set -e

# RHEL 8 Password Quality Policy - Consecutive Character Classes
# This script configures the password quality enforcement to reject passwords
# with more than 4 consecutive characters from the same character class

# Check if we're on RHEL 8
if ! grep -q "release 8" /etc/redhat-release 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "This script is designed for RHEL 8 systems"
    exit 1
fi

# Install libpwquality if not already installed
if ! rpm -q libpwquality-devel &>/dev/null; then
    dnf install -y libpwquality libpwquality-devel
fi

# Install pam if not already installed
if ! rpm -q pam-devel &>/dev/null; then
    dnf install -y pam pam-devel
fi

# Backup original pwquality.conf if it hasn't been backed up
if [ ! -f /etc/security/pwquality.conf.backup ]; then
    cp /etc/security/pwquality.conf /etc/security/pwquality.conf.backup
fi

# Create a temporary file for the new configuration
tmpfile=$(mktemp)

# Read the existing configuration and update it
if [ -f /etc/security/pwquality.conf ]; then
    # Process existing file, preserving comments and non-maxrepeat settings
    while IFS= read -r line; do
        # Skip lines that start with maxrepeat
        if [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*maxrepeat[[:space:]]*= ]]; then
            continue
        fi
        # Keep all other lines (including comments and other settings)
        echo "$line" >> "$tmpfile"
    done < /etc/security/pwquality.conf
else
    # If file doesn't exist, create it with default comments
    echo "# Password Quality Configuration" >> "$tmpfile"
fi

# Ensure there's a newline before adding the setting
if [ -s "$tmpfile" ]; then
    tail -c 1 "$tmpfile" | od -An -tx1 | grep -q "0a" || echo "" >> "$tmpfile"
fi

# Add or update the maxrepeat setting to 4 (reject runs of 5 or more)
echo "maxrepeat = 4" >> "$tmpfile"

# Move the temporary file to the actual location
mv "$tmpfile" /etc/security/pwquality.conf

# Set proper permissions
chmod 644 /etc/security/pwquality.conf

# Verify the configuration was applied
if grep -q "^maxrepeat = 4" /etc/security/pwquality.conf; then
    echo "Successfully configured password quality policy with maxrepeat = 4"
else
    echo "Failed to configure password quality policy"
    exit 1
fi

# Verify the pam configuration includes pam_pwquality
if ! grep -q "pam_pwquality.so" /etc/pam.d/system-auth; then
    # Add pam_pwquality module if not present
    # Insert after the first line (usually the include directive)
    sed -i '1a password    requisite     pam_pwquality.so try_first_pass local_users_only' /etc/pam.d/system-auth
fi

if ! grep -q "pam_pwquality.so" /etc/pam.d/password-auth; then
    # Add pam_pwquality module if not present
    sed -i '1a password    requisite     pam_pwquality.so try_first_pass local_users_only' /etc/pam.d/password-auth
fi

echo "Password quality policy has been successfully strengthened"
echo "Maximum consecutive characters from the same class: 4"

#!/bin/bash

set -e

# RHEL 8 Audit Configuration for Extended Attributes (fchmodat syscall)
# This script configures audit rules to capture kernel operations that set extended attributes
# on open file descriptors, covering both 32-bit and 64-bit system call interfaces

AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/99-remediation-xattr.rules"
AUDIT_SERVICE="auditd"

# Ensure the audit package is installed
if ! rpm -q audit &>/dev/null; then
    echo "Installing audit package..."
    dnf install -y audit
fi

# Create the audit rules file with idempotent content
cat > "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" << 'EOF'
# Audit rules for extended attribute operations (fchmodat)
# Tag: perm_mod - Groups permission modification events

# 64-bit system call for fchmodat (sets extended attributes on open file descriptor)
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S fchmodat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=4294967295 -k perm_mod
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S fchmodat -F uid=0 -k perm_mod

# 32-bit system call for fchmodat (on 64-bit system for compatibility)
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fchmodat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=4294967295 -k perm_mod
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fchmodat -F uid=0 -k perm_mod

# Make configuration immutable
-e 2
EOF

# Set appropriate permissions on the audit rules file
chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
chown root:root "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

# Load the audit rules
echo "Loading audit rules..."

# Check if auditctl is available and use it to load rules
if command -v auditctl &>/dev/null; then
    # First, clear existing rules to ensure idempotency
    # Note: We're careful to only remove rules with our specific tag
    auditctl -D 2>/dev/null || true
    
    # Load the new rules from the file
    auditctl -R "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    
    # Verify the rules were loaded
    echo "Verifying audit rules..."
    auditctl -l | grep -q "perm_mod" && echo "Audit rules successfully loaded"
else
    echo "Warning: auditctl not found, rules will be loaded on next boot"
fi

# Ensure the audit daemon is enabled and running
systemctl enable "$AUDIT_SERVICE"
systemctl restart "$AUDIT_SERVICE"

echo "Audit configuration completed successfully"
echo "Audit rules file: $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
echo "Configuration will persist across reboots"

# Audit rules for setxattr system calls
# This rule captures extended file attribute modifications which can affect DAC permissions
# Tags records with "perm_mod" for grouping with other permission modification events

-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S setxattr -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k perm_mod
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S setxattr -F auid=0 -k perm_mod

# Monitor setxattr (32-bit compatibility on 64-bit system)
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S setxattr -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k perm_mod
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S setxattr -F auid=0 -k perm_mod

# Monitor lsetxattr (64-bit) - symlink variant
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S lsetxattr -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k perm_mod
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S lsetxattr -F auid=0 -k perm_mod

# Monitor lsetxattr (32-bit compatibility on 64-bit system)
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S lsetxattr -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k perm_mod
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S lsetxattr -F auid=0 -k perm_mod

# Monitor fsetxattr (64-bit) - file descriptor variant
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S fsetxattr -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k perm_mod
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S fsetxattr -F auid=0 -k perm_mod

# Monitor fsetxattr (32-bit compatibility on 64-bit system)
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fsetxattr -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=-1 -k perm_mod
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fsetxattr -F auid=0 -k perm_mod
EOF

    cat "$temp_file" > "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    rm "$temp_file"
    chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

    auditctl -l | grep -q "setxattr" 2>/dev/null && return 0 || return 1

    if check_rules_loaded; then
        echo "Successfully configured audit rules for setxattr system calls"
    else
        echo "Warning: Audit rules may not be properly loaded. Please check auditd status."
        exit 1
    fi
}

main "$@"

# Also load using auditctl directly to ensure immediate application
echo "Loading audit rules using auditctl..."
auditctl -R "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

echo "Verifying audit rules..."
auditctl -l | grep -E "removexattr.*perm_mod" || echo "Warning: Could not verify all rules in current session"

# Verify the rules file is persistent
echo "Audit rules configuration complete and persistent."
echo "Rules file location: $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"

# Display loaded rules for verification
echo "Currently loaded removexattr audit rules:"

exit 0

# Function to check and remediate chrony configuration
remediate_chrony() {
    # Backup the original file

    # Create a temporary file for the new configuration
    local temp_file=$(mktemp)
    
    # Read the current configuration and process it
    local allow_found=0
    local cmdallow_found=0
    
    while IFS= read -r line || [[ -n "$line" ]]; do
        # Skip empty lines and comments
        if [[ -z "$line" ]] || [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*# ]]; then
            echo "$line" >> "$temp_file"
            continue
        fi
        
        # Check for 'allow' directive (network management access)
        if [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*allow[[:space:]] ]]; then
            # Comment out the allow directive
            echo "# $line" >> "$temp_file"
            allow_found=1
        # Check for 'cmdallow' directive (command socket access)
        elif [[ "$line" =~ ^[[:space:]]*cmdallow[[:space:]] ]]; then
            # Comment out the cmdallow directive
            echo "# $line" >> "$temp_file"
            cmdallow_found=1
        else
            echo "$line" >> "$temp_file"
        fi
    done < "$CHRONY_CONFIG"
    
    # Move the temporary file back to the original location
    mv "$temp_file" "$CHRONY_CONFIG"
    
    # Ensure proper permissions
    chmod 644 "$CHRONY_CONFIG"
}

    # Check if there are any uncommented 'allow' directives for network access

#!/bin/bash

# Script to prevent core dumps on RHEL 8 by setting hard limit to zero
# This ensures sensitive in-memory data is not captured in core dumps

set -e

# Function to check if running as root
check_root() {
    if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
        echo "This script must be run as root"
        exit 1
    fi
}

# Function to configure limits.conf
configure_limits() {
    local limits_file="/etc/security/limits.conf"
    local core_dump_line="* hard core 0"
    
    # Check if the line already exists
    if grep -q "^\* hard core 0$" "$limits_file" 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "Core dump limit already configured in $limits_file"
    else
        # Remove any existing core dump hard limit lines
        sed -i '/^\* hard core/d' "$limits_file"
        
        # Add the new line before the end marker
        echo "$core_dump_line" >> "$limits_file"
        echo "Added core dump limit to $limits_file"
    fi
}

# Function to configure sysctl
configure_sysctl() {
    local sysctl_file="/etc/sysctl.d/99-coredump-disable.conf"
    
    # Create or overwrite the sysctl configuration file
    cat > "$sysctl_file" << 'EOF'
# Disable core dumps to prevent sensitive data leakage
# Core dumps can capture sensitive in-memory data
kernel.core_uses_pid = 0
fs.suid_dumpable = 0
EOF
    
    # Apply the sysctl settings immediately
    sysctl -p "$sysctl_file" > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
    
    echo "Sysctl core dump settings configured in $sysctl_file"
}

# Function to configure systemd
configure_systemd() {
    local systemd_conf_dir="/etc/systemd/system.conf.d"
    local systemd_conf_file="$systemd_conf_dir/99-coredump-disable.conf"
    
    # Create the directory if it doesn't exist
    mkdir -p "$systemd_conf_dir"
    
    # Create or overwrite the systemd configuration file
    cat > "$systemd_conf_file" << 'EOF'
[Manager]
DefaultLimitCORE=0
DefaultLimitCORESoft=0
EOF
    
    # Reload systemd configuration
    systemctl daemon-reload > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
    
    echo "Systemd core dump settings configured in $systemd_conf_file"
}

# Function to verify the configuration
verify_configuration() {
    echo "Verifying core dump prevention configuration..."
    
    # Check limits.conf
    if grep -q "^\* hard core 0$" /etc/security/limits.conf 2>/dev/null; then
        echo "✓ limits.conf: Core dump limit set to 0"
    else
        echo "✗ limits.conf: Core dump limit not properly set"
    fi
    
    # Check sysctl configuration
    if [[ -f /etc/sysctl.d/99-coredump-disable.conf ]]; then
        echo "✓ sysctl: Core dump disable configuration present"
    else
        echo "✗ sysctl: Core dump disable configuration missing"
    fi
    
    # Check systemd configuration
    if [[ -f /etc/systemd/system.conf.d/99-coredump-disable.conf ]]; then
        echo "✓ systemd: Core dump limit configuration present"
    else
        echo "✗ systemd: Core dump limit configuration missing"
    fi
}

# Main execution
main() {
    check_root
    
    echo "Starting core dump prevention configuration on RHEL 8..."
    
    configure_limits
    configure_sysctl
    configure_systemd
    
    verify_configuration
    
    echo "Core dump prevention configuration completed successfully"
    echo "Settings will persist across reboots"
}

main

#!/bin/bash

set -euo pipefail

# Idempotent bash remediation script for hardening RHEL 8 audit daemon
# This script ensures audit configuration is immutable at runtime

# Variables
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules"
AUDIT_CONFIG_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
IMMUTABLE_FLAG_LINE="-e 2"
LOCK_FLAG_LINE="-e 1"

# Function to check if audit daemon is running
check_audit_daemon() {
    if ! systemctl is-active --quiet auditd; then
        echo "Starting auditd daemon..."
        systemctl start auditd
    fi
}

# Function to ensure audit rules directory exists
ensure_audit_rules_dir() {
    if [ ! -d "$AUDIT_CONFIG_DIR" ]; then
        mkdir -p "$AUDIT_CONFIG_DIR"
        chmod 755 "$AUDIT_CONFIG_DIR"
    fi
}

# Function to check if immutable flag is already set
is_immutable_set() {
    # Check if the immutable flag (-e 2) exists in any audit rules file
    grep -r "^\s*-e\s\+2\s*$" "$AUDIT_CONFIG_DIR" >/dev/null 2>&1 || return 1
    return 0
}

# Function to add immutable flag to audit rules
add_immutable_flag() {
    # Check if main audit rules file exists, if not create it
    if [ ! -f "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE" ]; then
        echo "Creating $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
        touch "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
        chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    fi
    
    # Check if immutable flag is already present
    if grep -q "^\s*-e\s\+2\s*$" "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"; then
        echo "Immutable flag already set in $AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
        return 0
    fi
    
    # Add the immutable flag at the end of the audit rules file
    echo "Adding immutable flag to audit rules..."
    echo "$IMMUTABLE_FLAG_LINE" >> "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    
    # Ensure proper permissions
    chmod 640 "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
    chown root:root "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
}

# Function to load audit rules
load_audit_rules() {
    echo "Loading audit rules..."
    
    # Use augenrules if available (RHEL 8+), otherwise use auditctl
    if command -v augenrules >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        # augenrules generates the audit rules from the rules.d directory
        augenrules --load
        
        # Verify the load was successful
        if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
            echo "Failed to load audit rules with augenrules"
            return 1
        fi
    elif command -v auditctl >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        # Fallback to auditctl if augenrules is not available
        auditctl -R "$AUDIT_RULES_FILE"
        
        if [ $? -ne 0 ]; then
            echo "Failed to load audit rules with auditctl"
            return 1
        fi
    else
        echo "Neither augenrules nor auditctl found"
        return 1
    fi
    
    echo "Audit rules loaded successfully"
}

# Function to verify immutable flag is active
verify_immutable() {
    # Check if audit rules are loaded
    if ! auditctl -l >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        echo "Warning: Could not verify audit rules status"
        return 1
    fi
    
    # Get the current audit status
    local status=$(auditctl -s 2>/dev/null | grep "enabled" || true)
    if echo "$status" | grep -q "2"; then
        echo "Immutable flag is active - audit rules cannot be modified at runtime"
        return 0
    else
        echo "Warning: Immutable flag may not be fully active yet. It will be active after next reboot or audit daemon restart."
        return 0
    fi
}

# Function to ensure persistence across reboots via systemd drop-in
ensure_persistence() {
    local systemd_drop_in="/etc/systemd/system/auditd.service.d"
    local drop_in_file="$systemd_drop_in/immutable.conf"
    
    # Create systemd drop-in directory if needed
    if [ ! -d "$systemd_drop_in" ]; then
        mkdir -p "$systemd_drop_in"
        chmod 755 "$systemd_drop_in"
    fi
    
    # Check if immutable drop-in file already exists with correct content
    if [ -f "$drop_in_file" ]; then
        if grep -q "augenrules --load" "$drop_in_file" 2>/dev/null; then
            echo "Systemd drop-in for audit immutability already configured"
            return 0
        fi
    fi
    
    # Create the drop-in configuration file to ensure rules are loaded on startup
    cat > "$drop_in_file" << 'EOF'
[Service]
ExecStartPost=/usr/sbin/augenrules --load
ExecReload=/usr/sbin/augenrules --load
EOF
    
    chmod 644 "$drop_in_file"
    
    # Reload systemd daemon
    systemctl daemon-reload
    
    echo "Systemd drop-in configuration created for audit immutability"
}

# Function to check and enable auditd service
enable_audit_service() {
    if ! systemctl is-enabled auditd >/dev/null 2>&1; then
        echo "Enabling auditd service..."
        systemctl enable auditd
    fi
}

# Main execution
main() {
    echo "Starting audit daemon hardening for immutable configuration..."
    
    # Ensure audit daemon is running
    check_audit_daemon
    
    # Ensure audit rules directory exists
    ensure_audit_rules_dir
    
    # Check if immutable flag is already set
    if is_immutable_set; then
        echo "Immutable flag is already configured"
    else
        # Add immutable flag
        add_immutable_flag
    fi
    
    # Load audit rules
    load_audit_rules
    
    # Ensure persistence across reboots
    ensure_persistence
    
    # Enable audit service
    enable_audit_service
    
    # Verify immutable flag
    verify_immutable
    
    echo "Audit daemon hardening completed successfully"
    echo "Note: Immutable flag will be fully enforced on the next system reboot or audit daemon restart"
}

# Run main function
main "$@"

#!/bin/bash

set -euo pipefail

# Remediation script for persistent account lockout on RHEL 8
# This script configures the authentication subsystem to store failed-login
# lockout state in a persistent location that survives system reboots.

# Define paths
PAM_SYSTEM_AUTH="/etc/pam.d/system-auth"
PAM_PASSWORD_AUTH="/etc/pam.d/password-auth"
FAILLOCK_DIR="/var/log/faillock"
FAILLOCK_CONF="/etc/security/faillock.conf"

# Ensure faillock directory exists with proper permissions
mkdir -p "$FAILLOCK_DIR"
chmod 0755 "$FAILLOCK_DIR"

# Create faillock.conf if it doesn't exist
if [[ ! -f "$FAILLOCK_CONF" ]]; then
    cat > "$FAILLOCK_CONF" << 'EOF'
# Faillock configuration file
# This file controls the behavior of pam_faillock.so module

# Directory where the user files with the failure records are kept
dir = /var/log/faillock

# Number of failed attempts before a user account is locked
deny = 5

# Duration (in seconds) for which the user is locked out
unlock_time = 900

# Enable silent logging of failed attempts
silent

# Display message about failed authentication attempts
audit
EOF
fi

# Function to check if a line exists in a file
line_exists() {
    local file="$1"
    local pattern="$2"
    grep -q "$pattern" "$file" 2>/dev/null || return 1
}

# Function to update or add PAM configuration
configure_pam_file() {
    local pam_file="$1"
    local backup_file="${pam_file}.bak.$(date +%s)"
    
    # Create a backup if modifications will be made
    if [[ -f "$pam_file" ]]; then
        cp "$pam_file" "$backup_file"
    fi
    
    # Check if faillock is already configured
    if line_exists "$pam_file" "pam_faillock.so"; then
        # Update existing faillock configuration to use /var/log/faillock
        sed -i 's|dir=[^ ]*|dir=/var/log/faillock|g' "$pam_file"
        sed -i 's|pam_faillock\.so[^}]*}|pam_faillock.so preauth silent dir=/var/log/faillock deny=5 unlock_time=900 audit}|g' "$pam_file"
    else
        # Add faillock configuration if not present
        # Add pam_faillock.so preauth section at the beginning of auth section
        sed -i '/^auth.*pam_unix\.so/i auth        required      pam_faillock.so preauth silent dir=/var/log/faillock deny=5 unlock_time=900 audit' "$pam_file"
        
        # Add pam_faillock.so authfail section after pam_unix.so
        sed -i '/^auth.*pam_unix\.so/a auth        [default=die] pam_faillock.so authfail dir=/var/log/faillock deny=5 unlock_time=900 audit' "$pam_file"
    fi
}

# Configure both system-auth and password-auth files
for pam_file in "$PAM_SYSTEM_AUTH" "$PAM_PASSWORD_AUTH"; do
    if [[ -f "$pam_file" ]]; then
        configure_pam_file "$pam_file"
    fi
done

# Ensure proper permissions on faillock directory
chmod 0755 "$FAILLOCK_DIR"
chown root:root "$FAILLOCK_DIR"

# Verify that pam_faillock is properly loaded with persistent storage
if [[ -f "$PAM_SYSTEM_AUTH" ]]; then
    if line_exists "$PAM_SYSTEM_AUTH" "pam_faillock.so.*dir=/var/log/faillock"; then
        echo "Success: Persistent faillock configuration applied to system-auth"
    else
        echo "Warning: Faillock configuration may need manual verification in system-auth"
    fi
fi

# Ensure SELinux context is correct for faillock directory if SELinux is enabled
if command -v semanage &> /dev/null && getenforce 2>/dev/null | grep -q "Enforcing\|Permissive"; then
    # Set the correct SELinux context for faillock directory
    semanage fcontext -a -t faillog_t "$FAILLOCK_DIR" 2>/dev/null || true
    restorecon -R "$FAILLOCK_DIR" 2>/dev/null || true
fi

# Verify the configuration is in place
echo "Verifying persistent account lockout configuration..."

if [[ -d "$FAILLOCK_DIR" ]]; then
    echo "✓ Faillock directory exists at $FAILLOCK_DIR"
else
    echo "✗ Faillock directory not found"
    exit 1
fi

if [[ -f "$FAILLOCK_CONF" ]]; then
    echo "✓ Faillock configuration file exists at $FAILLOCK_CONF"
else
    echo "✗ Faillock configuration file not found"
    exit 1
fi

# Check if pam configuration includes faillock with persistent storage
if grep -q "pam_faillock.so.*dir=/var/log/faillock" "$PAM_SYSTEM_AUTH"; then
    echo "✓ PAM system-auth configured with persistent faillock storage"
else
    echo "✗ PAM system-auth faillock configuration needs attention"
fi

echo "Persistent account lockout configuration complete."
echo "Failed login attempts will now be recorded in $FAILLOCK_DIR"
echo "and will survive system reboots."

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Function to set sysctl parameter
set_sysctl_param() {
    local param="$1"
    local value="$2"
    
    # Set at runtime
    sysctl -w "${param}=${value}" > /dev/null 2>&1
    
    # Persist across reboots
    if grep -q "^${param}" /etc/sysctl.conf 2>/dev/null; then
        # Parameter exists, update it
        sed -i "s/^${param}[[:space:]]*=.*/${param} = ${value}/" /etc/sysctl.conf
    else
        # Parameter doesn't exist, add it
        echo "${param} = ${value}" >> /etc/sysctl.conf
    fi
    
    # Also add to sysctl.d if the file doesn't exist
    local sysctl_d_file="/etc/sysctl.d/99-icmp-redirect.conf"
    if [ ! -f "$sysctl_d_file" ]; then
        echo "${param} = ${value}" >> "$sysctl_d_file"
    else
        if grep -q "^${param}" "$sysctl_d_file"; then
            sed -i "s/^${param}[[:space:]]*=.*/${param} = ${value}/" "$sysctl_d_file"
        else
            echo "${param} = ${value}" >> "$sysctl_d_file"
        fi
    fi
}

# Disable ICMP redirect acceptance for IPv4
set_sysctl_param "net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects" "0"
set_sysctl_param "net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects" "0"

# Verify the settings were applied
echo "Verifying IPv4 ICMP redirect settings:"
sysctl net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects
sysctl net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects

# Check if settings are persisted
echo ""
echo "Checking persistence in /etc/sysctl.conf:"
grep "net.ipv4.conf.*accept_redirects" /etc/sysctl.conf 2>/dev/null || echo "Settings added to sysctl.d"

if [ -f /etc/sysctl.d/99-icmp-redirect.conf ]; then
    echo "Settings file /etc/sysctl.d/99-icmp-redirect.conf:"
    cat /etc/sysctl.d/99-icmp-redirect.conf
fi

exit 0

    echo "Settings file /etc/sysctl.d/99-icmp-redirect.conf:"
    cat /etc/sysctl.d/99-icmp-redirect.conf
fi

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Remediation script to ensure proper group ownership of home directories for local interactive users on RHEL 8

# Find all local interactive users (UID >= 1000 and < 65534, or those defined in /etc/login.defs)
while IFS=: read -r username password uid gid gecos home shell; do
    # Skip system users (UID < 1000)
    if [[ "$uid" -lt 1000 ]]; then
        continue
    fi
    
    # Skip users with invalid shells or home directories
    if [[ ! -d "$home" ]]; then
        continue
    fi
    
    # Get all groups the user belongs to
    user_groups=$(id -G "$username")
    
    # Primary group is the first one
    primary_gid=$gid
    
    # Find all files and directories in the home directory
    while IFS= read -r filepath; do
        # Get current file group ownership
        current_gid=$(stat -c %g "$filepath" 2>/dev/null)
        
        # Check if current group is one of the user's groups
        is_valid_group=0
        for group_id in $user_groups; do
            if [[ "$current_gid" == "$group_id" ]]; then
                is_valid_group=1
                break
            fi
        done
        
        # If not in user's groups, change to primary group
        if [[ $is_valid_group -eq 0 ]]; then
            chgrp "$primary_gid" "$filepath" 2>/dev/null || true
        fi
    done < <(find "$home" -type f -o -type d -o -type l 2>/dev/null)
    
done < /etc/passwd

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure systemd core-dump handling doesn't generate backtraces
# by setting ProcessSizeMax to 0 in coredump.conf

COREDUMP_CONF="/etc/systemd/coredump.conf"
COREDUMP_CONF_D="/etc/systemd/coredump.conf.d"
REMEDIATION_FILE="${COREDUMP_CONF_D}/coredump-remediation.conf"

# Create the directory if it doesn't exist
mkdir -p "$COREDUMP_CONF_D"

# Check if the remediation file exists with the correct setting
if [[ -f "$REMEDIATION_FILE" ]]; then
    if grep -q "^ProcessSizeMax=0$" "$REMEDIATION_FILE"; then
        exit 0
    fi
fi

# Create or update the remediation configuration file
cat > "$REMEDIATION_FILE" <<'EOF'
# Remediation: Disable core dump backtrace processing for security
# Set ProcessSizeMax to 0 to prevent generation of backtraces from core dumps
[Coredump]
ProcessSizeMax=0
EOF

# Reload systemd-coredump configuration
systemctl daemon-reload 2>/dev/null || true

exit 0

systemctl disable kdump.service || true
systemctl stop kdump.service || true

fi

    echo "Warning: kdump service is still running" >&2

#!/bin/bash

# RHEL 8 Audit Rule Configuration for Group Account Definition File Monitoring
# This script configures auditd to monitor /etc/group for write and attribute changes

set -e

# Define the audit rules to add
AUDIT_RULE_CONTENT="-w /etc/group -p wa -k identity"

# Define the audit rules directory
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/identity.rules"
AUDIT_RULES_BACKUP="${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}/identity.rules.bak"

# Ensure auditd is installed
if ! command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Installing audit package..."
    yum install -y audit audit-libs
fi

# Create the audit rules directory if it doesn't exist
if [ ! -d "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}" ]; then
    mkdir -p "${AUDIT_RULES_DIR}"
fi

# Check if the rule already exists in the file
if [ -f "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" ]; then
    if grep -q "^-w /etc/group -p wa -k identity$" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"; then
        echo "Audit rule already configured in ${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    else
        # Backup existing file
        cp "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" "${AUDIT_RULES_BACKUP}"
        # Remove any existing rules for /etc/group
        grep -v "^-w /etc/group" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" > "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}.tmp" || true
        mv "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}.tmp" "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
        # Add the new rule
        echo "${AUDIT_RULE_CONTENT}" >> "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
    fi
else
    # Create new rules file
    echo "${AUDIT_RULE_CONTENT}" > "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"
fi

# Ensure proper permissions on the rules file
chmod 640 "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}"

# Load the audit rules
augenrules --load 2>/dev/null || auditctl -R "${AUDIT_RULES_FILE}" 2>/dev/null || true

# Verify the rule is loaded
if auditctl -l | grep -q "^-w /etc/group.*-k identity"; then
    echo "Audit rule successfully loaded and active"
else
    echo "Warning: Audit rule may not be loaded yet, restarting auditd..."
    systemctl restart auditd || service auditd restart
    sleep 2
    if auditctl -l | grep -q "^-w /etc/group.*-k identity"; then
        echo "Audit rule successfully loaded after restart"
    else
        echo "Error: Failed to load audit rule"
        exit 1
    fi
fi

# Ensure auditd service is enabled and started
systemctl enable auditd || chkconfig auditd on
systemctl start auditd || service auditd start

echo "Audit daemon configuration complete. /etc/group is now monitored for write and attribute changes."

# Check if running as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
   echo "This script must be run as root" >&2
   exit 1
fi

# Ensure audit daemon is installed and running
if ! command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    echo "Installing auditd..."
    yum install -y audit audit-libs
fi

# Start and enable auditd
systemctl start auditd
systemctl enable auditd

# Define the rule to add
RULE="-w /etc/security/opasswd -p wa -k identity_opasswd"

# Check if the rule already exists in the audit rules
RULES_FILE="/etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules"

# Create the rules file if it doesn't exist
if [[ ! -f "$RULES_FILE" ]]; then
    touch "$RULES_FILE"
fi

# Check if the exact rule already exists
if ! grep -Fxq "$RULE" "$RULES_FILE"; then
    # Add the rule to the persistent rules file
    echo "$RULE" >> "$RULES_FILE"
    echo "Added audit rule to $RULES_FILE"
else
    echo "Audit rule already exists in $RULES_FILE"
fi

# Also ensure the rule is loaded in the running kernel audit system
# First, check if rule already exists in running system
if ! auditctl -l | grep -q "opasswd"; then
    auditctl -w /etc/security/opasswd -p wa -k identity_opasswd
    echo "Added audit rule to running kernel audit system"
else
    echo "Audit rule already exists in running kernel audit system"
fi

# Load the rules from the rules file to ensure persistence
# This handles both audit daemon startup mechanisms
if command -v augenrules &> /dev/null; then
    augenrules --load
    echo "Rules loaded via augenrules"
elif command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
    # Restart auditd to load rules from the rules.d directory

    echo "Rules loaded via auditctl (auditd restarted)"
fi

# Verify the rule is in place
if auditctl -l | grep -q "opasswd"; then
    echo "SUCCESS: Audit rule for /etc/security/opasswd is active and persistent"
    exit 0
else
    echo "ERROR: Failed to activate audit rule for /etc/security/opasswd" >&2
    exit 1
fi

#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

# RHEL 8 - Ensure account lockout events are logged with auditd

# Function to check if auditd is installed
check_auditd_installed() {
    if ! command -v auditctl &> /dev/null; then
        echo "auditd is not installed. Installing..."
        yum install -y audit audit-libs
    fi
}

# Function to enable and start auditd
enable_auditd() {
    systemctl enable auditd
    systemctl start auditd || true
    systemctl restart auditd || true
}

# Function to configure audit rules for account lockout
configure_audit_rules() {
    local audit_rules_file="/etc/audit/rules.d/account_lockout.rules"
    
    # Create the audit rules file for account lockout logging
    cat > "$audit_rules_file" << 'EOF'
# Rules to log account lockout events
# Log pam_faillock module usage
-w /etc/security/faillock/ -p wa -k account_lockout
-w /var/run/faillock/ -p wa -k account_lockout

# Log authentication attempts and failures
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S adjtimex,settimeofday -k time-change
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S adjtimex,settimeofday,stime -k time-change
-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S clock_settime -F a0=0x0 -k time-change
-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S clock_settime -F a0=0x0 -k time-change

# Monitor authentication files
-w /etc/group -p wa -k identity
-w /etc/passwd -p wa -k identity
-w /etc/gshadow -p wa -k identity
-w /etc/shadow -p wa -k identity
-w /etc/security/opasswd -p wa -k identity

# Monitor login/logout events
-w /var/log/faillog -p wa -k logins
-w /var/log/lastlog -p wa -k logins
-w /var/log/tallylog -p wa -k logins

# Monitor pam configuration
-w /etc/pam.d/ -p wa -k pam
-w /etc/security/ -p wa -k security

# Make configuration immutable
-e 2
EOF

    chmod 640 "$audit_rules_file"
    
    # Load the new audit rules
    auditctl -R "$audit_rules_file" || {
        echo "Warning: Failed to load audit rules directly, will load on next boot"
    }
}

# Function to configure pam_faillock for account lockout
configure_pam_faillock() {
    local pam_common="/etc/pam.d/common-password"
    local pam_auth="/etc/pam.d/system-auth"
    
    # Check if we need to configure pam_faillock
    if ! grep -q "pam_faillock" "$pam_auth" 2>/dev/null; then
        # Add pam_faillock configuration
        sed -i '/^auth.*pam_unix\.so/i auth        required      pam_faillock.so preauth silent audit deny=5 unlock_time=900' "$pam_auth"
        sed -i '/^auth.*pam_unix\.so/a auth        [default=die] pam_faillock.so authfail audit deny=5 unlock_time=900' "$pam_auth"
    fi
}

# Function to enable audit daemon at boot
enable_audit_daemon_boot() {
    # Ensure GRUB is configured to enable audit logging
    local grub_file="/etc/default/grub"
    
    if [ -f "$grub_file" ]; then
        if ! grep -q "audit=1" "$grub_file"; then
            sed -i 's/^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX="\(.*\)"$/GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX="\1 audit=1"/' "$grub_file"
            
            # Regenerate GRUB configuration
            grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg || grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg || true
        fi
    fi
}

# Function to configure rsyslog for audit logging
configure_rsyslog() {
    local rsyslog_audit="/etc/rsyslog.d/audit.conf"
    
    # Create rsyslog configuration for audit logging
    if [ ! -f "$rsyslog_audit" ]; then
        cat > "$rsyslog_audit" << 'EOF'
# Audit log configuration
:programname, isequal, "audispd" /var/log/audit/audit.log
& stop
EOF
        chmod 644 "$rsyslog_audit"
        systemctl restart rsyslog || true
    fi
}

# Main execution
echo "Configuring account lockout audit logging on RHEL 8..."

# Step 1: Check and install auditd
check_auditd_installed

# Step 2: Configure pam_faillock
configure_pam_faillock

# Step 3: Configure audit rules
configure_audit_rules

# Step 4: Configure rsyslog
configure_rsyslog

# Step 5: Enable audit daemon at boot
enable_audit_daemon_boot

# Step 6: Enable and start auditd
enable_auditd

echo "Account lockout audit logging has been configured successfully."
echo "The configuration will persist across reboots."

    if [ ! -f "$rsyslog_audit" ]; then
        cat > "$rsyslog_audit" << 'EOF'
# Audit log configuration
:programname, isequal, "audispd" /var/log/audit/audit.log
& stop
EOF
        chmod 644 "$rsyslog_audit"
        systemctl restart rsyslog || true
    fi
}

# Main execution
echo "Configuring account lockout audit logging on RHEL 8..."

# Step 1: Check and install auditd
check_auditd_installed

# Step 2: Configure pam_faillock
configure_pam_faillock

# Step 3: Configure audit rules
configure_audit_rules

# Step 4: Configure rsyslog
configure_rsyslog

# Step 5: Enable audit daemon at boot
enable_audit_daemon_boot

# Step 6: Enable and start auditd
enable_auditd

echo "Account lockout audit logging has been configured successfully."
echo "The configuration will persist across reboots."

#!/bin/bash

# Idempotent remediation script to restrict process tracing on RHEL 8
# Restricts ptrace to direct descendant processes only

set -e

# Function to check if running as root
check_root() {
    if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
        echo "Error: This script must be run as root"
        exit 1
    fi
}

# Function to apply ptrace restriction immediately
apply_ptrace_restriction() {
    # Set kernel.yama.ptrace_scope to 2 (restrict ptrace to direct descendants)
    # 0 = unrestricted, 1 = restricted to descendants, 2 = restricted to descendants, 3 = all restricted
    # We use 2 as it's the appropriate setting for restricting to direct descendants
    
    echo "Applying ptrace scope restriction..."
    
    # Check current value
    current_value=$(sysctl -n kernel.yama.ptrace_scope 2>/dev/null || echo "not set")
    
    if [[ "$current_value" != "2" ]]; then
        echo "kernel.yama.ptrace_scope = 2" | tee -a /etc/sysctl.conf > /dev/null
        sysctl -w kernel.yama.ptrace_scope=2
        echo "ptrace scope restriction applied immediately"
    else
        echo "ptrace scope is already set to 2"
    fi
}

# Function to ensure persistence across reboots
ensure_persistence() {
    echo "Ensuring persistence across reboots..."
    
    # Remove duplicate entries if they exist
    if grep -q "^kernel.yama.ptrace_scope" /etc/sysctl.conf; then
        # Remove all existing entries
        sed -i '/^kernel.yama.ptrace_scope/d' /etc/sysctl.conf
    fi
    
    # Add the setting to sysctl.conf
    echo "kernel.yama.ptrace_scope = 2" >> /etc/sysctl.conf
    
    # Also create/update sysctl.d configuration for better management
    echo "kernel.yama.ptrace_scope = 2" > /etc/sysctl.d/99-ptrace-restriction.conf
    
    # Apply the configuration
    sysctl -p /etc/sysctl.d/99-ptrace-restriction.conf > /dev/null 2>&1 || true
    
    echo "Persistence configuration updated"
}

# Function to verify the restriction is applied
verify_restriction() {
    echo "Verifying ptrace restriction..."
    
    actual_value=$(sysctl -n kernel.yama.ptrace_scope 2>/dev/null || echo "not set")
    
    if [[ "$actual_value" == "2" ]]; then
        echo "✓ ptrace scope restriction successfully applied (value: $actual_value)"
        return 0
    else
        echo "✗ Failed to apply ptrace scope restriction (current value: $actual_value)"
        return 1
    fi
}

# Main execution
main() {
    check_root
    
    echo "=== RHEL 8 Process Tracing Restriction Script ==="
    echo "This script restricts ptrace to direct descendant processes only"
    echo ""
    
    apply_ptrace_restriction
    ensure_persistence
    
    if verify_restriction; then
        echo ""
        echo "=== Configuration Complete ==="
        echo "Process tracing is now restricted to direct descendant processes"
        echo "This restriction will persist across reboots"
        exit 0
    else
        echo ""
        echo "=== Configuration Failed ==="
        exit 1
    fi
}

main "$@"

#!/bin/bash

# Enable kernel memory allocator's object-poisoning feature on RHEL 8
# This script adds the slub_debug=P kernel parameter persistently across all kernels

set -e

# Define the kernel parameter to add
KERNEL_PARAM="slub_debug=P"

# Path to GRUB configuration
GRUB_CONFIG="/etc/default/grub"

# Check if system is RHEL 8
if ! grep -q "Red Hat Enterprise Linux release 8" /etc/redhat-release 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Warning: System does not appear to be RHEL 8" >&2
fi

# Check if the parameter is already present in GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX
if grep -q "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=.*${KERNEL_PARAM}" "$GRUB_CONFIG"; then
    echo "Kernel parameter ${KERNEL_PARAM} is already configured"
    exit 0
fi

# Backup the original GRUB config
cp "$GRUB_CONFIG" "$GRUB_CONFIG.backup.$(date +%s)"

# Add the kernel parameter to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX if not already present
if grep -q "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=" "$GRUB_CONFIG"; then
    # Parameter exists, append to it
    sed -i "s/^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"\(.*\)\"$/GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"\1 ${KERNEL_PARAM}\"/" "$GRUB_CONFIG"
else
    # Parameter doesn't exist, create it
    echo "GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"${KERNEL_PARAM}\"" >> "$GRUB_CONFIG"
fi

# Regenerate GRUB configuration for all installed kernels
# This command works for both BIOS and UEFI systems on RHEL 8
if [ -d /boot/efi/EFI/redhat ]; then
    # UEFI system
    grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/efi/EFI/redhat/grub.cfg
else
    # BIOS system
    grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg
fi

echo "Kernel parameter ${KERNEL_PARAM} has been successfully configured"
echo "Changes will take effect after the next system reboot"

# This ensures the host does not route packets between interfaces

# Check if running as root
if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
   echo "This script must be run as root"
   exit 1

# Disable IPv4 forwarding at runtime
sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=0 > /dev/null 2>&1

# Make the setting persistent by adding/updating /etc/sysctl.d/ configuration
# Create or update the configuration file for IPv4 forwarding

fi

echo "net.ipv4.ip_forward = 0" >> "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE"

# Apply the configuration without requiring a reboot
sysctl -p "$SYSCTL_CONFIG_FILE" > /dev/null 2>&1

if [[ "$CURRENT_VALUE" == "0" ]]; then

    echo "Current setting: net.ipv4.ip_forward = $CURRENT_VALUE"
    exit 0
else
